Implementation Report

ProcureX Procurement Platform

A formal implementation report describing the architecture, user workflows, frontend implementation, backend modules, database model, security foundation, testing approach, and visual evidence from the ProcureX reference interface.

System ProcureX Procurement Platform
Repository procurement-platform
Reference UI ../procurex-ui
Prepared for Engineering handover, implementation review, and technical maintenance
Prepared on July 17, 2026

1. Abstract

ProcureX is a modular procurement platform designed to support the full lifecycle of public and private procurement operations. The system includes public marketplace discovery, account registration, company verification, tender planning, tender publication, supplier bidding, bid evaluation, award grouping, award recommendation, contract negotiation, post-award tracking, communication, records management, support operations, and platform compliance administration.

The implementation uses a production monorepo named procurement-platform. The frontend is implemented with React, Vite, Material UI, Redux Toolkit, React Router, i18next, and supporting shared UI utilities. The backend is implemented with TypeScript, Express, Helmet, Prisma, modular API boundaries, PostgreSQL multi-schema modeling, and row-level security foundations. The original procurex-ui prototype is retained as the visual and workflow reference, while the production React client owns the operational implementation.

Academically, the system demonstrates a layered information-system architecture: presentation concerns are isolated in the React client, process orchestration is concentrated in domain services, persistence is represented through Prisma and PostgreSQL schemas, and governance is supported through audit, records, compliance, and row-level access controls. This report therefore evaluates not only what was implemented, but also the architectural quality, data integrity model, validation strategy, and future production-readiness path.

Documentation note: This report is a documentation deliverable only. It does not introduce public API changes, database schema changes, or application behavior changes.

2. Introduction

Procurement systems require strong traceability, controlled access, structured workflows, and clear collaboration between buyers, suppliers, evaluators, approvers, and administrators. ProcureX addresses these needs by organizing procurement activity around companies and workflow assignments rather than treating buyer and supplier as simple login roles.

The system separates presentation reference from production execution. The prototype in ../procurex-ui captures the intended user experience, while procurement-platform/client implements the production interface. This separation allows the project to preserve the design intent while progressively adding authentication, routing, state management, localization, API integration, tests, and database enforcement.

3. Objectives

The implementation objectives were defined around system completeness, maintainability, and future production readiness.

  1. Implement a production workspace that reflects the ProcureX reference user interface.
  2. Provide a modular backend where each procurement domain has a clear API boundary.
  3. Model identity, organizations, buyer and supplier capabilities, tenders, bids, evaluation, awards, contracts, records, and compliance data.
  4. Separate account type from procurement capability so a company can act as buyer, supplier, or both.
  5. Prepare row-level security foundations for organization-aware data access.
  6. Support screenshot-based UI parity validation between the reference prototype and the production React client.
  7. Provide tests and scripts that support local development, validation, database setup, and future enhancement.

4. Methodology

The development approach used a reference-driven migration methodology. The original static ProcureX interface remained the product and visual source of truth. Production implementation then translated that reference into React pages, guarded routes, domain modules, database models, and repeatable tests.

4.1 Reference UI Method

UI parity is maintained by capturing screenshots from both the static prototype and the production React target. This makes design drift visible and gives the team concrete evidence for report, review, and validation work.

4.2 Modular Development Method

Backend modules follow a consistent structure: 

routes.ts -> controller.ts -> service.ts -> repository.ts -> validators.ts -> types.ts

This pattern keeps HTTP transport, business behavior, persistence, validation, and contracts separated. It also gives future developers a predictable place to add new behavior.

5. System Architecture

ProcureX is implemented as a monorepo with separate workspaces for the frontend client, backend server, and shared contracts. The architecture supports independent growth of user interface features, API modules, database models, and future intelligence services. 

procurement-platform/
|-- client/       React, Vite, routing, state, UI implementation
|-- server/       Express APIs, Prisma, modules, database access, tests
|-- shared/       Shared enums and contracts
|-- ml-service/   Reserved for future Python intelligence workloads
|-- docs/         Architecture, database, report, UI parity assets
|-- scripts/      Page generation and screenshot capture scripts
`-- package.json  Workspace scripts

5.1 Architectural Principles

  • The reference UI is not served directly by production.
  • The React client owns production user interaction and route behavior.
  • The Express server owns API routing, module registration, and backend orchestration.
  • The Prisma schema is the source of database model truth.
  • Organization-aware security is enforced through identity rules and RLS foundations.

5.2 Workspace Surface

6. Frontend Implementation

The production client is implemented as a React 19 and Vite application. It uses Material UI for UI foundations, Redux Toolkit for feature state, React Router for navigation, i18next for localization, Axios for API calls, and Recharts for chart visualizations.

6.1 Feature Organization

Frontend features are grouped by procurement domain. Each feature can contain pages, API helpers, hooks, fixtures, state slices, and ProcureX-derived components.

Area Implementation Role
src/app Application shell, providers, router, route guards, and redirects.
src/features Domain features such as procurement, bidding, awards, identity, records, communication, admin, public, and workspace.
src/shared Reusable components, API utilities, hooks, types, and formatting helpers.
src/i18n English and Swahili localization resources.
src/styles Global styles, tokens, MUI theme configuration, and prototype compatibility styles.

6.2 Routing And Guards

The router uses a ProcureX page registry to render pages by stable page keys. Public pages are available without authentication, operational pages require authenticated and verified users, and admin pages require administrator access. 

/sign-in                         Public authentication route
/identity/verification           Authenticated identity route
/dashboard                       Verified workspace route
/procurement/create-tender       Verified procurement route
/admin                           Admin-only platform route

6.3 Public And Authentication Screens

6.4 Frontend Feature Implementation, Results, And Testing

This subsection navigates the production frontend feature by feature. Each feature was developed by moving from prototype page intent to a React implementation that can participate in routing, authentication, API communication, shared styling, localization, and automated testing. The frontend therefore acts as the user-facing orchestration layer: it does not own the database, but it makes backend and database capabilities visible through screens, forms, dashboards, navigation, workflow cards, tables, alerts, and role-aware page access.

6.4.1 Public Pages And Guest Marketplace

The public pages were implemented to support users who have not yet authenticated. The welcome, about, contact, privacy policy, terms and conditions, and guest marketplace screens were converted from the static reference interface into React components registered through the ProcureX page registry. They are routed as public pages, which means the user can reach them without a verified session and without inheriting the authenticated workspace shell.

Technically, this feature uses React 19, Vite, React Router, Material UI foundations, prototype-compatible CSS, shared page-shell components, and static assets from the public folder. The data flow is intentionally light: public pages render stable content, while marketplace entry points can be connected to public API helpers when live opportunity data is required. The result is a low-friction entry surface where suppliers, buyers, and visitors can understand the platform before registration. Validation is provided through public page component tests, route smoke tests, and UI parity screenshots that compare the React target against the original reference pages.

6.4.2 Authentication, Registration, Forgot Password, And Turnstile Protection

Authentication screens were implemented as dedicated feature components under the auth feature. The registration page captures account details and routes users into the identity journey. The sign-in page accepts credentials and connects the user to session-aware routing. Forgot password and reset-password experiences support account recovery. Turnstile protection is implemented as a reusable component so human verification can be attached to sensitive authentication actions without scattering challenge logic throughout every form.

The main technologies are React, React Router, Axios API helpers, Testing Library, Vitest, shared alert components, and the Turnstile widget component. The component flow is form input to local validation state, then API helper call, then success/error UI feedback, then route transition according to the authentication outcome. The result is an authentication surface that can be tested independently from the rest of the workspace. Validation is covered by auth component tests, route guard tests, page smoke tests, and Turnstile widget tests.

Technical Deep Dive: Auth State & Flow

State Management (Redux Toolkit): Authentication state is centrally managed via the authSlice, exposing thunks like signInWithCredentials and hydrateAuthSession.

export const signInWithCredentials = createAsyncThunk<AuthSessionResponse, { email: string; password: string; turnstileToken: string }, { rejectValue: string }>(
  'auth/signInWithCredentials',
  async (input, { rejectWithValue }) => {
    try {
      return await authApi.signIn(input);
    } catch (error) {
      return rejectWithValue(apiErrorMessage(error, 'Sign-in failed.'));
    }
  }
);

Session Expiry Handling: The slice explicitly tracks session expiry and hydration, ensuring the user is cleanly logged out across the application when a token expires:

      .addCase(hydrateAuthSession.rejected, (state) => {
        state.sessionExpired = Boolean(state.token);
        state.status = 'idle';
        state.user = null;
        state.token = null;
        state.expiresAt = null;
        state.isAuthenticated = false;
        clearStoredAuthToken();
      })

6.4.3 Identity Verification And Account Profile

Identity verification was implemented as the bridge between simple account creation and permission to use operational procurement modules. The identity verification page captures organization and verification information, while the verification-status page communicates whether the user is still drafting, waiting for review, approved, rejected, or otherwise blocked from full workspace access. The account profile page provides a user-facing place for profile and account state.

Implementation uses React feature components, shared form controls, shared domain types, Axios identity API helpers, and guarded routes. The frontend does not make final verification decisions; it submits and displays state owned by the backend identity module. The result is a clearer separation between onboarding UI and compliance authority. Testing includes identity verification component tests, account profile tests, route guard tests, and screenshot evidence for identity-related screens.

6.4.4 Workspace Dashboard And App Launcher

The workspace dashboard and app launcher were implemented as the main verified-user operating surface. After authentication and verification, users need a consistent way to locate procurement, bidding, evaluation, awards, communication, records, and administrative modules. The dashboard summarizes work, while the launcher presents the application modules in a structured, navigable form.

This feature uses React, shared workspace chrome, feature fixtures, Material UI layout primitives, Redux Toolkit where feature state is required, and Recharts where summary metrics are visualized. The flow is session state to protected route to workspace shell to dashboard or launcher module navigation. The result is a single operational entry point for verified users. Testing includes workspace dashboard tests, route protection tests, page smoke tests, and UI parity screenshots for the app launcher and workspace dashboard.

6.4.5 Procurement Planning And Tender Creation

Procurement planning and tender creation were implemented as buyer-side React workflows. These screens represent the process of preparing a procurement need, selecting tender metadata, entering requirements, defining milestones, organizing commercial items, and preparing the tender for publication. The UI is structured around controlled forms, workflow sections, summary panels, and reusable tender-planning components.

The implementation uses React, TypeScript, feature hooks, procurement API helpers, shared formatting utilities, Material UI components, and prototype-compatible styles. The component flow is buyer action to form state, then validation and API helper behavior, then visual confirmation or error display. The result is a buyer-facing implementation of the procurement preparation stage. Testing includes procurement API helper tests, tender planning component tests, create-tender tests, page smoke tests, and screenshot parity for create tender and tender publication pages.

Technical Deep Dive: Procurement State & Flow

State Management (Redux Toolkit): Procurement state manages active tenders and complex draft objects for the multi-step tender creation process:

type ProcurementState = {
  tenders: ProcurementTender[];
  selectedTenderId: string | null;
  createTenderDrafts: CreateTenderDraft[];
  publishedTenders: CreateTenderDraft[];
  selectedDraftId: string | null;
  lastSubmittedTenderId: string | null;
};

Draft Handling: procurementSlice provides reducers specifically designed to support saving drafts, resuming, and submitting tenders:

    saveCreateTenderDraft(state, action: { payload: CreateTenderDraft }) {
      const draft = { ...action.payload, status: action.payload.status === 'PUBLISHED' ? 'PUBLISHED' : 'DRAFT' } satisfies CreateTenderDraft;
      const index = state.createTenderDrafts.findIndex((item) => item.id === draft.id);
      if (index >= 0) state.createTenderDrafts[index] = draft;
      else state.createTenderDrafts.unshift(draft);
      state.selectedDraftId = draft.id;
    },
    submitCreateTenderForEvaluation(state, action: { payload: CreateTenderDraft }) {
      const submitted: CreateTenderDraft = { ...action.payload, status: 'SUBMITTED', submittedAt: action.payload.submittedAt ?? new Date().toISOString() };
      state.lastSubmittedTenderId = submitted.id;
      // ... update state array
    }

6.4.6 Supplier Marketplace And Supplier Tender Detail

Supplier marketplace and supplier tender detail screens were implemented to support opportunity discovery from the supplier perspective. The marketplace presents available tenders and opportunity summaries, while supplier tender detail allows a supplier to inspect a specific tender before saving it or preparing a bid. This feature separates public or marketplace discovery from authenticated supplier participation.

The implementation uses React feature pages, procurement API helpers, typed domain structures, shared status badges, shared formatting, and responsive layout components. The data flow is route selection to marketplace listing or tender-detail state, then user action such as saving or opening the bidding workspace. The result is a supplier-facing discovery path connected to the broader procurement lifecycle. Testing includes supplier tender detail component tests, procurement API tests, marketplace tests, page smoke tests, and screenshot validation for supplier marketplace and supplier tender detail screens.

6.4.7 Bidding Workspace

The bidding workspace was implemented as the supplier's controlled area for responding to tender opportunities. It presents the bid context, response status, documents, requirements, receipts, and submission progress. The UI is designed to keep supplier work organized so a supplier can move from reviewing an opportunity to preparing a formal response.

Technologies used include React, TypeScript, bidding feature API helpers, feature hooks, shared domain types, Material UI, and Testing Library. The frontend flow is supplier tender detail to bidding workspace, then draft or response actions through bidding API helpers. The result is a visible supplier workflow that maps to backend bid, bid-version, document, response, and receipt concepts. Testing includes bidding API tests, bidding workspace component tests, route smoke tests, and UI parity screenshots.

Technical Deep Dive: Bidding State & Flow

Type Definitions (types.ts): The frontend uses strongly typed interfaces to handle complex bid envelopes and nested document states:

export type BidDraftPayload = {
  workflowType?: BidWorkflowType;
  administrative: Record<string, unknown>;
  technical: Record<string, unknown>;
  financial: Record<string, unknown>;
  declarations: Record<string, unknown>;
  responses: Array<{ requirementKey: string; response: Record<string, unknown> }>;
  documents: BidDocumentInput[];
  envelopes?: Record<string, unknown>;
  // ... and other completeness flags
};

Submission Flow: The wizard components progressively build the BidDraftPayload and execute the final submission, transforming UI steps into API structures:

export type BidSubmissionSchemaStepDto = {
  id: BidSubmissionStepId;
  label: string;
  envelope: BidDocumentEnvelope;
  required: boolean;
  fields: BidSubmissionSchemaFieldDto[];
};

6.4.8 Bid Evaluation

Bid evaluation was implemented as the reviewer-facing transition from submitted supplier bids to structured procurement decisions. The evaluation screen organizes bids, criteria, scores, review context, and decision support. It gives evaluators a user interface that aligns with the backend evaluation workspace, criteria, score, and assignment data model.

The feature uses React, evaluation feature modules, shared table and status components, Recharts where visual summary evidence is needed, and Testing Library for component validation. The flow is evaluation route to workspace context, then review and scoring UI, then progression toward award recommendation. The result is a structured evaluation surface rather than an informal list of supplier responses. Testing includes page smoke tests, evaluation-related client tests, awards flow tests where evaluation output is consumed, and screenshot validation for the bid evaluation page.

Technical Deep Dive: Evaluation State & Flow

Type Definitions (types.ts): Evaluation uses a complex workspace structure consolidating bids, criteria, rankings, and audit history:

export type EvaluationWorkspace = {
  tender: { id: string; reference: string; title: string; buyerName: string; /* ... */ } | null;
  availability: { isReady: boolean; reason: string | null; };
  summary: {
    submittedBidCount: number;
    evaluatedBidCount: number;
    recommendedBidder: { bidId: string; supplierName: string; } | null;
    activeStageId: string | null;
    // ...
  };
  criteria: EvaluationWorkspaceCriterion[];
  bids: EvaluationWorkspaceBid[];
  rankings: EvaluationRankingRow[];
  audit: { evaluatedBy: string | null; events: EvaluationAuditEvent[]; };
};

Save Mechanism: Modifying the evaluation workspace requires structured updates to both numerical scores and qualitative decisions:

export type SaveEvaluationWorkspaceInput = {
  scores: Array<{
    bidId: string;
    criterionId: string;
    score: number;
    comment: string;
  }>;
  decisions: Array<{
    bidId: string;
    status: EvaluationDecisionStatus;
    comment: string;
  }>;
  complete?: boolean;
};

6.4.9 Award Recommendation, Award Response, And Contract Negotiation

Award and contract screens were implemented as a connected set of lifecycle pages. Award recommendation represents the formal proposal after evaluation. Award response represents supplier-side acceptance or response behavior. Contract negotiation presents the transition from award decision to agreement terms and contract formation.

The implementation uses React, the awardsContracts feature, shared award-contract components, domain-specific action forms, role-aware access helpers, TypeScript types, feature fixtures, and API helpers. The flow is evaluation output to recommendation, recommendation to award response or approval review, then contract negotiation and version context. The result is a production UI path for moving from procurement decision to contractual commitment. Testing includes awards-contract flow tests, action form tests, role-access tests, page component tests, and screenshot validation for award recommendation, award response, and contract negotiation.

Technical Deep Dive: Award & Contract Negotiation States

Type Definitions (types.ts): The frontend mirrors the backend lifecycle using explicit wizard steps and role contexts:

export type AwardContractStep =
  | 'evaluation-result'
  | 'award-decision'
  | 'approval'
  | 'award-notification'
  | 'standstill-period'
  | 'supplier-acceptance'
  | 'pre-contract-documents'
  | 'draft-contract'
  | 'overview'
  | 'buyer-review'
  | 'supplier-review'
  | 'negotiation'
  | 'contract-owner-approval'
  | 'signatures'
  | 'execution';

export type WorkflowAccess = {
  viewerRole: ViewerRole;
  canManageBuyerActions: boolean;
  canSubmitSupplierActions: boolean;
  canSignBuyer: boolean;
  canSignSupplier: boolean;
  readOnlyReason: string | null;
};

Versioned Drafting: The interface handles negotiation via versioned contract payloads, where parties can suggest and agree on changes before signing:

export type ContractDetailDto = {
  id: string;
  reference: string;
  status: string;
  buyerName: string;
  supplierName: string | null;
  amount: number | null;
  versions?: Array<Record<string, unknown>>;
  clauses?: ContractLifecycleItemDto[];
  negotiations?: ContractLifecycleItemDto[];
  signatures: Array<{ id: string; role: string; status: string; signerName: string; signedAt: string | null }>;
};

6.4.10 Post-Award Tracking

Post-award tracking was implemented to represent contract execution after award and negotiation. This page supports visibility into delivery, milestones, purchase orders, invoices, risks, and operational follow-up. It is important because procurement quality does not end at supplier selection; the platform must also support execution and accountability after the award.

The feature uses React, awardsContracts shared components, contract lifecycle fixtures, TypeScript domain types, shared status presentation, and Testing Library. The flow is contract context to execution status, then milestone, invoice, payment, or delivery evidence display. The result is a user-facing post-award control surface. Testing includes awards-contract flow tests, post-award component tests, role-access tests, and screenshot validation for post-award tracking.

Technical Deep Dive: Post-Award Execution & Tracking

Tab Layout: The post-award view groups tracking data into distinct tabs matching key execution facets:

export type PostAwardTabId =
  | 'milestones'
  | 'payments'
  | 'issues'
  | 'variations'
  | 'closure'
  | 'performance';

Tracking Details: The contract detail payload encompasses milestones, variations, payments, risks, and warranties, enabling complete visual compliance audits:

export type ContractDetailDto = {
  // ... core metadata
  milestones: Array<ContractLifecycleItemDto & { amount?: number | null; evidence?: ContractLifecycleItemDto[] }>;
  risks: Array<ContractLifecycleItemDto & { level?: string; score?: number; mitigationAction?: string }>;
  variations: Array<ContractLifecycleItemDto & { changeType?: string; costImpact?: number | null; timeImpactDays?: number | null }>;
  invoices?: Array<Record<string, unknown>>;
  payments?: Array<Record<string, unknown>>;
  closeout: Record<string, unknown> | null;
};

6.4.11 Communication Center

The communication center was implemented as a collaboration surface for procurement-related messages, priorities, attachments, and workflow updates. It centralizes communication so important procurement context does not remain outside the system in disconnected email or informal channels.

Technologies include React, communication feature components, shared cards and status components, feature fixtures, TypeScript types, and API helpers. The flow is user route to message list, then message selection or update context, with backend communication records providing the persistent model. The result is a structured communication interface aligned with auditability goals. Testing includes communication center component tests, page smoke tests, backend communication service tests, and UI parity screenshots.

6.4.12 Records History

Records history was implemented to provide a visible traceability layer. The page presents evidence of what happened in procurement workflows, including actions, dates, statuses, references, and historical context. This is especially important for procurement systems because later reviewers must be able to understand the sequence of events behind a decision.

The implementation uses React, records feature pages, shared timeline and status components, formatting helpers, and route protection. The flow is protected route to records view, then filtered or summarized record entries from the records model. The result is a user-facing audit surface that complements backend audit events and database record entries. Testing includes page smoke tests, records-related validation, and screenshot parity for records history.

6.4.13 Admin Dashboard, Users, Compliance, Analytics, Search, And Audit

Administrative screens were implemented as admin-only surfaces. The dashboard gives platform oversight, users supports identity and organization review, compliance handles cases and rules, analytics presents operational measurement, search supports investigation, and audit exposes event-level evidence. These screens are intentionally separated from buyer and supplier workflows because administrators are platform compliance users, not procurement participants.

The implementation uses React, React Router admin guards, admin feature components, shared admin layout, Material UI components, charting where metrics are shown, TypeScript fixtures, and API helpers. The flow is admin session to admin route guard, then admin page selection, then platform-level review or action. The result is a governance interface for platform oversight. Testing includes admin dashboard tests, admin users tests, admin communication tests, admin shared component tests, admin tender review tests, route guard tests, and screenshot parity for admin pages.

6.4.14 Support Pages And Notifications

Support pages and notifications were implemented to improve operational usability. Support pages give users a channel for assistance, while notification types and components provide a pattern for communicating events, warnings, and workflow updates inside the application.

The feature uses React, support page components, shared notification types, shared notification cards, feature API helpers, and Testing Library. The flow is user action to support or notification surface, then ticket or message representation through backend support and notification models. The result is a more complete operational platform, not only a procurement transaction interface. Testing includes support page tests, notifications tests, shared component tests, and backend support service tests.

6.4.15 Localization, Shared Components, Styling, And UI Parity

Localization, shared components, styling, and UI parity were implemented as cross-cutting frontend foundations. i18next and react-i18next provide English and Swahili translation resources. Shared components such as top bars, tables, badges, timelines, empty states, loading pages, and form controls reduce duplication. CSS tokens, global styles, the MUI theme, and prototype-compatible styles preserve a consistent visual language.

The UI parity workflow uses Playwright screenshot capture to compare the reference prototype and production React target. The flow is route mapping to reference capture, route mapping to React capture, then saved desktop and mobile screenshots. The result is a visual validation method that supports migration from prototype to production. Testing includes shared component tests, language switcher tests, language/layout checks, page smoke tests, and UI parity screenshot generation.

The main frontend result is a production-ready user interface structure that keeps the visual language of the prototype but adds routing, access control, localization, API boundaries, reusable components, and repeatable tests. The result is not only a set of static screens; it is an application shell capable of supporting real authenticated workflows.

7. Backend Implementation

The backend is a TypeScript Express application that provides the controlled service boundary between the React client and the PostgreSQL data layer. It centralizes HTTP app creation, security middleware, module registration, JSON parsing, CORS configuration, health reporting, 404 handling, and centralized error responses. This makes the server a disciplined application-service layer rather than a collection of disconnected route handlers.

The backend entrypoint is organized around createApp() in server/src/app.ts. The application validates production security configuration, sets proxy trust, applies Helmet security headers, configures a content security policy, restricts CORS origins, limits JSON bodies to 2 MB, exposes /health, mounts domain routers from the module registry, and finally applies not-found and error middleware. This sequence is important because cross-cutting security and parsing behavior are established before any domain module receives a request.

7.1 Express Bootstrap And HTTP Boundary

The bootstrap layer implements the outermost boundary of the system. Its responsibility is not procurement logic, but safe and predictable HTTP behavior. Helmet is configured with a content security policy that limits script, image, style, font, frame, connection, base URI, and frame ancestor sources. CORS is resolved from configured allowed origins, with local origins available for development. Requests that do not match an approved origin are rejected before they can reach module code.

Backend Concern Implementation Architectural Value
Security headers helmet() with explicit CSP directives. Reduces browser-side attack surface and documents accepted resource origins.
CORS Origin callback checks configured client origins and local development origins. Prevents arbitrary browser origins from invoking protected APIs.
Request body size express.json({ limit: '2mb' }). Limits accidental or hostile oversized JSON payloads.
Health reporting GET /health returns service status and registered modules. Supports operational checks and automated assertions in tests.
Error shape Centralized 404 and error middleware. Keeps client-facing failure responses consistent across modules.

7.2 Module Pattern

Each module uses the same implementation pattern so behavior remains consistent across procurement domains. The pattern is academically significant because it separates transport, validation, orchestration, persistence, and type contracts. This lowers coupling and makes the system easier to reason about during testing, review, and future extension.

File Responsibility
routes.ts Defines HTTP endpoints and maps them to controller handlers. Route files should remain thin and should not contain business decisions.
controller.ts Handles request and response concerns, including parameter extraction, status codes, and response formatting.
service.ts Owns business behavior, workflow orchestration, permission-sensitive decisions, and coordination between repositories or providers.
repository.ts Owns persistence and data access through Prisma or provider-specific integrations.
validators.ts Owns request validation, usually with Zod schemas, so invalid input is rejected before service behavior runs.
types.ts Owns module-local contracts, DTOs, enums, and metadata used by the module implementation and tests.

A typical request therefore travels through a clear path: 

HTTP request
  -> Express middleware
  -> module route
  -> controller
  -> validator
  -> service
  -> repository
  -> Prisma/PostgreSQL
  -> response or centralized error handler

This request lifecycle supports maintainability because a change in one concern has an obvious home. For example, a new validation rule belongs in validators.ts, a procurement workflow rule belongs in service.ts, and a query optimization belongs in repository.ts.

7.3 Registered API Modules

API modules are registered through the backend module index and mounted under stable base paths. The registry in server/src/modules/index.ts functions as a controlled map of backend capabilities. It also allows /health to report exactly which modules are active in the running server. 

/api/public
/api/identity
/api/organization
/api/procurement
/api/bidding
/api/evaluation
/api/award-contract
/api/financial
/api/compliance-admin
/api/communication
/api/dashboard
/api/records
/api/intelligence
/api/integration
/api/documents
/api/support
Module System Role
public Publishes public content and unauthenticated platform information.
identity Controls registration, authentication, session lookup, verification, profile changes, signatures, and admin verification decisions.
organization Represents company membership, buyer/supplier capabilities, and organization-level operating context.
procurement Owns tender creation, planning, amendments, publication, requirements, milestones, commercial items, and marketplace behavior.
bidding Owns bid drafts, submitted bid versions, bid documents, responses, receipts, samples, and supplier/buyer visibility rules.
evaluation Owns evaluation workspaces, assignments, criteria, stages, and scoring behavior.
award-contract Owns award recommendations, award groups, winners, approvals, notices, contract formation, negotiation, lifecycle tracking, close-out, and supplier performance.
financial Models purchase orders, invoices, payment schedules, commitments, approvals, and confirmations.
compliance-admin Supports compliance cases, rules, risk signals, admin actions, review, enforcement, and platform oversight.
communication Stores workflow communication, priorities, statuses, and attachments.
dashboard Aggregates operational metrics and workspace summaries for the client experience.
records Exposes auditable record entries and historical procurement evidence.
intelligence Prepares market snapshots, price benchmarks, supplier match signals, and risk analysis boundaries.
integration Represents external systems, registry records, synchronization runs, and integration events.
documents Manages document-object metadata and upload-related persistence boundaries.
support Provides support tickets and ticket comments for operational assistance.

7.4 Identity API Example

Identity is the most complete route surface because it controls account creation, authentication, session lookup, verification, profile updates, and admin verification review. 

POST /api/identity/registration/start
POST /api/identity/registration/verify-otp
POST /api/identity/registration/activate-email
POST /api/identity/registration/set-password
POST /api/identity/auth/sign-in
GET  /api/identity/session
POST /api/identity/verification/submit
POST /api/identity/admin/verifications/:id/decision

Identity is also the conceptual gate for the rest of the platform. It defines whether a session is authenticated, whether an organization is verified, and whether the account is a platform administrator. Buyer and supplier behavior is deliberately not encoded as account type; it is derived from organization capability and workflow context.

7.5 Backend Feature Implementation, Results, And Testing

This subsection navigates the backend feature by feature. Each backend module is implemented as a domain boundary with a route file, controller, service, repository, validators, and local types. The route receives the HTTP request, the controller translates HTTP concerns, the validator rejects invalid input, the service applies business behavior, the repository performs persistence through Prisma, and the centralized error handler returns consistent failures. This pattern gives every feature a predictable technical shape.

7.5.1 Express App Bootstrap And Security Middleware

The backend application is created through the Express bootstrap in server/src/app.ts. This layer implements cross-cutting infrastructure before domain modules run. It validates production security configuration, enables proxy trust, applies Helmet, configures content security policy directives, restricts CORS origins, parses JSON with a 2 MB limit, exposes /health, mounts registered modules, and applies 404 and error middleware.

Technologies used include TypeScript, Express, Helmet, CORS middleware, environment-driven security configuration, and Supertest/Vitest for app-level assertions. The request flow is client request to middleware, then route registry, then module router, then controller. The result is a secure, consistent HTTP boundary. Tests validate app creation, health responses, module registration visibility, and standardized request behavior.

7.5.2 Public Content Module

The public module implements unauthenticated content boundaries for pages such as about, privacy policy, and terms and conditions. Its purpose is to serve stable platform information without requiring an authenticated procurement session. Public content is separated from operational modules so informational pages cannot accidentally inherit buyer, supplier, or admin assumptions.

The module follows the same route-controller-service-repository pattern as other modules. Express exposes the public base path, validators protect supported keys or inputs, services decide which public content is available, and repositories use Prisma content models where dynamic content is needed. The result is a clean public API surface. Validation is provided by public service tests, public client API tests, app health registration checks, and page rendering tests on the client.

7.5.3 Identity, Authentication, Session, Verification, And Signing

The identity module is the platform's trust entry point. It implements registration start, OTP verification, email activation, password setup, sign-in, sign-out, session lookup, forgot-password, reset-password, verification draft, registry lookup, verification submission, profile updates, admin verification review, digital signatures, and signing credentials. This module owns the distinction between normal users and platform administrators.

Technologies used include TypeScript, Express, Prisma, Zod validators, delivery-provider abstractions, Nodemailer-ready SMTP support, Resend email delivery boundaries, SMS/WhatsApp provider boundaries, Turnstile security configuration, password and token workflows, and Vitest tests. The flow is request to identity route, validation of account or verification payload, service-level orchestration, repository persistence in identity and organization tables, then a controlled response to the client. The result is a full onboarding and verification API. Testing includes identity production-auth tests, delivery-provider tests, signing tests, module-validator tests, app tests, and client auth component tests.

Technical Deep Dive: Identity Models & Validation

Database Model (schema.prisma): The identity module relies on the User and VerificationProfile models within the identity schema.

model User {
  id                 String             @id @default(uuid()) @db.Uuid
  email              String             @unique
  accountType        AccountType        @default(USER) @map("account_type")
  verificationStatus VerificationStatus @default(NOT_STARTED) @map("verification_status")
  sessions           Session[]
  // ... relations to tenders, bids, contracts
  @@schema("identity")
}

model VerificationProfile {
  id             String             @id @default(uuid()) @db.Uuid
  userId         String             @map("user_id") @db.Uuid
  organizationId String?            @map("organization_id") @db.Uuid
  status         VerificationStatus @default(DRAFT)
  // ... fields for registry and payload
  @@schema("identity")
}

Zod Validation: API boundaries use Zod for strict payload verification before hitting the service layer. For example, the sign-in and registration schemas enforce human-verification via Turnstile:

export const startRegistrationSchema = z.object({
  email: z.string().email(),
  phone: z.string().min(7).max(32),
  turnstileToken: turnstileTokenSchema,
  location: tanzaniaLocationSchema.optional()
});

export const signInSchema = z.object({
  email: z.string().email(),
  password: z.string().min(1),
  turnstileToken: turnstileTokenSchema
});

7.5.4 Organization And Capability Model

The organization module represents the company context in which procurement work happens. ProcureX intentionally avoids making buyer and supplier permanent login roles. Instead, a user belongs to an organization, and that organization can receive buyer and supplier capabilities. This design allows one company to participate in multiple procurement capacities without creating separate login identities.

Implementation uses Express module registration, Prisma organization models, TypeScript types from shared contracts, service-level membership logic, and organization-aware repository access. The result is an organization model that supports realistic procurement participation, access checks, profiles, trust tiers, and future permission overrides. Validation is supported by module-validator tests, identity verification tests that create or update organization state, RLS verification, and client route guards that consume session capability information.

7.5.5 Procurement And Tender Module

The procurement module implements buyer-side tender work and supplier-facing opportunity discovery. It owns tender creation, procurement planning, tender listing, publication, marketplace visibility, saved tenders, tender amendments, requirements, milestones, documents, categories, and commercial items. This module forms the operational center of the platform because bidding, evaluation, awards, and contracts all depend on tenders.

Technologies used include TypeScript services, Express routers, controllers, Zod validators, Prisma repositories, PostgreSQL constraints, and seed scripts for marketplace and award-ready demo data. The request flow is buyer or supplier request to procurement route, payload validation, service decision about ownership or visibility, Prisma query or mutation, and response to the client. The result is a backend boundary for tender lifecycle behavior. Testing includes procurement controller tests, service tests, repository tests, marketplace seed tests, award-ready seed tests, and database validation.

Technical Deep Dive: Procurement Models & Validation

Database Model (schema.prisma): The procurement module relies heavily on the Tender and ProcurementPlan models.

model Tender {
  id                String            @id @default(uuid()) @db.Uuid
  title             String
  type              TenderType        @default(GOODS)
  status            TenderStatus      @default(DRAFT)
  procurementMethod ProcurementMethod @default(OPEN_TENDER) @map("procurement_method")
  visibility        Visibility        @default(PRIVATE)
  budget            Decimal?          @db.Decimal(19, 4)
  currency          String            @default("TZS") @db.VarChar(8)
  ownerId           String            @map("owner_id") @db.Uuid
  ownerOrgId        String            @map("owner_org_id") @db.Uuid
  // ... constraints and lifecycle dates
  @@schema("procurement")
}

Zod Validation: Complex rules are enforced when creating tenders, such as normalizing categories and enforcing closing dates in the future:

const draftClosingDateSchema = z.string().trim().min(1)
  .refine((value) => new Date(value).getTime() > Date.now(), {
    message: 'Closing date must be in the future.'
  });

export const createTenderBodySchema = z.object({
  title: z.string().trim().min(5).max(200),
  type: tenderTypeInputSchema,
  description: z.string().trim().min(20).max(5000),
  budget: z.coerce.number().positive().max(999999999999999.99).optional(),
  closingDate: draftClosingDateSchema.optional(),
  categories: z.array(categoryInputSchema).max(20).optional().default([]),
  // ... additional strict metadata fields
}).strict();

7.5.6 Bidding Module

The bidding module implements supplier responses to tenders. It models bid drafts, active bids, submitted bid versions, bid documents, bid responses, receipts, administrative envelopes, bid samples, buyer organization references, and supplier organization references. It is designed so a bid is not just a file upload; it is a structured response with ownership, status, versioning, evidence, and receipt information.

Technologies used include Express, TypeScript services, Zod validation, Prisma bidding models, PostgreSQL uniqueness constraints, and Vitest module tests. The data flow is supplier request to bidding route, validation of bid context, service rule confirming tender and supplier eligibility, repository persistence, and receipt or status response. The result is a supplier response API aligned with the procurement model. Testing includes bidding module tests, procurement/bidding API tests, active-bid uniqueness migration validation, and client bidding workspace tests.

Technical Deep Dive: Bidding Models & Validation

Database Model (schema.prisma): The bidding module manages the supplier's response via the Bid and BidDocument models.

model Bid {
  id               String       @id @default(uuid()) @db.Uuid
  tenderId         String       @map("tender_id") @db.Uuid
  supplierOrgId    String       @map("supplier_org_id") @db.Uuid
  submitterId      String       @map("submitter_id") @db.Uuid
  status           BidStatus    @default(DRAFT)
  totalAmount      Decimal?     @map("total_amount") @db.Decimal(19, 4)
  currency         String?      @db.VarChar(8)
  // ... nested responses, envelopes, and receipts
  @@schema("bidding")
}

Zod Validation: The bidding schemas support highly structured, multi-envelope responses (Administrative, Technical, Financial) rather than simple file uploads:

export const bidDraftBodySchema = z.object({
  workflowType: z.enum(['goods', 'works', 'services', 'consultancy', 'generic']).default('generic'),
  administrative: z.record(z.unknown()).default({}),
  technical: z.record(z.unknown()).default({}),
  financial: z.record(z.unknown()).default({}),
  responses: z.array(bidResponseSchema).max(300).default([]),
  documents: z.array(bidDocumentSchema).max(100).default([]),
  envelopes: z.record(z.unknown()).default({}),
  totalAmount: z.coerce.number().min(0).max(999999999999999.99).optional(),
  currency: z.string().trim().min(3).max(8).optional()
}).strict();

7.5.7 Evaluation Module

The evaluation module implements the formal review stage after bidding. It owns evaluation workspaces, criteria, evaluator assignments, stages, scoring, and workflow context. Its purpose is to turn submitted bids into structured evidence that can support award recommendations rather than informal decision making.

The module uses the route-controller-service-repository-validator pattern, Prisma evaluation models, TypeScript contracts, and seed data for evaluation-intake scenarios. Requests flow from evaluation routes to validators, then services that check workspace and assignment context, then repositories that read or write scores and related records. The result is a backend structure for transparent bid scoring. Testing includes evaluation intake seed tests, app/module validation, client bid evaluation smoke coverage, and award-ready demo validation where evaluation output feeds recommendations.

Technical Deep Dive: Evaluation Models & Validation

Database Model (schema.prisma): The evaluation process is managed through the EvaluationWorkspace, WorkflowAssignment, and EvaluationScore models.

model EvaluationWorkspace {
  id              String           @id @default(uuid()) @db.Uuid
  tenderId        String           @unique @map("tender_id") @db.Uuid
  buyerOrgId      String           @map("buyer_org_id") @db.Uuid
  status          EvaluationStatus @default(NOT_STARTED)
  activeStage     EvaluationStage  @default(OPENING) @map("active_stage")
  // ... criteria and assignments
  @@schema("evaluation")
}

Zod Validation: The evaluation schema validates bulk scoring arrays and stage transition states to keep the frontend and backend in sync during complex reviews:

export const saveWorkspaceBodySchema = z.object({
  scores: z.array(z.object({
    bidId: uuidSchema,
    criterionId: uuidSchema,
    score: z.coerce.number().finite().min(0),
    comment: z.string().trim().max(2000).optional().default('')
  }).strict()).default([]),
  decisions: z.array(z.object({
    bidId: uuidSchema,
    status: decisionStatusSchema,
    comment: z.string().trim().max(2000).optional().default('')
  }).strict()).default([]),
  activeStageId: activeStageSchema.optional(),
  complete: z.boolean().optional().default(false)
}).strict();

7.5.8 Award-Contract Lifecycle Module

The award-contract module implements one of the deepest business areas in ProcureX. It covers award recommendations, award groups, winners, clauses, negotiations, bid packs, approval routes, approval steps, tie-breakers, delivery feasibility checks, award notices, supplier responses, standstill periods, contract formation, contract parties, contract clauses, versions, signatures, milestones, evidence, deliverables, inspections, risks, variations, disputes, terminations, close-out, urgent actions, supplier performance, payments, warranties, and required documents.

Technologies used include TypeScript domain services, Express routing, Prisma contract models, Zod validators, PostgreSQL contract schema, demo seed scripts, and client awards-contract flow tests. The request flow is award or contract action to module route, validator, service decision about lifecycle state, repository read/write, and audit-ready response. The result is a backend lifecycle model from recommendation through contract execution. Testing includes award-contract service tests, seed-demo tests, award-ready seed tests, and client flow tests that exercise the corresponding UI.

Technical Deep Dive: Award & Contract Models & Validation

Database Model (schema.prisma): The contract lifecycle is managed through the Contract, ContractSignature, and ContractMilestone models within the contract schema.

model Contract {
  id               String         @id @default(uuid()) @db.Uuid
  tenderId         String?        @map("tender_id") @db.Uuid
  buyerOrgId       String         @map("buyer_org_id") @db.Uuid
  supplierOrgId    String         @map("supplier_org_id") @db.Uuid
  status           ContractStatus @default(DRAFT)
  type             ContractType   @default(LUMP_SUM)
  totalValue       Decimal?       @map("total_value") @db.Decimal(19, 4)
  currency         String         @default("TZS") @db.VarChar(8)
  signedAt         DateTime?      @map("signed_at")
  startDate        DateTime?      @map("start_date") @db.Date
  endDate          DateTime?      @map("end_date") @db.Date
  // ... clauses, milestones, invoices, and risks
  @@schema("contract")
}

Zod Validation: Contract actions require extensive validation. For example, negotiating a clause or terminating a contract requires specific payloads to maintain an auditable trail:

export const terminationBodySchema = z.object({
  terminationType: z.nativeEnum(ContractTerminationType),
  reason: z.string().trim().min(1).max(4000),
  contractClause: z.string().trim().max(240).default(''),
  faultParty: z.string().trim().max(120).default(''),
  noticeDate: z.string().trim().date().optional(),
  cureDeadline: z.string().trim().date().optional(),
  terminationEffectiveDate: z.string().trim().date().optional(),
  supplierResponse: z.string().trim().max(4000).default(''),
  finalDecision: z.string().trim().max(4000).default('')
}).strict();

7.5.9 Financial Module

The financial module supports procurement execution after contract formation. It represents purchase orders, invoices, budget commitments, three-way match results, payment approvals, payment confirmations, payment schedules, and contract payments. The module makes financial follow-up part of the procurement lifecycle instead of leaving it outside the system.

Implementation uses Express module registration, TypeScript service patterns, Prisma financial models, PostgreSQL enum statuses, and links to contract and organization records. The flow is contract or finance request to validated route, service-level check of contract or organization context, repository persistence, and response to the client. The result is financial evidence connected to awards and contracts. Validation is supported by Prisma schema validation, award contract service tests where financial records support contract flows, and seed data inspection.

7.5.10 Communication Module

The communication module implements structured messages and attachments related to procurement work. Its purpose is to keep workflow communication inside the platform where it can be connected to tenders, bids, contracts, records, and audit evidence.

Technologies include Express, Prisma communication models, TypeScript service and repository layers, Zod validators, and Vitest repository and service tests. The flow is message request to route, validation of communication kind, priority, status, owner context, and attachments, then repository persistence. The result is a collaboration boundary that supports traceable procurement communication. Testing includes communication repository tests, service tests, client communication center tests, and screenshot parity.

7.5.11 Records Module

The records module exposes auditable history and record entries. Its purpose is to support users who need to reconstruct what happened in a procurement process. The backend records boundary complements the compliance audit model by giving operational users access to structured historical evidence.

The implementation follows the module pattern and uses Prisma records models, TypeScript service logic, validators, and organization-aware access expectations. The flow is records request to route, validation of filters or context, service selection of relevant records, repository query, and response. The result is backend support for records-history screens and audit review. Validation includes module-validator tests, RLS verification for owner-aware records, and client records page smoke or screenshot validation.

7.5.12 Dashboard Module

The dashboard module aggregates operational information for workspace views. Instead of making the frontend assemble every metric from many unrelated endpoints, dashboard services can provide summarized data shaped for workspace display, administrator overview, or module indicators.

Technologies include Express, TypeScript services, Prisma queries, dashboard validators, and Vitest service tests. The flow is dashboard route to validation, service aggregation, repository or fixture-backed retrieval, and response to dashboard UI. The result is a clean backend boundary for operational summaries. Testing includes dashboard service tests, app module registration checks, client workspace dashboard tests, and page smoke tests.

7.5.13 Compliance-Admin Module

The compliance-admin module implements platform oversight behavior. It supports compliance cases, compliance rules, risk signals, supplier risk profiles, risk forecasts, collusion alerts, reviews, violation cases, enforcement records, appeals, and admin actions. This module is separated from normal procurement modules because it represents platform governance rather than buyer or supplier operations.

Implementation uses TypeScript services, Express module routing, Prisma compliance models, Zod validators, PostgreSQL compliance schema, and admin route protection on the client. The flow is admin-only request to compliance route, validation, service-level decision, repository persistence, and response. The result is a backend governance boundary. Testing includes compliance-admin service tests, admin frontend tests, module-validator tests, and RLS policy review for owner-aware compliance data.

7.5.14 Documents Module

The documents module represents document-object metadata and prepares the platform for file-related workflows. Procurement systems rely on tender documents, bid attachments, verification documents, contract evidence, inspection evidence, termination evidence, and support attachments. The module separates document metadata from the business modules that reference those documents.

Technologies include Express, TypeScript, Prisma document models, AWS S3-ready dependencies, Busboy-ready upload dependencies, Zod validation, and document repository boundaries. The flow is document request to module route, validation of owner and object metadata, storage/provider boundary where applicable, repository persistence, and response. The result is a future-ready document architecture. Validation includes Prisma validation, module validation, and business module tests where documents participate in procurement, bidding, identity, or contract workflows.

7.5.15 Intelligence Module

The intelligence module provides a boundary for market snapshots, price benchmarks, supplier match signals, risk analysis, and future machine learning or analytics work. It is intentionally separated from the core Express procurement flows so scoring or prediction logic can evolve without destabilizing transactional procurement behavior.

Technologies include TypeScript services, Express route registration, Prisma intelligence models, Elasticsearch-ready dependencies, Redis-ready dependencies, and the reserved ml-service folder for future Python workloads. The flow is intelligence request to module route, validation, service retrieval of benchmark or signal data, repository query, and response. The result is an analytics-ready backend boundary. Testing includes intelligence module tests, app registration tests, and schema validation.

7.5.16 Integration Module

The integration module represents external systems, registry records, synchronization runs, and integration events. It prepares ProcureX to communicate with external registries, compliance data sources, document providers, or enterprise systems without embedding those concerns inside procurement or identity modules.

Implementation uses Express, TypeScript services, Prisma integration models, validators, and repository boundaries. The flow is integration request or sync event to route, validation, service orchestration, repository persistence, and response or recorded event. The result is a stable extension point for external-system connectivity. Testing includes module-validator tests, Prisma validation, app registration checks, and future sync-run tests as integrations become active.

7.5.17 Support Module

The support module implements operational assistance through support tickets and comments. It gives users a structured way to ask for help, report issues, or follow up on platform problems without mixing support conversations with procurement records.

Technologies include Express, TypeScript, Prisma support models, Zod validators, service tests, and client support page tests. The flow is support request to route, validation of ticket priority or status, service creation or update behavior, repository persistence, and response to the client. The result is a support API boundary that can be expanded into workflow notifications or administrative assistance. Testing includes support service tests, client support page tests, module-validator tests, and app registration checks.

The main backend result is a modular service layer that can be tested independently from the UI. This improves technical quality because a procurement rule, such as who may update a tender or who may submit a bid, can be implemented and tested in the backend instead of being trusted only to client-side screens.

8. Database And Security Implementation

The database layer uses PostgreSQL and Prisma. Prisma is the application model and migration source, while PostgreSQL provides the relational storage engine, named schemas, constraints, indexes, and row-level security. This combination gives the implementation a typed application data model and a database-native access-control foundation.

The Prisma datasource declares multiple PostgreSQL schemas: identity, organization, procurement, bidding, evaluation, contract, financial, compliance, communication, documents, records, intelligence, integration, content, and support. This schema separation gives each domain a database namespace that mirrors the backend module architecture.

8.1 Multi-Schema Data Architecture

The multi-schema design is a form of logical database modularity. It does not require separate physical databases, but it prevents the data model from becoming a single undifferentiated table space. In practical terms, identity tables can evolve separately from bidding tables, and compliance records can be reviewed without mistaking them for operational procurement entities.

Schema Representative Models Purpose
identity User, Account, Session, VerificationProfile, DigitalSignature Authentication, account identity, verification, signatures, and session state.
organization Organization, OrganizationMember, OrganizationCapability, BuyerProfile, SupplierProfile Company ownership, membership, capability, trust, and risk context.
procurement Tender, ProcurementPlan, TenderRequirement, TenderAmendment, SavedTender Planning, tender creation, publication, amendments, requirements, and marketplace visibility.
bidding Bid, BidVersion, BidDocument, BidResponse, BidSample Supplier responses, bid packaging, administrative envelopes, receipts, and samples.
evaluation EvaluationWorkspace, WorkflowAssignment, EvaluationCriterion, EvaluationScore Evaluation stages, evaluator access, scoring, and recommendation preparation.
contract AwardRecommendation, AwardGroup, AwardWinner, Contract, ContractMilestone, SupplierPerformanceRecord Award approval, award grouping, contract negotiation, performance, lifecycle management, and close-out.
financial PurchaseOrder, Invoice, BudgetCommitment, PaymentApproval, PaymentConfirmation Purchase orders, invoicing, budget controls, payment approval, and settlement evidence.
compliance AuditEvent, ComplianceCase, ComplianceRule, RiskSignal, AdminAction Governance, traceability, rules, risk monitoring, investigation, enforcement, and audit history.
communication, records, documents, support CommunicationItem, RecordEntry, DocumentObject, SupportTicket Collaboration, record preservation, document metadata, and user assistance.

8.2 Core Entity Relationships

ProcureX is modeled around organizations and workflow evidence. A User owns an Account and participates in an Organization through OrganizationMember. The organization receives OrganizationCapability records such as BUYER and SUPPLIER. A buyer organization owns tenders through buyerOrgId, while submitted bids contain both buyerOrgId and supplierOrgId. This makes procurement access a relationship between organizations, not a property of a single login role. 

User -> Account -> Session
User -> OrganizationMember -> Organization
Organization -> OrganizationCapability -> BUYER | SUPPLIER
Organization(BUYER) -> Tender -> TenderRequirement | TenderMilestone | TenderCommercialItem
Organization(SUPPLIER) -> Bid -> BidVersion | BidDocument | BidResponse | BidReceipt
Tender -> EvaluationWorkspace -> EvaluationCriterion | EvaluationScore
EvaluationWorkspace -> AwardRecommendation -> AwardGroup -> AwardWinner
AwardRecommendation -> Contract -> ContractVersion | ContractMilestone | ContractRisk
Contract -> PurchaseOrder -> Invoice -> PaymentApproval | PaymentConfirmation
Workflow activity -> CommunicationItem | RecordEntry | AuditEvent

This relationship model supports a realistic procurement environment. One company may publish tenders in one context and submit bids in another. Evaluators and approvers are represented through workflow assignments and approval records rather than broad global roles. Records, audit events, and compliance cases provide the historical layer needed for accountability.

8.3 Core Data Rules

  • Accounts are only USER or ADMIN.
  • A normal user belongs to a company through OrganizationMember.
  • A company can act as buyer, supplier, or both through OrganizationCapability.
  • Tender ownership uses buyerOrgId.
  • Bid ownership uses both buyerOrgId and supplierOrgId.
  • Evaluation permissions use workflow assignments and RLS-aware access.

These rules prevent a common procurement-system design flaw: treating buyer, supplier, evaluator, and approver as permanent login categories. ProcureX instead treats them as contextual capabilities and workflow responsibilities. This provides a better foundation for organizations that have multiple departments, multiple users, or dual buyer-supplier participation.

8.4 Migration History

The database has evolved through incremental Prisma migrations rather than a single monolithic schema drop. The migration sequence shows the implementation moving from core initialization into identity, production signature behavior, procurement planning, award and contract workflows, compliance administration, bidding uniqueness, tender amendments, administrative envelopes, bid samples, and award groups. 

202605280001_init
202606030002_identity_registration_verification
202606060003_public_content_pages
202606060004_identity_signature_production
202606110001_p1_iam_access_trust
202606110005_procurement_planning
202606150001_award_contract_workflow
202606150002_account_menu_support
202606180001_signing_credentials
202606180002_compliance_admin_rules
202606190001_admin_datastore
202606190002_admin_full_cycle
202606200001_bidding_active_bid_unique
202606240001_contract_lifecycle_mvp
202606250001_award_contract_compliance_workflows
202606260001_award_contract_business_references
202606280001_add_saved_tenders
20260628154217_add_saved_tenders
20260629122511_local_project_run
202607020001_dual_user_capabilities
202607060001_active_bid_uniqueness
202607070001_tender_amendments
20260707095539_add_tender_amendments
202607090001_bidding_administrative_envelope
20260709142321_add_bid_samples
20260710120000_award_groups

The presence of duplicate or local-project migrations in the history should be treated as an implementation-quality observation rather than a functional failure. Before production release, migration naming, consolidation policy, and deployment discipline should be reviewed so production databases receive a clean, deterministic migration stream.

8.5 Row-Level Security

Row-level security protects procurement data by applying database access rules that respect current account and organization context. RLS SQL lives under server/prisma/sql/rls/, and the server database utilities live under server/src/db/. 

server/prisma/schema.prisma
server/prisma/sql/rls/001_app_context.sql
server/prisma/sql/rls/002_enable_rls.sql
server/src/db/context.ts
server/src/db/prisma.ts

The application sets database session variables inside a transaction before running protected work. The variables are then read by PostgreSQL helper functions such as app.current_user_id(), app.current_organization_id(), app.current_account_type(), and app.current_capabilities(). Policy predicates then use helper checks such as app.is_admin(), app.same_org(...), and app.has_capability(...). 

withDbContext(context, work)
  -> set_config('app.current_user_id', ...)
  -> set_config('app.current_organization_id', ...)
  -> set_config('app.current_account_type', ...)
  -> set_config('app.current_capabilities', ...)
  -> execute Prisma work inside the transaction

This design is stronger than client-side filtering alone. Even if an application query is accidentally too broad, RLS policies can still prevent rows from another organization from being returned or modified. The implemented RLS policies cover identity self-access, organization-owned rows, public marketplace tender visibility, buyer-only tender writes, supplier-only bid writes, and evaluation score access through buyer organization context.

8.6 Product Security Model

The most important security decision is that buyer and supplier are not account types. They are organization capabilities. This makes it possible for the same company to buy, supply, or do both while still keeping platform administrators separate from procurement operators.

This security model also clarifies administrative authority. An ADMIN account is a platform compliance account, not a buyer or supplier. Admin access is therefore treated as platform oversight, while procurement actions are attached to organizations and workflow records. This distinction helps preserve accountability because business actions and oversight actions can be audited separately.

8.7 Database Feature Implementation, Results, And Testing

This subsection navigates the database feature by feature. Database implementation was completed through Prisma schema models, PostgreSQL named schemas, generated Prisma Client access, migration files, seed scripts, and row-level security SQL. The design goal was to model each procurement domain explicitly rather than storing business events in a few generic tables. This gives the system stronger traceability, clearer relationships, more meaningful constraints, and better access control.

8.7.1 PostgreSQL Multi-Schema Design

The database uses PostgreSQL schemas to separate the system into identity, organization, procurement, bidding, evaluation, contract, financial, compliance, communication, documents, records, intelligence, integration, content, and support domains. Prisma declares these schemas in the datasource and maps models into the correct namespace using @@schema. This design keeps the database modular without requiring separate physical databases.

The result is an architecture where domain ownership is visible at the persistence layer. Identity tables are not mixed with bidding tables, compliance records are not mixed with tender items, and support tickets do not sit beside contract milestones in an unstructured table. Testing and validation are performed through db:validate, db:migrate, generated Prisma Client checks, repository tests, service tests, and seed scripts that exercise relationships across schemas.

8.7.2 Identity Schema

The identity schema models users, accounts, preferences, policy acceptances, sessions, MFA factors, identity challenges, verification profiles, verification profile history, digital signatures, signing credentials, verification documents, and screening checks. These models exist because procurement access depends on knowing who the user is, whether the account is active, whether the user's organization has been verified, and whether signing or verification evidence exists.

The identity schema connects to organization data through verification profiles and organization membership. It supports frontend registration, sign-in, verification status, profile, and admin review features. The result is a strong identity foundation for procurement workflows. Testing evidence includes identity production-auth tests, delivery-provider tests, signing tests, module validators, seed execution, and RLS policies for users, accounts, sessions, and verification profiles.

8.7.3 Organization Schema

The organization schema models organizations, members, capabilities, organization profiles, buyer profiles, supplier profiles, trust tier history, and permission overrides. It exists to represent companies and institutional participation rather than treating procurement behavior as a simple user role. A normal account can belong to an organization, and that organization can act as buyer, supplier, or both.

This schema connects identity to procurement by giving tenders a buyer organization and bids a supplier organization. It also supports route guards, RLS context, profile review, trust/risk display, and future permission expansion. Validation is supported by Prisma validation, identity verification tests, seed data, RLS verification, and module-validator checks.

8.7.4 Procurement Schema

The procurement schema models tenders, tender amendments, saved tenders, procurement plans, plan lines, tender categories, tender documents, tender requirements, tender milestones, and tender commercial items. It exists to preserve the structure of procurement work: a tender is not only a title and description, but a set of requirements, documents, milestones, commercial expectations, publication status, and amendment history.

Procurement models connect to organization through buyerOrgId and connect to later bidding, evaluation, award, and contract records. The result is a buyer-owned tender data model that can support marketplace visibility and supplier review. Testing includes Prisma validation, procurement repository tests, service tests, controller tests, marketplace seed tests, award-ready seed tests, and migration checks for tender amendments and saved tenders.

8.7.5 Bidding Schema

The bidding schema models bids, bid versions, bid documents, bid responses, bid receipts, and bid samples. It exists to preserve supplier response history and to avoid treating a bid as a single mutable file. Bid versions allow the platform to retain response state over time, while documents, responses, receipts, and samples preserve supporting evidence.

Bidding connects tenders, buyer organizations, and supplier organizations. The data model supports active-bid uniqueness, supplier ownership, buyer visibility, administrative envelopes, and sample tracking. The result is a structured response model that can feed evaluation. Testing evidence includes bidding tests, procurement API tests, active-bid uniqueness migration validation, marketplace demo seed validation, and client bidding workspace tests.

8.7.6 Evaluation Schema

The evaluation schema models evaluation workspaces, workflow assignments, evaluation criteria, and evaluation scores. It exists to turn submitted bids into structured review evidence. Instead of storing a final winner without explanation, the system can preserve who was assigned, which criteria were used, what scores were recorded, and what stage the evaluation reached.

Evaluation connects procurement and bidding records to award recommendations. It supports evaluator journeys, bid evaluation screens, and award-ready demo data. Validation is supported by Prisma schema validation, evaluation intake seed tests, award-ready seed tests, RLS evaluation score policies, and frontend evaluation smoke coverage.

8.7.7 Contract And Award Schema

The contract schema is the richest lifecycle area. It models award recommendations, award groups, award winners, award clauses, negotiations, bid packs, approval steps, approval routes, tie-breakers, feasibility checks, notices, supplier responses, standstill periods, notifications, contracts, contract parties, contract clauses, negotiations, versions, signatures, milestones, milestone evidence, deliverables, acceptances, management plans, mobilization items, KPIs, inspections, risks, variations, issues, disputes, terminations, termination evidence, settlement, replacement plans, close-out, supplier performance, payment schedules, payments, goods inspections, performance scores, warranties, required documents, workflow approvals, and urgent actions.

This schema exists because contract management is not a single award status. It is a long process that includes recommendation, approval, notice, negotiation, execution, performance monitoring, payment, dispute, termination, and close-out. It connects evaluation output to financial and records data. Testing includes award-contract service tests, seed-demo tests, award-ready demo tests, awards-contract client flow tests, migration validation, and UI parity screenshots for award, contract negotiation, and post-award tracking.

8.7.8 Financial Schema

The financial schema models purchase orders, invoices, budget commitments, three-way match results, payment approvals, and payment confirmations. It exists because procurement implementation must support what happens after the contract is created: purchasing, invoicing, approval, payment, and reconciliation evidence.

Financial records connect to buyer organizations, supplier organizations, contracts, and invoices. The result is a database layer capable of connecting contract performance to payment activity. Testing evidence includes Prisma validation, award-contract service tests where payments and invoices support lifecycle flows, seed data inspection, and migration validation.

8.7.9 Compliance Schema

The compliance schema models audit events, compliance cases, compliance rules, risk signals, supplier risk profiles, risk forecasts, collusion alerts, compliance reviews, violation cases, evidence, enforcement records, appeal records, and admin actions. It exists to preserve governance, investigation, and oversight evidence.

Compliance data connects to organizations, users, procurement records, and admin actions. The result is a platform-level governance model separate from buyer and supplier workflow data. Testing includes compliance-admin service tests, RLS verification for owner-aware compliance tables, admin frontend tests, module-validator tests, and Prisma validation.

8.7.10 Communication Schema

The communication schema models communication items and attachments. It exists to keep procurement-related messages inside the system and to connect messages with workflow context. Communication kind, priority, and status values allow the platform to represent different message types and urgency levels.

Communication records can support tender clarification, contract negotiation, administrative follow-up, and support evidence. The result is a structured collaboration model.
