Testing includes communication repository tests, service tests, client communication center tests, Prisma validation, and screenshot evidence.

8.7.11 Documents Schema

The documents schema models document objects. It exists to provide a shared metadata layer for files used throughout identity verification, tender documents, bid attachments, contract evidence, inspection evidence, termination evidence, and support attachments. By separating document metadata from domain tables, the system can reuse document handling while preserving domain-specific references.

The documents schema connects to organization ownership and business records through references. It is prepared for object storage through AWS S3-ready dependencies and upload parsing through Busboy-ready dependencies. Validation includes Prisma schema validation, module registration checks, document-related business tests in other modules, and RLS policies for document owner organization isolation.

8.7.12 Records Schema

The records schema models record entries. It exists to provide an operational history layer for users and administrators. While audit events capture system-level evidence, records can expose workflow history in a form that supports user review, procurement follow-up, and compliance reporting.

Record entries connect to procurement, bidding, award, contract, communication, and organization contexts. The result is a database foundation for the records-history page. Testing includes Prisma validation, RLS verification, records module validation, page smoke coverage, and screenshot parity.

8.7.13 Intelligence Schema

The intelligence schema models market snapshots, price benchmarks, and supplier match signals. It exists to prepare the platform for analytics, benchmarking, supplier recommendation, risk scoring, and future machine learning outputs. The schema separates intelligence records from transactional procurement records so analytical expansion does not destabilize core workflows.

Intelligence records can connect to tenders, suppliers, categories, and market data. The result is an analytics-ready data boundary. Testing includes intelligence module tests, Prisma validation, module registration checks, and future compatibility with the reserved ml-service architecture.

8.7.14 Integration Schema

The integration schema models external systems, registry records, integration sync runs, and integration events. It exists to support controlled communication with external registries, government or compliance services, document systems, and enterprise systems.

Integration records preserve sync history, source system information, external identifiers, and events. The result is a database foundation for auditable integrations rather than opaque external calls. Testing includes Prisma validation, module-validator tests, app registration checks, and future sync-run tests as external integrations become active.

8.7.15 Content And Public-Page Schema

The content schema models public page versions and related public content state. It exists so public-facing information such as terms, privacy policy, and about-page content can be represented as managed platform data rather than only static code. Public page keys and status enums support draft, published, and archived lifecycle behavior.

The result is a content model that can support public page management while keeping unauthenticated content separate from procurement data. Testing includes public service tests, public client API tests, Prisma validation, and page smoke tests for public screens.

8.7.16 Support Schema

The support schema models support tickets and support ticket comments. It exists to give the platform an operational help channel. Support priority and status enums allow the system to distinguish routine, urgent, resolved, and ongoing assistance cases.

Support records connect users and organizations to assistance history. The result is a data foundation for support pages and future admin support workflows. Testing includes support service tests, client support page tests, Prisma validation, module-validator tests, and app registration checks.

8.7.17 RLS And Tenant Isolation

Row-level security was implemented through PostgreSQL helper functions and policies. The application sets context variables inside withDbContext(): app.current_user_id, app.current_organization_id, app.current_account_type, and app.current_capabilities. SQL helper functions read those values as app.current_user_id(), app.current_organization_id(), app.current_account_type(), and app.current_capabilities().

Policies then use checks such as app.is_admin(), app.same_org(...), and app.has_capability(...). This means access can be enforced by the database, not only by frontend screens or service filters. The result is tenant isolation for organization-owned rows and self-access policies for identity records. Validation includes verify:rls, database validation scripts, policy review, and service tests that depend on correct organization context.

8.7.18 Migrations And Seed-Data Validation

Migrations were implemented as incremental Prisma migration directories. They capture schema evolution from initialization through identity, public content, signatures, IAM access, procurement planning, awards, support, compliance rules, admin datastore, bidding uniqueness, saved tenders, dual capabilities, tender amendments, administrative envelopes, bid samples, and award groups.

Seed scripts were implemented to create repeatable development and demo states. The base seed prepares core data, while optional seeds prepare marketplace tenders, award-ready evaluation data, award-contract demo workflows, and evaluation intake scenarios. db:seed:twice validates idempotency by running the seed process more than once. The result is a database that can be rebuilt, demonstrated, and tested consistently. Validation includes db:validate, db:migrate, db:seed, db:seed:twice, seed-specific tests, repository tests, and service tests.

The main database result is a normalized, auditable model of the procurement lifecycle. Instead of only storing high-level tender and bid records, the database stores the supporting evidence required for verification, evaluation, award approval, contract execution, payment, compliance, communication, and records review. This makes the database a governance asset, not merely an application storage layer.

9. Implementation And Technical Quality

Technical quality is evaluated by examining how the implementation handles separation of concerns, maintainability, security, traceability, scalability, and known limitations. ProcureX is not only a collection of screens and tables; it is a procurement information system whose correctness depends on process integrity, evidence preservation, and controlled access.

9.1 Separation Of Concerns

The monorepo separates client, server, shared contracts, documentation, scripts, and future machine-learning services. Inside the server, modules separate HTTP routing from domain behavior and persistence. Inside the database, PostgreSQL schemas separate identity, organization, procurement, bidding, evaluation, contract, financial, compliance, records, and support domains. This repeated separation at repository, application, and database levels is a major architectural strength because it reduces accidental coupling.

9.2 Maintainability

Maintainability is supported by predictable folders, typed TypeScript implementation, Prisma models, Zod validation, shared contracts, and focused tests. A developer who understands one module can usually navigate another because the same structural pattern appears across the backend. The frontend uses a comparable feature-oriented structure, so procurement, bidding, evaluation, awards, communication, records, public content, and admin surfaces can evolve without every change becoming an application-wide refactor.

The main maintainability risk is breadth. The data model is already extensive, especially in awards, contracts, lifecycle management, compliance, and finance. As business logic becomes richer, the project should keep service functions focused, expand repository tests, and resist embedding cross-domain rules in UI components.

9.3 Security Posture

The current security posture includes browser security headers, restricted CORS, bounded JSON parsing, route guards on the client, identity/session APIs on the server, admin route separation, Prisma database access, and PostgreSQL RLS foundations. The most important security principle is organization-aware access: data visibility should follow the current user, organization, account type, and capability context.

Additional production hardening should focus on complete authentication token policy, secure cookie or bearer-token handling, production email and SMS provider configuration, stricter audit coverage for every decision-changing action, file upload scanning, and repeatable RLS verification in CI or deployment checks.

9.4 Traceability And Auditability

Procurement systems must explain why a decision was made, who made it, what evidence was available, and which records support the result. ProcureX addresses this through explicit models for audit events, record entries, communication items, verification history, screening checks, approval steps, award notices, contract versions, evidence, disputes, termination records, payment approvals, and compliance cases.

The academic value of this design is that records are not treated as an afterthought. They are modeled as part of the procurement lifecycle, allowing operational activity to become reviewable evidence.

9.5 Scalability Readiness

ProcureX is prepared for moderate architectural scaling because the frontend, backend, database model, and future intelligence service are separated. The backend modules can be optimized independently, Prisma repositories can be profiled per workflow, and the future ml-service can host supplier matching, risk analysis, or benchmark workloads without overloading the Express application with machine-learning concerns.

Scalability work should remain evidence-driven. The next production steps should include query profiling, index review for high-volume tables, pagination enforcement, background job boundaries for heavy document or notification work, and caching only where repeated reads have been measured.

9.6 Limitations And Technical Debt

The implementation is strong as a foundation, but several limitations remain. Some modules are more mature than others, and several backend boundaries exist ahead of complete business behavior. Migration history contains local or repeated naming that should be cleaned before production release. RLS foundations exist, but sensitive workflows should receive expanded policy coverage and automated verification. Finally, screenshot parity validates visual alignment but does not replace end-to-end workflow testing against live APIs.

10. Module-By-Module Workflow Implementation

ProcureX is organized by workflow modules. Each workflow has a visible user interface and a backend/data model boundary that supports future production behavior.

10.1 Procurement Lifecycle

Procurement covers opportunity discovery, tender creation, tender publication, tender details, tender documents, supplier tender views, and procurement guidance.

10.2 Bidding And Evaluation

Bidding supports supplier response preparation, bid versions, documents, responses, and receipts. Evaluation supports scoring workspaces, criteria, evaluator assignments, stages, and award recommendation handoff.

10.3 Awards And Contracts

Awards and contracts convert evaluation outcomes into recommendations, approvals, contract versions, purchase orders, invoices, and post-award tracking.

10.4 Communication And Records

Communication and records support collaboration and traceability. They preserve procurement messages, attachments, record entries, and audit evidence.

10.5 Administration And Compliance

Administration supports platform oversight. Admin accounts handle verification, compliance review, user management, audit review, analytics, and search. Admin is a platform compliance role and is separate from buyer or supplier operation.

11. Deep User Journey Implementation

This section explains how users move through ProcureX from first contact to completed procurement activity. Each journey stage links a visible screen from the ProcureX reference UI to the implementation behind it. The screenshots are visual evidence from procurex-ui; the production implementation is owned by the React client, route guards, backend modules, Prisma data model, and RLS-aware access foundation.

11.1 Public Discovery Journey

The public journey is the entry point for visitors who have not yet authenticated. Its purpose is to build confidence, explain the platform, expose marketplace value, and move the visitor toward registration, sign-in, or contact.

Stage 1: Visitor Lands On ProcureX

User GoalUnderstand what ProcureX is and decide whether the platform is relevant for procurement work.
Screen UsedThe welcome page introduces the procurement platform and gives users a clear first impression of the product.
Actions AvailableThe visitor can navigate to public information pages, explore opportunities, register, sign in, or contact the platform team.
System BehaviorThe React public route renders without requiring a session. The public page is mapped through the ProcureX page registry and uses the reference UI wording and layout.
Data Or ModulePublic frontend module, static page assets, shared public shell, and route mapping.
Next StepThe visitor proceeds to learn about ProcureX, inspect the guest marketplace, or begin registration.

Stage 2: Visitor Reviews Platform Information

User GoalLearn the purpose of the platform before creating an account or participating in procurement.
Screen UsedThe About ProcureX page provides product background and positions the platform as a structured procurement workspace.
Actions AvailableThe visitor can continue exploring, return to marketplace discovery, contact the platform, or move toward onboarding.
System BehaviorThe page remains public and does not depend on authentication. It is implemented as a production React page derived from the reference UI.
Data Or ModulePublic frontend feature, static content, route registry, and public navigation.
Next StepThe visitor usually proceeds to guest marketplace review or account registration.

Stage 3: Visitor Explores Public Opportunities

User GoalPreview procurement opportunities and understand why registration is useful.
Screen UsedThe guest marketplace gives a controlled public view of procurement opportunity discovery.
Actions AvailableThe visitor can inspect opportunity summaries, compare marketplace value, and decide to register or sign in for full participation.
System BehaviorThe public route exposes marketplace context without granting access to protected bidding or tender management workflows.
Data Or ModulePublic feature, procurement marketplace UI, future procurement API boundary, and opportunity fixture/reference content.
Next StepA supplier or buyer candidate starts onboarding through role selection and registration.

Stage 4: Visitor Requests Help Or Clarification

User GoalAsk questions before joining, request support, or initiate a formal platform inquiry.
Screen UsedThe contact page provides a public communication point outside the authenticated workspace.
Actions AvailableThe visitor can review contact options and use the page as a bridge to platform support or onboarding.
System BehaviorThe public route remains outside protected workflow modules while preparing the path for future communication API integration.
Data Or ModulePublic frontend feature and future communication module boundary.
Next StepThe visitor either contacts the platform team or returns to registration.

Stage 5: Visitor Reviews Trust And Legal Pages

User GoalUnderstand privacy, legal obligations, and platform expectations before creating an account.
Screen UsedPrivacy Policy and Terms and Conditions pages provide formal trust and compliance information.
Actions AvailableThe user can read policy information, return to public discovery, or proceed with registration.
System BehaviorThese pages are public routes and are implemented as static reference-derived React pages.
Data Or ModulePublic feature and compliance-oriented documentation content.
Next StepThe user continues to role selection or registration after reviewing policy terms.

11.2 Registration And Identity Journey

The onboarding journey converts a public visitor into an authenticated platform user. It captures account intent, registration details, authentication, verification, and profile status.

Stage 1: User Selects Their Entry Path

User GoalChoose the most relevant path for joining ProcureX based on intended platform use.
Screen UsedThe role selection page gives the user a guided pre-registration decision point.
Actions AvailableThe user can indicate buyer, supplier, or related participation intent and continue into registration.
System BehaviorThe frontend treats this as a public navigation step. The deeper product rule remains that account type is not buyer or supplier; organization capabilities represent those behaviors later.
Data Or ModuleAuth frontend feature, route registry, shared identity rules, and organization capability model.
Next StepThe user opens the registration form and starts account creation.

Stage 2: User Creates An Account

User GoalCreate a ProcureX identity that can later be connected to an organization.
Screen UsedThe registration page collects the information required to start onboarding.
Actions AvailableThe user enters registration details, submits the form, and moves into verification and account activation steps.
System BehaviorThe identity API supports registration start, OTP verification, email activation, and password setup. The frontend keeps the visible onboarding flow aligned with the reference UI.
Data Or Module/api/identity, User, Account, Session, IdentityChallenge, and verification-related models.
Next StepThe user signs in or completes identity verification depending on account state.

Stage 3: User Signs In

User GoalAuthenticate and enter the protected ProcureX application surface.
Screen UsedThe sign-in page accepts credentials and starts the authenticated session.
Actions AvailableThe user can sign in, recover a forgotten password, or return to registration if they do not have an account.
System BehaviorThe identity backend validates credentials and the frontend stores session state through the configured auth token flow. Route guards then decide where the user may navigate.
Data Or ModuleAuth frontend feature, identity backend, Account, Session, auth token utility, and route guards.
Next StepThe user proceeds to verification if not approved, or to the workspace if verified.

Stage 4: User Completes Identity And Company Verification

User GoalSubmit required profile, registry, and verification information so the organization can participate in protected procurement workflows.
Screen UsedThe identity verification screen supports verification draft, registry lookup, and final submission behavior.
Actions AvailableThe user can review company data, save verification details, submit documents, and send the profile for admin review.
System BehaviorThe identity module stores verification profile and document state. Admin users later review and approve or reject the verification request.
Data Or ModuleVerificationProfile, VerificationDocument, Organization, OrganizationMember, identity routes, and admin verification routes.
Next StepThe user monitors verification status until the organization is approved for protected workflows.

Stage 5: User Tracks Verification Status

User GoalKnow whether the account and organization can access the operational ProcureX workspace.
Screen UsedThe verification status page shows the current profile and approval state.
Actions AvailableThe user can review profile state, update details where allowed, and wait for or respond to admin review.
System BehaviorRoute guards allow identity/profile access before full verification but protect operational modules until verification requirements are satisfied.
Data Or ModuleIdentity frontend, verification APIs, ProtectedRoute, verification status model, and account profile state.
Next StepOnce approved, the user enters the app launcher and workspace dashboard.

11.3 Workspace Entry Journey

The workspace journey begins after authentication and verification. It gives the organization user a single place to choose modules and monitor procurement work.

Stage 1: Verified User Opens The App Launcher

User GoalChoose the module needed for the current procurement task.
Screen UsedThe app launcher presents the system as a set of operational applications.
Actions AvailableThe user can open dashboard, procurement, bidding, evaluation, awards, communication, records, or permitted admin areas.
System BehaviorThe route is protected and requires verification. The frontend determines visible navigation from available route surfaces and session context.
Data Or ModuleWorkspace feature, app router, ProtectedRoute, session endpoint, and organization capability rules.
Next StepThe user opens the dashboard or jumps directly to the required workflow module.

Stage 2: Verified User Reviews Workspace Dashboard

User GoalUnderstand current procurement activity and select the next task.
Screen UsedThe dashboard acts as a command center for buyer, supplier, and workflow users.
Actions AvailableThe user can review status cards, navigate to active tenders, inspect tasks, and open workflow modules.
System BehaviorThe React workspace feature renders a verified page and can combine fixture, API, and future live data into operational summaries.
Data Or ModuleWorkspace feature, shared dashboard components, session data, procurement data, bidding data, evaluation state, and records indicators.
Next StepThe user continues into buyer procurement, supplier participation, evaluation, awards, communication, or records.

11.4 Buyer Procurement Journey

The buyer journey moves from market awareness to tender creation, publication, detail review, and document management. Buyer behavior is connected to organization capability, not to a separate buyer login role.

Stage 1: Buyer Reviews Marketplace Context

User GoalUnderstand active opportunities and market context before creating or managing tenders.
Screen UsedThe marketplace screen shows procurement opportunities and supports both discovery and tender context.
Actions AvailableThe buyer can inspect opportunity structure, navigate to tender creation, or review existing tender-related surfaces.
System BehaviorThe protected procurement route renders only for verified users. Access can later be refined using organization capabilities and RLS policies.
Data Or ModuleProcurement frontend feature, procurement backend, Tender model, OrganizationCapability, and route guards.
Next StepThe buyer opens the create tender workflow.

Stage 2: Buyer Creates A Tender

User GoalDefine the procurement need, tender details, requirements, commercial items, dates, and documentation expectations.
Screen UsedThe create tender screen provides the buyer-facing entry point for building a structured tender.
Actions AvailableThe buyer can enter tender information, organize requirements, prepare documents, and move toward publication readiness.
System BehaviorThe frontend captures the visible workflow while the procurement module boundary is responsible for tender persistence and validation as behavior is expanded.
Data Or ModuleTender, TenderRequirement, TenderMilestone, TenderCommercialItem, TenderDocument, procurement service, and validators.
Next StepThe buyer prepares the tender for publication.

Stage 3: Buyer Publishes The Tender

User GoalReview the tender and make it available to eligible suppliers.
Screen UsedThe tender publication screen represents the transition from draft preparation to published opportunity.
Actions AvailableThe buyer can review publication fields, confirm readiness, and progress the tender to the next lifecycle state.
System BehaviorThe procurement module owns tender status transitions and visibility rules. The database tracks tender status and ownership by buyer organization.
Data Or ModuleTenderStatus, Visibility, ProcurementMethod, Tender, buyerOrgId, and procurement API boundary.
Next StepSuppliers discover the tender and begin reviewing details.

Stage 4: Buyer Reviews Tender Details

User GoalInspect the tender as a complete operational record before or after publication.
Screen UsedThe tender details screen presents tender metadata, sections, status, and related workflow context.
Actions AvailableThe buyer can review details, navigate to documents, monitor status, and prepare for bidding and evaluation phases.
System BehaviorThe frontend displays tender state and route context. The backend procurement module is the boundary for retrieving and updating tender data.
Data Or ModuleTender, TenderCategory, TenderRequirement, TenderMilestone, and procurement route state.
Next StepThe buyer manages tender documents or monitors supplier response activity.

Stage 5: Buyer Manages Tender Documents

User GoalAttach, review, and organize procurement documents that suppliers and evaluators need.
Screen UsedThe tender document screen presents documents as part of the tender package.
Actions AvailableThe buyer can review document details, prepare document availability, and support later audit or evaluation needs.
System BehaviorThe documents and procurement modules separate document object handling from tender lifecycle state.
Data Or ModuleTenderDocument, DocumentObject, documents module, procurement module, and records/audit linkage.
Next StepThe published tender moves into supplier review and bidding.

11.5 Supplier Participation Journey

The supplier journey moves from understanding the process to discovering opportunities, reviewing tender details, and preparing a bid response.

Stage 1: Supplier Learns The Participation Process

User GoalUnderstand how to participate in tenders and what steps are required before submitting bids.
Screen UsedThe supplier journey page explains the procurement guide and supplier process.
Actions AvailableThe supplier can review process guidance and move into marketplace discovery or tender review.
System BehaviorThe protected procurement guide route gives verified users process orientation before operational action.
Data Or ModuleProcurement guide page, procurement frontend feature, route guards, and organization capability context.
Next StepThe supplier opens the marketplace to find suitable opportunities.

Stage 2: Supplier Discovers Opportunities

User GoalFind tenders that match supplier services, sector, capability, or eligibility.
Screen UsedThe supplier marketplace displays procurement opportunities for consideration.
Actions AvailableThe supplier can scan opportunities, inspect summaries, filter conceptually by fit, and open tender details.
System BehaviorThe procurement frontend renders marketplace information under a verified route. Future supplier matching can be layered through intelligence APIs.
Data Or ModuleProcurement module, Tender, SupplierProfile, SupplierMatchSignal, and intelligence module boundary.
Next StepThe supplier opens a tender detail page for deeper review.

Stage 3: Supplier Reviews Tender Detail

User GoalUnderstand tender requirements, deadlines, documents, and whether to participate.
Screen UsedThe supplier tender detail screen presents opportunity information from the supplier perspective.
Actions AvailableThe supplier can review eligibility, inspect documents, assess requirements, and decide whether to start a bid.
System BehaviorThe procurement route displays tender context while access rules protect tender details according to publication and organization permissions.
Data Or ModuleTender, TenderDocument, TenderRequirement, supplier organization context, procurement API, and RLS policies.
Next StepThe supplier opens the bidding workspace to prepare a submission.

Stage 4: Supplier Prepares A Bid

User GoalPrepare, organize, and submit a compliant response to a tender.
Screen UsedThe bidding workspace gives suppliers a dedicated operational surface for bid work.
Actions AvailableThe supplier can manage bid versions, documents, responses, status, and submission readiness.
System BehaviorThe bidding module owns bid state and links bids to both buyer and supplier organizations. This supports access control from both sides of the procurement transaction.
Data Or ModuleBid, BidVersion, BidDocument, BidResponse, BidReceipt, buyerOrgId, supplierOrgId, and bidding API boundary.
Next StepSubmitted bids proceed to evaluation.

11.6 Evaluator Journey

Evaluation is the transition from supplier submission to structured procurement decision. Evaluators review bid responses, score criteria, and support award recommendations.

Stage 1: Evaluator Reviews And Scores Bids

User GoalEvaluate bids fairly against criteria and prepare a defensible decision record.
Screen UsedThe bid evaluation screen organizes evaluation work, scoring, stages, and review context.
Actions AvailableThe evaluator can review suppliers, compare bid responses, apply scores, inspect criteria, and contribute to evaluation outcomes.
System BehaviorEvaluation permissions are based on workflow assignments, not broad account roles. The evaluation module controls workspace status, criteria, score records, and stage transitions.
Data Or ModuleEvaluationWorkspace, WorkflowAssignment, EvaluationCriterion, EvaluationScore, EvaluationStage, and evaluation API boundary.
Next StepEvaluation results are moved into award recommendation and approval review.

11.7 Approver And Award Reviewer Journey

The award reviewer journey converts evaluation output into formal recommendation, approval, and contract readiness.

Stage 1: Reviewer Opens Awarding Workspace

User GoalReview procurement outcomes after evaluation and understand award readiness.
Screen UsedThe awarding and contracts screen centralizes post-evaluation decisions.
Actions AvailableThe reviewer can inspect recommendations, approval state, contract status, and post-award activity.
System BehaviorThe award-contract module links evaluation results to recommendation and contract records. Route guards keep this workflow inside verified access.
Data Or ModuleAwardRecommendation, ApprovalStep, Contract, ContractVersion, PurchaseOrder, Invoice, and award-contract API boundary.
Next StepThe reviewer opens the recommendation page for approval-focused review.

Stage 2: Reviewer Examines Award Recommendation

User GoalValidate the recommended supplier, evaluation basis, and approval readiness.
Screen UsedThe award recommendation screen presents the decision package for review.
Actions AvailableThe reviewer can inspect recommendation details, review approval steps, and prepare the recommendation for decision.
System BehaviorThe backend stores recommendation status and approval steps so the decision can be audited later.
Data Or ModuleAwardRecommendation, RecommendationStatus, ApprovalStep, ApprovalStatus, evaluation linkage, and audit events.
Next StepApproved awards proceed into contract negotiation.

11.8 Contract Participant Journey

Contract participants work after award approval. Their focus is negotiation, version review, signature readiness, delivery tracking, and invoice or purchase order follow-up.

Stage 1: Participant Negotiates Contract Terms

User GoalMove from award decision to agreed contract terms.
Screen UsedThe contract negotiation screen supports discussion, review, and negotiation of contract terms.
Actions AvailableParticipants can review contract details, inspect negotiation context, and progress agreement activity.
System BehaviorThe award-contract module maintains contract records and versions so agreement history can be retained.
Data Or ModuleContract, ContractVersion, ContractStatus, SignatureStatus, CommunicationItem, and award-contract API boundary.
Next StepThe contract moves into execution and post-award tracking.

Stage 2: Participant Tracks Post-Award Delivery

User GoalTrack contract execution after the award has been made.
Screen UsedThe post-award tracking screen monitors contract progress, fulfillment, and operational follow-up.
Actions AvailableParticipants can review purchase orders, invoice status, delivery progress, and contract activity.
System BehaviorThe financial and award-contract modules model purchase orders and invoices while records and audit events preserve traceability.
Data Or ModulePurchaseOrder, Invoice, InvoiceStatus, Contract, RecordEntry, AuditEvent, and financial API boundary.
Next StepUsers communicate updates or review records as delivery continues.

11.9 Communication And Records Journey

Communication and records support collaboration, transparency, and auditability across the full procurement lifecycle.

Stage 1: User Communicates About Procurement Work

User GoalSend or review structured communication related to procurement activity.
Screen UsedThe communication center organizes workflow-related messages and updates.
Actions AvailableThe user can review messages, inspect priorities, follow conversation context, and coordinate with other participants.
System BehaviorThe communication module stores message records and attachments so important updates can be connected to procurement workflows.
Data Or ModuleCommunicationItem, CommunicationAttachment, CommunicationKind, CommunicationPriority, and communication API boundary.
Next StepThe user proceeds with workflow action or records review based on the communication outcome.

Stage 2: User Reviews Records And History

User GoalConfirm what happened, when it happened, and which workflow record supports the decision.
Screen UsedThe records history screen provides an auditable view of procurement activity.
Actions AvailableThe user can review record entries, inspect historical events, and use the history for accountability.
System BehaviorThe records and audit models preserve workflow evidence across tender, bid, evaluation, award, contract, and communication activity.
Data Or ModuleRecordEntry, AuditEvent, records API boundary, compliance admin linkage, and RLS access context.
Next StepThe user uses the record for operational follow-up, compliance review, or audit evidence.

11.10 Platform Administrator Journey

Admin users operate the compliance and oversight surface. They are not buyer or supplier users. Their journey focuses on verification review, platform search, user oversight, compliance cases, analytics, and audit.

Stage 1: Admin Opens Platform Dashboard

User GoalMonitor platform health, verification activity, compliance state, and operational indicators.
Screen UsedThe admin dashboard gives administrators a consolidated oversight surface.
Actions AvailableThe admin can move to search, users, compliance, analytics, or audit workflows.
System BehaviorThe route is wrapped by AdminRoute, which separates platform administration from normal user workflows.
Data Or ModuleAdmin frontend feature, compliance-admin backend, identity admin routes, session account type, and audit models.
Next StepThe admin investigates specific users, records, cases, or activity trends.

Stage 2: Admin Searches Across The Platform

User GoalFind users, organizations, tenders, records, or compliance items that need attention.
Screen UsedThe admin search page supports investigative discovery across platform data.
Actions AvailableThe admin can search, inspect results, and navigate into the relevant admin workflow.
System BehaviorAdmin-only access protects search behavior while backend modules provide domain boundaries for the records being searched.
Data Or ModuleCompliance-admin feature, identity module, procurement records, audit events, and integration-ready search boundaries.
Next StepThe admin opens user management, compliance, or audit detail depending on findings.

Stage 3: Admin Reviews Users And Organizations

User GoalReview account status, organization verification, and user-related platform state.
Screen UsedThe admin users screen provides a management and review surface for platform identities.
Actions AvailableThe admin can inspect users, review organizations, and support verification or compliance decisions.
System BehaviorIdentity admin endpoints support listing verification requests and making verification decisions.
Data Or ModuleUser, Account, Organization, OrganizationMember, VerificationProfile, VerificationDocument, and identity admin routes.
Next StepThe admin resolves verification items or escalates compliance concerns.

Stage 4: Admin Handles Compliance Cases

User GoalMonitor risk signals, review compliance cases, and support platform governance.
Screen UsedThe admin compliance screen presents compliance workload and case handling context.
Actions AvailableThe admin can review cases, inspect risk indicators, and track administrative actions.
System BehaviorThe compliance-admin module owns compliance case status, risk signals, and admin action boundaries.
Data Or ModuleComplianceCase, ComplianceCaseStatus, RiskSignal, AdminAction, AuditEvent, and compliance-admin API boundary.
Next StepThe admin moves to analytics for trends or audit for event-level review.

Stage 5: Admin Reviews Analytics

User GoalUnderstand platform trends, activity volumes, compliance indicators, and procurement performance signals.
Screen UsedThe admin analytics page provides a measurement-oriented view of platform activity.
Actions AvailableThe admin can inspect charts, compare indicators, and identify areas requiring operational follow-up.
System BehaviorThe frontend uses charting components while backend module boundaries prepare analytics and intelligence expansion.
Data Or ModuleAdmin analytics feature, compliance-admin module, intelligence module, MarketSnapshot, PriceBenchmark, and RiskSignal.
Next StepThe admin investigates specific anomalies or reviews audit events.

Stage 6: Admin Reviews Audit Evidence

User GoalVerify platform activity and support accountability during review or investigation.
Screen UsedThe admin audit screen exposes event and audit-oriented evidence.
Actions AvailableThe admin can inspect audit activity, review severity, and connect events to users, organizations, or procurement records.
System BehaviorThe audit model records system events and can be connected to compliance, records, identity, and procurement workflows.
Data Or ModuleAuditEvent, AuditSeverity, AdminAction, RecordEntry, compliance-admin module, and records module.
Next StepThe admin closes the review, opens a compliance case, or continues investigation.

Across all journeys, route guards and session state determine whether a screen is public, authenticated, verified, or admin-only. The backend module structure gives every journey a stable API boundary, while Prisma and RLS foundations prepare the system for organization-aware production access control.

12. Testing And Validation

The platform includes test coverage and validation scripts across server, client, database, seed data, and UI parity concerns. The testing strategy is layered: unit tests validate isolated behavior, integration tests validate module interactions, database commands validate schema correctness, seed tests validate repeatable demo data, and screenshot capture validates visual parity with the reference interface.

12.1 Automated Testing

The root test command runs server and client tests: 

npm test

Server tests cover the Express app, module registration, production identity behavior, delivery-provider behavior, procurement controller behavior, procurement repository and service behavior, bidding, communication, compliance admin, dashboard, public content, intelligence, support, award-contract service behavior, signing, and seed-data behavior. These tests are valuable because they verify the domain-service layer rather than relying only on UI smoke coverage.

Client tests cover route guards, authentication routes, page smoke rendering, admin screens, authentication components, identity verification screens, procurement pages, bidding API helpers, communication center, public content, support pages, tender planning, workspace dashboard, shared static-page rendering, language switching, notifications, and the awards-contract flow. Together these tests confirm that protected, public, verified, and admin-only surfaces render according to the expected client-side state.

Validation Layer Representative Coverage Evidence Provided
Server module tests Identity, procurement, bidding, awards, communication, compliance, dashboard, intelligence, public, support. Domain behavior, validation, repository behavior, provider boundaries, and error handling.
Client tests Route guards, page smoke tests, feature components, API helpers, shared components, flow tests. Rendering correctness, navigation protection, interaction behavior, and UI resilience.
Seed tests Marketplace demo, award-ready demo, award-contract demo, evaluation intake demo. Repeatable demo-state generation and realistic workflow fixtures.
Database checks Prisma validation, migration, seed-twice, RLS verification. Schema validity, migration execution, idempotency, and tenant isolation behavior.

12.2 Database Validation

Database validation and setup scripts support local development and RLS verification: 

npm run db:validate
npm run db:migrate
npm run db:seed:twice
npm --workspace server run verify:rls

db:validate confirms that the Prisma schema is internally valid. db:migrate applies development migrations. db:seed:twice is especially important because it tests whether seeding can be repeated without corrupting local data or creating duplicate records. verify:rls validates the security premise that users and organizations must not read or mutate records outside their allowed context.

The project also includes optional demo seed scripts for marketplace tenders, award-ready evaluation data, award-contract workflows, and evaluation intake. These datasets support validation because reviewers can inspect realistic buyer, supplier, evaluator, award, contract, and compliance states without inventing data manually.

12.3 Screenshot Validation

The UI parity capture script validates that the production React target can be compared against the reference prototype. Screenshots are captured for desktop and mobile viewports. 

npm run dev:client
npm --workspace client run ui:parity:screenshots

Screenshot validation is not a replacement for functional tests, but it is a useful visual-regression technique for this project because the production React client is intentionally derived from the static ProcureX prototype. The capture workflow compares the reference prototype and the React target across desktop and mobile viewports. This provides evidence that migration work preserves the intended layout, hierarchy, and user journey.

12.4 Validation Limitations

The current validation strategy gives meaningful coverage, but it should continue to mature. Screenshot validation proves visual similarity, not database correctness. Unit tests prove isolated behavior, not the complete user journey under production infrastructure. RLS verification proves selected isolation policies, but new sensitive tables should be added to the verification suite as they become operationally important. A production-grade validation program should therefore add end-to-end API-backed browser tests, migration deployment checks, file-upload security tests, performance baselines, and audit-log completeness tests.

13. Conclusion And Recommendations

The ProcureX implementation establishes a strong foundation for a production procurement platform. It combines a visual reference workflow, production React pages, modular Express APIs, Prisma database modeling, and organization-aware access rules. The design allows the product to preserve the experience of the original prototype while moving toward a maintainable and secure application architecture.

13.1 Conclusion

The implementation successfully models the major procurement lifecycle areas: public discovery, onboarding, verification, procurement planning, tender management, bidding, evaluation, awards, contracts, communication, records, and administration. The separation of account type from organization capability is especially important because it supports realistic procurement operations where companies may act as buyers, suppliers, or both.

From a system-design perspective, ProcureX demonstrates a coherent layered architecture. The React client owns presentation and navigation, the Express server owns API boundaries and domain orchestration, Prisma owns the typed application data model, PostgreSQL owns relational persistence and RLS enforcement, and documentation plus screenshot assets preserve the design intent. This separation is academically sound because each layer has a distinct responsibility and can be evaluated independently.

The strongest implementation qualities are modularity, traceability, and organization-aware access modeling. The most important remaining risks are production hardening, uneven module maturity, broader RLS coverage, migration hygiene, and the need for more end-to-end tests against live API-backed workflows.

13.2 Recommendations

  1. Continue converting prototype-derived screens into richer production React workflows where backend APIs are ready, while keeping page behavior aligned with actual domain services.
  2. Expand module tests as each domain moves from foundation status to full business behavior, especially for bidding, evaluation, awards, contract lifecycle, finance, and compliance workflows.
  3. Keep UI parity screenshots updated during design or layout changes, but supplement them with API-backed end-to-end tests for critical buyer, supplier, evaluator, approver, and admin journeys.
  4. Strengthen RLS verification as more sensitive procurement records become active, and require new sensitive tables to include access-policy tests before release.
  5. Review migration history before production deployment so local or duplicate migration names do not become long-term operational debt.
  6. Introduce the future intelligence service only after stable integration contracts are defined, including clear ownership for supplier matching, market benchmarking, and risk-scoring outputs.
  7. Add operational monitoring for health checks, API errors, slow database queries, failed delivery-provider calls, audit-log creation, and support-ticket volume.
  8. Document a production data-governance policy covering retention, audit review, evidence handling, file storage, administrative access, and incident response.

Overall, ProcureX is a credible foundation for a production procurement platform. It already contains the essential architectural pieces: lifecycle workflow modules, organization-aware identity modeling, PostgreSQL-backed persistence, security foundations, visual evidence, and a growing test suite. The recommended next phase is not a broad rewrite, but disciplined hardening: complete the highest-value workflow services, prove access isolation, validate live end-to-end journeys, and prepare migration and monitoring practices for production operation.

14. Code-Level Feature Walkthrough

This section explains how to read the ProcureX implementation at code level. It does not reproduce every line of source code, because the source files remain the authoritative implementation, but it describes what each major directory and file group is doing, why the code exists, how feature data moves from screen to server to database, and which tokens, providers, services, and tests support the behavior.

Security note: Provider credentials and API keys must never be copied into academic reports. Environment examples should use placeholders only. If a tracked environment example contains real-looking SMTP, Resend, Briq, Beem, or WhatsApp credentials, those values should be rotated and replaced with non-secret placeholders.

14.1 Repository Reading Map

The implementation is easiest to understand by reading it as a set of cooperating directories. client/src/features contains the user-facing feature modules. client/src/shared contains common API, formatting, type, and component utilities. server/src/modules contains backend domain modules. server/src/security contains access and anti-abuse controls. server/src/db contains Prisma and RLS context utilities. server/prisma/schema.prisma defines the data model, while server/prisma/migrations stores schema evolution. 

client/src/app/                 Router, providers, route guards, app shell
client/src/features/auth/       Sign-in, registration, session state, auth API calls
client/src/features/identity/   Verification and account profile UI
client/src/features/procurement Tender, marketplace, planning, tender detail UI
client/src/features/bidding/    Supplier bid workspace UI and API helpers
client/src/features/evaluation/ Evaluation pages and scoring workflow UI
client/src/features/awardsContracts/ Award, contract, post-award workflow UI
client/src/shared/api/          Axios client, bearer-token storage, API error helpers
server/src/app.ts               Express bootstrap, security headers, CORS, health, error handling
server/src/modules/*            Backend feature modules
server/src/security/*           Turnstile, access policy, rate limiting, runtime security config
server/src/db/*                 Prisma client and database context for RLS
server/prisma/schema.prisma     PostgreSQL multi-schema data model

14.2 Authentication And Token Flow

Authentication uses several different token or code types, and each has a different purpose. The frontend auth slice reads a stored bearer token from client/src/shared/api/authToken.ts, keeps it in Redux auth state, and clears it on sign-out or failed hydration. The token is stored under the browser localStorage key procurex.authToken. The shared Axios client in client/src/shared/api/http.ts reads this value before each request and sends it as Authorization: Bearer <token>.

On the backend, sign-in creates an opaque random session token using Node crypto. The server returns the raw token to the client only once. The database stores a SHA-256 hash of the token as tokenHash, not the raw token. Later requests send the raw bearer token, the server hashes it again, and the identity repository searches for an active matching session. This design reduces damage if the sessions table is exposed, because stored session records do not directly contain reusable raw tokens.

Token Or Code Where It Appears Purpose Why It Is Used
Bearer session token Client localStorage, Axios authorization header, backend identity service. Authenticates API requests after sign-in. Allows stateless HTTP requests while the database keeps revocable session records.
Session token hash identity.sessions.tokenHash. Stores a non-raw representation of the session token. Prevents direct reuse of database session values as bearer tokens.
Turnstile token Registration, sign-in, forgot-password, reset-code flows. Proves a human/browser challenge was completed. Reduces automated abuse of identity endpoints.
Phone OTP code Registration start and phone verification challenges. Confirms the user controls the submitted phone number. Supports identity confidence and prevents unverified phone ownership.
Email activation code Email activation challenge after phone verification. Confirms the user controls the submitted email address. Ensures future account recovery and notifications reach the right user.
Password reset code Forgot-password and reset-password flows. Allows password recovery after email challenge. Avoids immediately revealing whether an account exists while still allowing recovery.
Digital-signature keyphrase Identity verification submission and signing credential code. Unlocks signing behavior for verification payloads. Creates signed evidence for verification consent and submitted identity data.

14.3 Authentication Feature File-By-File

The authentication feature starts on the client. The auth API helper in client/src/features/auth/api/index.ts defines the exact HTTP calls for registration start, OTP verification, OTP resend, email activation, activation resend, password setup, sign-in, forgot password, reset-code resend, reset-code verification, password reset, session lookup, access lookup, and sign-out. The auth slice in client/src/features/auth/slice.ts wraps those calls in Redux Toolkit async thunks, so UI components receive loading, success, error, token, user, and session-expired state consistently.

The server receives those requests through server/src/modules/identity/routes.ts and controller.ts. The validators file checks request body shape. The service file owns behavior: email normalization, phone normalization, OTP generation, password hashing, session token creation, challenge attempt limits, resend cooldowns, audit events, registry lookup, screening, signing, and verification submission. The repository file owns Prisma reads and writes. This is why authentication is not one file: the feature is split so UI state, HTTP transport, validation, business rules, and database persistence each have a clear home. 

Sign-in reading path:
SignInProcurexPage.tsx
  -> auth slice signInWithCredentials()
  -> authApi.signIn()
  -> apiClient POST /api/identity/auth/sign-in
  -> identity routes/controller
  -> identity validators
  -> identity service signIn()
  -> password verification and Turnstile/audit behavior
  -> repository createSession(tokenHash)
  -> response { token, user, expiresAt, isFirstSignIn }
  -> Redux stores token and user
  -> Axios sends Authorization: Bearer token on later requests

14.4 Turnstile Anti-Abuse Flow

Turnstile is used to protect sensitive identity entry points from automated abuse. The client sends a turnstileToken with registration, sign-in, forgot-password, reset-code, and reset-password actions. The server helper in server/src/security/turnstile.ts verifies the token. In local development, the helper allows a local-dev-turnstile: token prefix or Cloudflare's testing secret. In production, it posts the secret and response token to the Cloudflare Turnstile siteverify endpoint.

The purpose of this design is to keep anti-abuse logic outside page components and outside identity business rules. Pages only collect the token, the identity API transports it, and the security helper verifies it. This makes the implementation easier to test and safer to maintain.

14.5 SMS, Email, And WhatsApp Provider Routing

Identity notifications are implemented through provider classes in server/src/modules/identity/notifications.ts. The system supports multiple providers so local development, production email, SMS, and optional WhatsApp delivery can be selected by environment variables without rewriting identity service logic.

Provider Class External Service Used For Selection Variable
DevConsoleIdentityNotifications Local console only Development OTP, activation, and reset code logging. IDENTITY_NOTIFICATION_PROVIDER, IDENTITY_EMAIL_PROVIDER, IDENTITY_PHONE_PROVIDER
SmtpEmailProvider SMTP, configured for local Gmail-style testing Email activation and password reset in non-production. IDENTITY_EMAIL_PROVIDER=smtp
ResendEmailProvider Resend Production email activation and password reset. IDENTITY_EMAIL_PROVIDER=resend or default production email route
BeemSmsProvider Beem Africa SMS API Phone OTP delivery by SMS. IDENTITY_PHONE_PROVIDER=sms and IDENTITY_SMS_PROVIDER=beem
BriqSmsProvider Briq SMS API Phone OTP delivery by SMS. IDENTITY_PHONE_PROVIDER=sms and IDENTITY_SMS_PROVIDER=briq
MetaWhatsAppOtpProvider Meta WhatsApp Cloud API Local/test WhatsApp OTP delivery. IDENTITY_PHONE_PROVIDER=whatsapp
BeemWhatsAppProvider Beem WhatsApp template/session APIs Template and session WhatsApp messages. Called through production notification methods when configured.

The provider decision happens in RoutedIdentityNotifications. First, it decides whether email should use dev-console, SMTP, or Resend. Second, it decides whether phone delivery should use dev-console, SMS, or WhatsApp. If phone delivery is SMS, ProductionIdentityNotifications selects Beem or Briq through IDENTITY_SMS_PROVIDER. This explains who the SMS API provider is: the code supports Beem Africa and Briq, and the active provider is whichever value the runtime environment selects.

14.6 Permissions, Capabilities, And Feature Gates

Permissions are computed in server/src/security/accessPolicy.ts. The code takes account type, verification status, organization capabilities, trust tier, risk level, and screening status, then returns permissions and feature gates. Admin accounts receive platform-wide permissions. Normal users receive procurement permissions through organization capability and trust/risk context. The temporary development switch currently permits core procurement permissions while trust gates are being iterated.

This explains why buyer and supplier are not stored as account types. They are organization capabilities. A user account can remain a normal USER, while the organization determines whether tender creation, bidding, evaluation, award management, contract management, signing, or tracking is available. Frontend route guards consume the session user and feature gates, while backend services call identity methods such as requireSession, requirePermission, or requireAdmin.

14.7 Feature-To-Code Navigation Matrix

Feature Client Files/Directories Server Files/Directories Database Area What To Read For Code Meaning
Authentication client/src/features/auth, client/src/shared/api server/src/modules/identity, server/src/security/turnstile.ts identity.User, identity.Session, identity.IdentityChallenge Read API helper methods first, then Redux thunks, then identity service methods for tokens, hashes, challenges, and sessions.
Identity verification client/src/features/identity identity/service.ts, registryProviders.ts, screeningProviders.ts, signing.ts VerificationProfile, VerificationDocument, DigitalSignature, ScreeningCheck Read registry lookup, profile update, submit verification, signature creation, screening, trust evaluation, and admin decision methods.
Procurement/tenders client/src/features/procurement, client/src/features/tenderPlanning server/src/modules/procurement procurement.Tender, requirements, milestones, documents, amendments, saved tenders. Read procurement service methods for marketplace, create, update, publish, review, save, close, and tender document flows.
Bidding client/src/features/bidding server/src/modules/bidding bidding.Bid, versions, documents, responses, receipts, samples. Read bidding validators and service methods to understand supplier response status, ownership, and submitted evidence.
Evaluation client/src/features/evaluation server/src/modules/evaluation EvaluationWorkspace, assignments, criteria, scores. Read workspace and scoring flows to see how submitted bids become structured review evidence.
Awards/contracts client/src/features/awardsContracts server/src/modules/award-contract contract.AwardRecommendation, award groups, contracts, versions, milestones, risks, payments. Read action forms, role access, service methods, and repository mapping to understand recommendation through post-award lifecycle.
Admin/compliance client/src/features/admin server/src/modules/compliance-admin, identity admin routes ComplianceCase, RiskSignal, AdminAction, AuditEvent Read admin route guards, compliance service methods, verification decisions, and audit/admin action creation.
Documents Feature-specific upload/use surfaces server/src/modules/documents documents.DocumentObject Read bearer-token extraction, session requirement, object metadata persistence, and storage-driver configuration.
Communication, records, support client/src/features/communication, records, support server/src/modules/communication, records, support CommunicationItem, RecordEntry, SupportTicket Read service/repository files to see how collaboration, history, and support evidence are persisted.

14.8 How To Read A File Line By Line

For any feature file, read from imports to exported functions. Imports show dependencies. Constants show policy values, such as expiry minutes, retry limits, feature-gate defaults, provider names, or API paths. Type declarations show the shape of data entering and leaving the file. Helper functions usually normalize, validate, hash, format, or map data. Exported functions, classes, and React components are the public behavior of the file. Tests show what the authors considered important enough to protect. 

Code reading order:
1. Directory name: which feature owns this file?
2. Imports: what does this file depend on?
3. Constants: what policy values does it encode?
4. Types: what data shape does it accept and return?
5. Helpers: what repeated behavior is hidden behind names?
6. Main function/class/component: what business action happens?
7. Repository/API calls: where does data go next?
8. Errors: what can fail, and how is failure reported?
9. Tests: what behavior is verified?

14.9 Example: Reading Authentication End To End

To understand authentication deeply, start in the sign-in page, then move to authApi.signIn(), then the Redux thunk signInWithCredentials, then the Axios client, then identity routes, controller, validator, service, and repository. The key idea is that the password is verified only on the server, the bearer token is generated only on the server, the database stores the token hash, and the browser stores only the raw token needed to call the API.

The same reading technique applies to every feature. Tender creation starts in the create-tender UI, moves through procurement API helpers, then server procurement routes, then validators, then service permission checks, then repository writes to the procurement schema. Bidding starts in supplier tender detail or bidding workspace, then moves through bidding API helpers and bidding module services. Awarding starts in evaluation or award recommendation screens, then moves through award-contract action forms, role access, server validators, lifecycle services, and contract schema records.

14.10 Testing As Code Explanation

Tests are part of the explanation of how features are used. Authentication tests explain session creation, provider behavior, and production restrictions. Procurement tests explain tender creation, publish rules, saved tenders, document downloads, and permission checks. Bidding tests explain supplier response behavior. Award-contract tests explain lifecycle actions. Client tests explain which screens must render, which route guards must block access, and which components must respond to user actions. Seed tests explain how realistic datasets are created for marketplace, evaluation, and awards.

Therefore, a complete academic reading of ProcureX should include tests beside implementation files. The implementation file tells what the system does. The test file tells what behavior the project currently promises to preserve.

15. Engineering Handover Deep Dive

This section expands the implementation report into a handover manual for engineers who need to understand, maintain, test, and extend ProcureX. It is intentionally long and print-oriented: each handover plate starts on a fresh printed page and ties a system behavior back to concrete client, server, database, route, screenshot, or test evidence.

15.1 Engineering Handover Orientation

Purpose, reading order, and ownership boundaries for maintainers.

This chapter is written as an engineering handover layer on top of the formal report. Each plate is intentionally print-friendly: it starts on a new page, names the owned behavior, identifies the code paths or runtime contracts involved, and explains how a maintainer should reason about the implementation without changing application behavior.

The handover scope is the implemented ProcureX workspace as represented in the current repository. The frontend is the React application in client/src; the backend is the Express and Prisma application in server/src; shared account and organization contracts live in shared/src; local infrastructure is managed by Docker Compose. The static procurex-ui prototype remains a visual and workflow reference, while the production application is the React client registered through the page registry and router.

The report avoids speculative implementation claims. When a subsystem is documented here, the text describes an observable route, component, module boundary, schema model, script, test, or screenshot asset that exists in the repository.

Reading NeedStart in the page registry, route file, module route file, or Prisma schema depending on whether the question is about UI, transport, business behavior, or persistence.
Layer OrderBrowser route, feature component, API helper, Express route, controller, validator, service, repository, Prisma model, test.
Main RuleBuyer and supplier are organization capabilities. Login account type remains USER or ADMIN.

15.2.1 Module Handover: Public Content And Marketplace Entry

Implemented module boundary: /api/public.

Public Content And Marketplace Entry is maintained as a bounded feature area. A maintainer should read the route declarations first, then controller handlers, validators, service orchestration, repository persistence, Prisma records, and tests. This mirrors the runtime request path and keeps HTTP concerns separate from business rules.

Client ownershipclient/src/features/public
Server ownershipserver/src/modules/public
API base path/api/public
Primary data recordsPublicPageVersion and public-facing procurement content
Route surfacePublic pages, guest-facing content, and marketplace entry points

Maintenance Method

When investigating a user issue, start with the visible route or screen, identify the API helper called by that screen, verify the server endpoint mounted under the base path above, and then inspect the Prisma records named in the data row. For cross-module issues, follow stable identifiers such as account id, organization id, tender id, bid id, recommendation id, contract id, document id, or audit event id.

15.3.1 Screen Handover: Welcome

User-facing route / in the implemented React client.

The welcome screen establishes brand identity and routes guests toward marketplace discovery, registration, and sign-in.

Route/
Feature areaPublic entry
Registry relationshipThe screen is exposed through the ProcureX page registry and then selected by the router. Public screens render directly. Workspace screens are wrapped with protected, verified, or admin guards depending on the workflow.
Maintenance checkWhen changing this screen, confirm the route still renders, the screenshot capture route remains mapped, and the related API helper continues to return data in the shape expected by the component.

User-facing route /about in the implemented React client.

The about page explains the system value proposition without depending on authenticated workspace state.

Route/about

Registry relationshipThe screen is exposed through the ProcureX page registry and then selected by the router. Public screens render directly. Workspace screens are wrapped with protected, verified, or admin guards depending on the workflow.

15.4.1 Database Handover: Identity And Account Records

Prisma schema group in server/prisma/schema.prisma.

These records describe people, account credentials, sessions, verification evidence, signing credentials, recovery actions, policy acceptance, and screening outcomes.

Schema areaIdentity And Account Records
Principal modelsUser, UserPreference, Account, UserPolicyAcceptance, Session, MfaFactor, IdentityChallenge, VerificationProfile, VerificationProfileHistory, DigitalSignature, SigningCredential, SignedAction, KeyphraseRecovery, VerificationDocument, ScreeningCheck
Maintenance interpretationUse these models as the persistence source of truth for the named workflow. Services should validate workflow intent before repositories write records, and UI components should treat API responses as projections of these records rather than direct database access.

When extending this area, keep identifiers stable across related records. ProcureX workflows usually depend on account id, organization id, tender id, bid id, evaluation workspace id, recommendation id, contract id, document id, or audit event id.

15.4.2 Database Handover: Organization And Capability Records

Prisma schema group in server/prisma/schema.prisma.

These records connect users to companies and express buyer or supplier ability as organization capabilities rather than login roles.

Schema areaOrganization And Capability Records
Principal modelsOrganization, OrganizationMember, OrganizationCapability, OrganizationProfile, BuyerProfile, SupplierProfile, TrustTierHistory, PermissionOverride
Maintenance interpretationUse these models as the persistence source of truth for the named workflow. Services should validate workflow intent before repositories write records, and UI components should treat API responses as projections of these records rather than direct database access.

When extending this area, keep identifiers stable across related records. ProcureX workflows usually depend on account id, organization id, tender id, bid id, evaluation workspace id, recommendation id, contract id, document id, or audit event id.

15.4.3 Database Handover: Tender And Planning Records

Prisma schema group in server/prisma/schema.prisma.

These records support procurement planning, tender creation, publication, marketplace discovery, amendments, documents, requirements, milestones, commercial lines, and saved opportunities.

Schema areaTender And Planning Records
Principal modelsTender, TenderAmendment, SavedTender, ProcurementPlan, ProcurementPlanLine, TenderCategory, TenderDocument, TenderRequirement, TenderMilestone, TenderCommercialItem
Maintenance interpretationUse these models as the persistence source of truth for the named workflow. Services should validate workflow intent before repositories write records, and UI components should treat API responses as projections of these records rather than direct database access.

When extending this area, keep identifiers stable across related records. ProcureX workflows usually depend on account id, organization id, tender id, bid id, evaluation workspace id, recommendation id, contract id, document id, or audit event id.

15.4.4 Database Handover: Bid Response Records

Prisma schema group in server/prisma/schema.prisma.

These records represent supplier workspaces, response versions, document evidence, response answers, submission receipts, and sample procurement evidence.

Schema areaBid Response Records
Principal modelsBid, BidVersion, BidDocument, BidResponse, BidReceipt, BidSample
Maintenance interpretationUse these models as the persistence source of truth for the named workflow. Services should validate workflow intent before repositories write records, and UI components should treat API responses as projections of these records rather than direct database access.

When extending this area, keep identifiers stable across related records. ProcureX workflows usually depend on account id, organization id, tender id, bid id, evaluation workspace id, recommendation id, contract id, document id, or audit event id.

Prisma schema group in server/prisma/schema.prisma.

These records hold evaluator workspace state, assignments, criteria, scoring evidence, and review progression.

Schema areaEvaluation Records
Principal modelsEvaluationWorkspace, WorkflowAssignment, EvaluationCriterion, EvaluationScore
Maintenance interpretationUse these models as the persistence source of truth for the named workflow. Services should validate workflow intent before repositories write records, and UI components should treat API responses as projections of these records rather than direct database access.

When extending this area, keep identifiers stable across related records. ProcureX workflows usually depend on account id, organization id, tender id, bid id, evaluation workspace id, recommendation id, contract id, document id, or audit event id.

Prisma schema group in server/prisma/schema.prisma.

These records transform evaluation output into award decisions, approval routes, notices, response handling, feasibility checks, tie breakers, and award packs.

Schema areaAward Decision Records
Principal modelsAwardRecommendation, AwardGroup, AwardWinner, AwardClause, AwardNegotiation, AwardBidPack, ApprovalStep, AwardApprovalRoute, AwardApprovalStep, AwardTieBreaker, DeliveryFeasibilityCheck, AwardNotice, AwardResponse, StandstillPeriod, AwardNotification
Maintenance interpretationUse these models as the persistence source of truth for the named workflow. Services should validate workflow intent before repositories write records, and UI components should treat API responses as projections of these records rather than direct database access.

When extending this area, keep identifiers stable across related records. ProcureX workflows usually depend on account id, organization id, tender id, bid id, evaluation workspace id, recommendation id, contract id, document id, or audit event id.

15.4.7 Database Handover: Contract Formation Records

Prisma schema group in server/prisma/schema.prisma.

These records describe contract structure, participating parties, negotiated clauses, versions, signatures, milestone evidence, deliverables, and acceptance.

Schema areaContract Formation Records
Principal modelsContract, ContractParty, ContractClause, ContractNegotiation, ContractVersion, ContractSignature, ContractMilestone, ContractMilestoneEvidence, ContractDeliverable, ContractAcceptance
Maintenance interpretationUse these models as the persistence source of truth for the named workflow. Services should validate workflow intent before repositories write records, and UI components should treat API responses as projections of these records rather than direct database access.

When extending this area, keep identifiers stable across related records. ProcureX workflows usually depend on account id, organization id, tender id, bid id, evaluation workspace id, recommendation id, contract id, document id, or audit event id.

Schema areaPost-Award Management Records
Principal modelsContractManagementPlan, ContractMobilizationItem, ContractKpi, ContractInspection, ContractRisk, ContractVariation, ContractIssue, ContractDispute, ContractTermination, TerminationNotice, TerminationEvidence, TerminationValuation, TerminationSettlement, ReplacementProcurementPlan, ContractCloseout, SupplierPerformanceRecord

When extending this area, keep identifiers stable across related records. ProcureX workflows usually depend on account id, organization id, tender id, bid id, evaluation workspace id, recommendation id, contract id, document id, or audit event id.

When extending this area, keep identifiers stable across related records. ProcureX workflows usually depend on account id, organization id, tender id, bid id, evaluation workspace id, recommendation id, contract id, document id, or audit event id.

Prisma schema group in server/prisma/schema.prisma.

Enums make workflow states explicit across Prisma records, validators, services, and UI labels.

Schema areaCore Enumerations
Principal modelsAccountType, VerificationStatus, SessionStatus, OrganizationKind, OrganizationCapabilityName, MemberStatus, TrustTier, RiskLevel, TenderType, TenderStatus, ProcurementMethod, Visibility, BidStatus, EvaluationStatus, EvaluationStage, ApprovalStatus, RecommendationStatus, ContractStatus, SignatureStatus, InvoiceStatus, AuditSeverity, ComplianceCaseStatus, CommunicationStatus
