15.5.1 Data Flow Handover: Session Restoration

End-to-end workflow reading guide.

Browser reads stored bearer token; Axios attaches Authorization header; client auth state requests /api/identity/session; identity service validates token hash and session status; route guards decide whether to render, redirect to sign-in, redirect to verification, or redirect admins to admin routes.

Technical Reading Path

  1. Start with the route or page that initiates the workflow.
  2. Read the feature API helper to see the HTTP method and payload shape.
  3. Inspect the backend route and controller to understand request handling.
  4. Read validators and service methods for business rules.
  5. Read repository calls and Prisma models for persistence behavior.
  6. Check tests and seed data for preserved examples of the behavior.

This path prevents confusion between display state, transport shape, business policy, and stored records. ProcureX is deliberately layered, so each concern has a different owner.

15.5.2 Data Flow Handover: Registration And Activation

End-to-end workflow reading guide.

Registration starts through identity routes; validation normalizes applicant input; notification providers deliver or log OTP and activation codes according to environment; verified users set a password; session and profile flows then project account state to the client.

Technical Reading Path

  1. Start with the route or page that initiates the workflow.
  2. Read the feature API helper to see the HTTP method and payload shape.
  3. Inspect the backend route and controller to understand request handling.
  4. Read validators and service methods for business rules.
  5. Read repository calls and Prisma models for persistence behavior.
  6. Check tests and seed data for preserved examples of the behavior.

This path prevents confusion between display state, transport shape, business policy, and stored records. ProcureX is deliberately layered, so each concern has a different owner.

15.5.3 Data Flow Handover: Organization Verification

End-to-end workflow reading guide.

The protected verification screen saves drafts, performs registry lookup, submits verification evidence, records screening outcome, and exposes administrator review and decision routes through identity admin endpoints.

Technical Reading Path

  1. Start with the route or page that initiates the workflow.
  2. Read the feature API helper to see the HTTP method and payload shape.
  3. Inspect the backend route and controller to understand request handling.
  4. Read validators and service methods for business rules.
  5. Read repository calls and Prisma models for persistence behavior.
  6. Check tests and seed data for preserved examples of the behavior.

This path prevents confusion between display state, transport shape, business policy, and stored records. ProcureX is deliberately layered, so each concern has a different owner.

15.5.4 Data Flow Handover: Tender Creation

End-to-end workflow reading guide.

Buyer opens create tender; procurement API submits structured tender data; validators enforce shape; service checks account and organization context; repository writes tender core data plus categories, requirements, milestones, documents, and commercial items.

Technical Reading Path

  1. Start with the route or page that initiates the workflow.
  2. Read the feature API helper to see the HTTP method and payload shape.
  3. Inspect the backend route and controller to understand request handling.
  4. Read validators and service methods for business rules.
  5. Read repository calls and Prisma models for persistence behavior.
  6. Check tests and seed data for preserved examples of the behavior.

This path prevents confusion between display state, transport shape, business policy, and stored records. ProcureX is deliberately layered, so each concern has a different owner.

15.5.5 Data Flow Handover: Tender Publication

End-to-end workflow reading guide.

A prepared tender moves through publication endpoint; tender status changes; marketplace data begins exposing the opportunity to supplier discovery screens; supplier detail and bidding routes can then reason about published tender identifiers.

Technical Reading Path

  1. Start with the route or page that initiates the workflow.
  2. Read the feature API helper to see the HTTP method and payload shape.
  3. Inspect the backend route and controller to understand request handling.
  4. Read validators and service methods for business rules.
  5. Read repository calls and Prisma models for persistence behavior.
  6. Check tests and seed data for preserved examples of the behavior.

This path prevents confusion between display state, transport shape, business policy, and stored records. ProcureX is deliberately layered, so each concern has a different owner.

15.5.6 Data Flow Handover: Bid Drafting

End-to-end workflow reading guide.

Supplier opens tender schema or draft; bidding service links tender and supplier organization; draft responses, documents, samples, and metadata are saved against bid records until submit or withdraw changes the lifecycle state.

Technical Reading Path

  1. Start with the route or page that initiates the workflow.
  2. Read the feature API helper to see the HTTP method and payload shape.
  3. Inspect the backend route and controller to understand request handling.
  4. Read validators and service methods for business rules.
  5. Read repository calls and Prisma models for persistence behavior.
  6. Check tests and seed data for preserved examples of the behavior.

This path prevents confusion between display state, transport shape, business policy, and stored records. ProcureX is deliberately layered, so each concern has a different owner.

15.5.7 Data Flow Handover: Bid Submission

End-to-end workflow reading guide.

Submit validates draft readiness; repository performs transaction-safe state changes; bid version and receipt records preserve the submitted envelope, hashes, timestamps, and supplier response evidence.

Technical Reading Path

  1. Start with the route or page that initiates the workflow.
  2. Read the feature API helper to see the HTTP method and payload shape.
  3. Inspect the backend route and controller to understand request handling.
  4. Read validators and service methods for business rules.
  5. Read repository calls and Prisma models for persistence behavior.
  6. Check tests and seed data for preserved examples of the behavior.

This path prevents confusion between display state, transport shape, business policy, and stored records. ProcureX is deliberately layered, so each concern has a different owner.

15.5.8 Data Flow Handover: Evaluation Workspace Save

End-to-end workflow reading guide.

Evaluator opens tender workspace; evaluation module returns submitted bid records, criteria, assignments, and score state; save workspace updates criteria and scores; recommendation handoff data can then feed award-contract workflows.

Technical Reading Path

  1. Start with the route or page that initiates the workflow.
  2. Read the feature API helper to see the HTTP method and payload shape.
  3. Inspect the backend route and controller to understand request handling.
  4. Read validators and service methods for business rules.
  5. Read repository calls and Prisma models for persistence behavior.
  6. Check tests and seed data for preserved examples of the behavior.

This path prevents confusion between display state, transport shape, business policy, and stored records. ProcureX is deliberately layered, so each concern has a different owner.

End-to-end workflow reading guide.

Award-contract module lists recommendations; decision drafts, approval steps, clauses, standstill, notices, budget commitments, tie breakers, and negotiation records are updated through recommendation routes.

Technical Reading Path

  1. Start with the route or page that initiates the workflow.
  2. Read the feature API helper to see the HTTP method and payload shape.

Contract endpoints create versions, clauses, negotiations, signatures, milestones, evidence, status updates, management plan records, commencement records, deliverables, acceptances, inspections, invoices, payments, risks, variations, disputes, terminations, warranties, and closeout records.

Communication center lists messages; compose creates CommunicationItem records; replies, read state, archive state, attachments, recipients, and tender links maintain a structured collaboration trail.

  2. Read the feature API helper to see the HTTP method and payload shape.

15.5.12 Data Flow Handover: Admin Review

End-to-end workflow reading guide.

AdminRoute verifies account type and admin permission; admin screens call compliance-admin and identity admin routes; actions update users, cases, rules, reviews, risk profiles, audit views, datastore entries, tender review decisions, and action history.

Technical Reading Path

  1. Start with the route or page that initiates the workflow.
  2. Read the feature API helper to see the HTTP method and payload shape.
  3. Inspect the backend route and controller to understand request handling.
  4. Read validators and service methods for business rules.
  5. Read repository calls and Prisma models for persistence behavior.
  6. Check tests and seed data for preserved examples of the behavior.

This path prevents confusion between display state, transport shape, business policy, and stored records. ProcureX is deliberately layered, so each concern has a different owner.

15.5.13 Data Flow Handover: Records Export

End-to-end workflow reading guide.

Records module aggregates operational history; dashboard, charts, insights, and detail routes support inspection; CSV and PDF export routes package history for review outside the live workspace.

Technical Reading Path

  1. Start with the route or page that initiates the workflow.
  2. Read the feature API helper to see the HTTP method and payload shape.
  3. Inspect the backend route and controller to understand request handling.
  4. Read validators and service methods for business rules.
  5. Read repository calls and Prisma models for persistence behavior.
  6. Check tests and seed data for preserved examples of the behavior.

This path prevents confusion between display state, transport shape, business policy, and stored records. ProcureX is deliberately layered, so each concern has a different owner.

End-to-end workflow reading guide.

Public contact can create initial support evidence; authenticated ticket routes list, create, detail, comment, and update status; support pages route users toward help centre content or ticket workflows.

This path prevents confusion between display state, transport shape, business policy, and stored records. ProcureX is deliberately layered, so each concern has a different owner.

End-to-end workflow reading guide.

Help centre routes return category and FAQ data; suggestion and popular routes support discovery; message route applies rate limiting before processing support-style help requests.

  4. Read validators and service methods for business rules.
  5. Read repository calls and Prisma models for persistence behavior.

This path prevents confusion between display state, transport shape, business policy, and stored records. ProcureX is deliberately layered, so each concern has a different owner.

15.6.1 Testing Handover: Server Application Tests

Verification surface for implemented behavior.

Health endpoint, module registration, middleware behavior, and error handling.

Command familynpm test, npm run test:client, workspace-specific Vitest commands, and npm --workspace server run verify:rls for database security verification.
Why this mattersTests are executable documentation. They show which behavior the implementation considers stable and which inputs, states, and user actions have been protected against regression.
Maintainer actionWhen changing this area, update or add focused tests beside the affected feature. Keep assertions tied to user-visible behavior, route behavior, service rules, repository persistence, or security guarantees.

A handover reader should treat test files as the quickest way to learn what a module promises. Implementation files explain how behavior works, while tests explain which behavior must remain true after maintenance.

Registration, production auth restrictions, delivery providers, signing, sessions, and challenge behavior.

15.7.1 Operations Handover: Workspace Setup

Development and maintenance workflow.

Run commands from procurement-platform. Install dependencies with npm install. Use npm workspaces for shared, server, and client packages. Root scripts delegate to workspace scripts so common commands remain discoverable.

Primary locationprocurement-platform
Common commandsnpm install, npm run infra:up, npm run db:validate, npm run db:migrate, npm run db:seed, npm run dev:server:example, npm run dev:client:example, npm test, npm run build
Operational readingPrefer root README, workspace README files, Docker README, scripts README, and architecture documentation before changing commands.

This routine keeps setup knowledge close to the repository. A maintainer should be able to rebuild local services, validate schema, seed data, run the API, run the client, and execute checks without relying on hidden machine state.

15.7.2 Operations Handover: Local Infrastructure

Development and maintenance workflow.

Docker Compose starts PostgreSQL, Redis, Elasticsearch, and MinIO. Server example environment points Prisma, Redis, search, and object storage settings at those local services.

Primary locationprocurement-platform
Common commandsnpm install, npm run infra:up, npm run db:validate, npm run db:migrate, npm run db:seed, npm run dev:server:example, npm run dev:client:example, npm test, npm run build
Operational readingPrefer root README, workspace README files, Docker README, scripts README, and architecture documentation before changing commands.

This routine keeps setup knowledge close to the repository. A maintainer should be able to rebuild local services, validate schema, seed data, run the API, run the client, and execute checks without relying on hidden machine state.

15.7.3 Operations Handover: Database Lifecycle

Development and maintenance workflow.

Use db:validate to check Prisma schema, db:migrate for local development migrations, db:deploy for deployment-style migration execution, and db:seed or seed variants for realistic local datasets.

Primary locationprocurement-platform
Common commandsnpm install, npm run infra:up, npm run db:validate, npm run db:migrate, npm run db:seed, npm run dev:server:example, npm run dev:client:example, npm test, npm run build
Operational readingPrefer root README, workspace README files, Docker README, scripts README, and architecture documentation before changing commands.

This routine keeps setup knowledge close to the repository. A maintainer should be able to rebuild local services, validate schema, seed data, run the API, run the client, and execute checks without relying on hidden machine state.

15.7.4 Operations Handover: Server Runtime

Development and maintenance workflow.

The server starts through tsx in watch mode for development. createApp configures Helmet, CORS, JSON parsing, health, registered modules, 404 handling, and centralized error responses.

Primary locationprocurement-platform
Common commandsnpm install, npm run infra:up, npm run db:validate, npm run db:migrate, npm run db:seed, npm run dev:server:example, npm run dev:client:example, npm test, npm run build
Operational readingPrefer root README, workspace README files, Docker README, scripts README, and architecture documentation before changing commands.

This routine keeps setup knowledge close to the repository. A maintainer should be able to rebuild local services, validate schema, seed data, run the API, run the client, and execute checks without relying on hidden machine state.

15.7.5 Operations Handover: Client Runtime

Development and maintenance workflow.

The client starts through Vite and uses VITE_API_BASE_URL to reach the server. React Router selects public, protected, verified, and admin surfaces.

Primary locationprocurement-platform
Common commandsnpm install, npm run infra:up, npm run db:validate, npm run db:migrate, npm run db:seed, npm run dev:server:example, npm run dev:client:example, npm test, npm run build
Operational readingPrefer root README, workspace README files, Docker README, scripts README, and architecture documentation before changing commands.

This routine keeps setup knowledge close to the repository. A maintainer should be able to rebuild local services, validate schema, seed data, run the API, run the client, and execute checks without relying on hidden machine state.

15.7.6 Operations Handover: UI Parity Screenshots

Development and maintenance workflow.

The screenshot workflow reads docs/ui-parity/routes.json, opens prototype and React routes, injects session context for protected pages, and writes desktop and mobile images under docs/ui-parity/screenshots.

Primary locationprocurement-platform
Common commandsnpm install, npm run infra:up, npm run db:validate, npm run db:migrate, npm run db:seed, npm run dev:server:example, npm run dev:client:example, npm test, npm run build
Operational readingPrefer root README, workspace README files, Docker README, scripts README, and architecture documentation before changing commands.

This routine keeps setup knowledge close to the repository. A maintainer should be able to rebuild local services, validate schema, seed data, run the API, run the client, and execute checks without relying on hidden machine state.

15.7.7 Operations Handover: Build Order

Development and maintenance workflow.

The root build runs shared first, then server, then client. This ensures shared account and capability contracts compile before both consumers.

Primary locationprocurement-platform
Common commandsnpm install, npm run infra:up, npm run db:validate, npm run db:migrate, npm run db:seed, npm run dev:server:example, npm run dev:client:example, npm test, npm run build
Operational readingPrefer root README, workspace README files, Docker README, scripts README, and architecture documentation before changing commands.

This routine keeps setup knowledge close to the repository. A maintainer should be able to rebuild local services, validate schema, seed data, run the API, run the client, and execute checks without relying on hidden machine state.

15.7.8 Operations Handover: Maintenance Handover Routine

Development and maintenance workflow.

For every change, identify the feature owner, update code at the correct layer, run the narrow test first, run broader checks when behavior crosses module boundaries, and refresh documentation only when behavior or setup changes.

Primary locationprocurement-platform
Common commandsnpm install, npm run infra:up, npm run db:validate, npm run db:migrate, npm run db:seed, npm run dev:server:example, npm run dev:client:example, npm test, npm run build
Operational readingPrefer root README, workspace README files, Docker README, scripts README, and architecture documentation before changing commands.

This routine keeps setup knowledge close to the repository. A maintainer should be able to rebuild local services, validate schema, seed data, run the API, run the client, and execute checks without relying on hidden machine state.

15.8 Engineering Handover Closing Notes

Summary of the expanded handover chapter.

18Registered backend modules documented.
35Production target screenshots mapped to routes.
15+Prisma schema groups explained for handover.

The expanded report now functions as both a narrative implementation document and a practical engineering handover manual. It explains where each major feature lives, how user journeys move through client routes and backend modules, what database records support the workflow, which tests preserve behavior, and how local development is operated.

The most important handover principle is that ProcureX is organized around stable boundaries. The client owns rendering and route-level experience, the API owns validation and orchestration, repositories own persistence, Prisma owns model truth, shared contracts own cross-workspace vocabulary, and Docker/workspace scripts own reproducible local setup.

Future maintainers should preserve that separation. When new behavior is added, it should land in the feature boundary that owns it, pass through validators and services before persistence, use explicit organization and account context, and leave tests or documented verification steps behind.

16. Detailed Module Implementation Atlas

This chapter expands the implementation report into a module-by-module technical atlas. Each module is described through its ownership boundary, route surface, controller-service-repository flow, data evidence, frontend integration, validation posture, tests, and maintenance responsibilities.

The atlas is written for technical, engineering, and assessment readers. It intentionally keeps public API, runtime code, Prisma schema, and package scripts unchanged; it only documents how the current implementation is organized.

No.ModuleBase pathRoutesImplementation purpose
1Public/api/public3Published public page content, legal versions, and public policy metadata.
2Identity/api/identity35Accounts, sessions, verification, admin account type, and user access context.
3Organization/api/organization1Company membership, buyer/supplier capabilities, buyer profiles, and supplier profiles.
4Procurement/api/procurement44Tender creation, publication, marketplace visibility, requirements, milestones, and commercial items.
5Bidding/api/bidding14Supplier bid drafts, sealed versions, responses, bid documents, receipts, and submission state.
6Evaluation/api/evaluation7Evaluation workspaces, criteria, workflow assignments, scores, recommendations, and approvals.
7Award Contract/api/award-contract91Award handoff, contract negotiation, contract versions, signatures, and post-award contract state.
8Post Award/api/post-award148Standalone contract execution workspace for delivery, acceptance, finance, issues, variations, close-out, and history.
9Financial/api/financial1Purchase orders, invoices, matching checks, payment review, and financial records.
10Compliance Admin/api/compliance-admin46Platform admin search, compliance review, audit events, risk signals, and admin actions.
11Communication/api/communication12Messages, clarifications, notifications, alerts, attachments, and tender communication history.
12Dashboard/api/dashboard3Workspace command-center metrics, action queues, deadlines, and active procurement work.
13Records/api/records8Procurement history, audit-ready record entries, and cross-module evidence indexes.
14Intelligence/api/intelligence4Market snapshots, price benchmarks, supplier match signals, and module registry data.
15Integration/api/integration1External systems, sync runs, webhook-style events, and platform integration status.
16Documents/api/documents12Object storage metadata, checksums, encryption references, and document attachments.
17Support/api/support8Help Center support tickets, comments, status updates, and account-menu activity support.
18Help Centre/api/help-centre7Deterministic ProcureX FAQ assistant and Help Centre knowledge base.

16.1.1 Public Module Purpose And Ownership

Registered backend module: public.

Published public page content, legal versions, and public policy metadata.

This boundary is implemented as a TypeScript Express module under server/src/modules/public. It owns the HTTP shape, validation, orchestration, persistence access, and status metadata for its product area.

3API routes in routes.ts.
7Implementation files found.
6Related test files mapped.
Base API path/api/public
Implementation locationserver/src/modules/public/
Primary filesindex.ts, types.ts, routes.ts, controller.ts, service.ts, repository.ts, validators.ts
Shared contract exportsmoduleDefinition, ModuleStatus, PublicPageRouteKey, PublicPageVersionDto, CurrentLegalVersionsDto, routeKeyToPageKey, pageKeyToRouteKey
Assessment readingStart with types.ts for module identity, then read routes.ts, validators.ts, service.ts, and repository.ts in that order.

This ownership model keeps the assessment boundary clear: routes expose the public HTTP surface, validators define accepted input, services describe business behavior, repositories isolate persistence, and tests document expected behavior.

16.1.2 Public API Route Implementation

HTTP boundary, controller handoff, and route families.

The module router is created in server/src/modules/public/routes.ts. Each route delegates to a controller handler, so HTTP parsing stays separate from business orchestration.

MethodRouteController handler
GET/api/public/status
GET/api/public/pages/:pageKeygetPage
GET/api/public/legal/currentcurrentLegalVersions
Route familiesstatus: 1, pages: 1, legal: 1
Controller handlersRequestHandler, RequestHandler, RequestHandler
Implementation ruleWhen adding endpoints, keep the route small, validate in the controller boundary, and place workflow decisions inside the module service.

For assessment review, the route table demonstrates that the module is not a loose collection of functions. It is a declared HTTP contract with explicit verbs, paths, and controller entry points.

16.1.3 Public Controller, Service, Repository, And Data Flow

How requests move through the module implementation.

A request enters the module through Express routing, is normalized by the controller, passes through Zod validation where schemas exist, then reaches service methods that coordinate state transitions and repository calls.

Controller layercontroller.ts receives route parameters, query values, request body data, uploads, and response status decisions.
Validation layermoduleStatusQuerySchema, publicPageParamsSchema
Service layerstatus, getPage, currentLegalVersions, getPageByKey
Repository layerhealth, latestPublishedPage
Related migrations202606060003_public_content_pages

The practical maintenance rule is to keep each concern in its layer. A controller should not become a database adapter, and a repository should not decide workflow policy. This separation makes the implementation readable for both engineering handover and formal assessment.

16.1.4 Public Frontend And Workflow Integration

How the module appears in the React workspace and user journey.

The backend boundary is paired with client feature areas where available. The client owns rendering, route guards, page state, upload controls, and user interaction, while the server owns validation and durable workflow state.

Client feature directoriesclient/src/features/public/
UI parity screenswelcome, guest-marketplace, about-procurex, contact
Route registry sourceclient/src/app/router.tsx and client/src/features/procurexPageRegistry.tsx connect page keys to route protection and lazy-loaded React pages.
ScreenReact pathPrototype page
welcome/welcome
about-procurex/aboutabout-procurex
contact/contactcontact
guest-marketplace/guest-marketplaceguest-marketplace

This pairing is important because the implementation is not only backend code. The real product behavior emerges when guarded routes, page components, API helpers, services, repositories, and tests all describe the same user workflow.

16.1.5 Public Validation, Security, Testing, And Maintenance

Assessment controls for stable module behavior.

The module is maintained by keeping schema checks, service decisions, repository persistence, audit behavior, and UI expectations aligned. Tests are treated as executable documentation for the implemented behavior.

Input validationmoduleStatusQuerySchema, publicPageParamsSchema
Security postureUse authenticated request context, organization context, role or capability checks, signed-action requirements, and centralized API errors where the workflow is sensitive.
Related testsclient/src/features/public/api/index.test.ts
client/src/features/public/components/procurex/ContactProcurexPage.test.tsx
client/src/features/public/components/procurex/PublicContentProcurexPage.test.tsx
client/src/features/public/components/procurex/WelcomeProcurexPage.test.tsx
server/dist/src/modules/public/service.test.js
server/src/modules/public/service.test.ts
Maintenance checklistPreserve route contracts, keep validation schemas close to route input, add service tests for workflow decisions, and update this report when behavior or setup changes.

For engineering handover, this page explains what must stay true after change. For assessment, it shows how the module implementation can be traced from a user-facing action to validation, persistence, and regression coverage.

Accounts, sessions, verification, admin account type, and user access context.

A request enters the module through Express routing, is normalized by the controller, passes through Zod validation where schemas exist, then reaches service methods that coordinate state transitions and repository calls.

The practical maintenance rule is to keep each concern in its layer. A controller should not become a database adapter, and a repository should not decide workflow policy. This separation makes the implementation readable for both engineering handover and formal assessment.

16.3.1 Organization Module Purpose And Ownership

Registered backend module: organization.

Company membership, buyer/supplier capabilities, buyer profiles, and supplier profiles.

Module Skeleton Each ProcureX backend module follows the same starting shape:

1API routes in routes.ts.
8Implementation files found.
10Related test files mapped.
Base API path/api/organization
Implementation locationserver/src/modules/organization/
Primary filesindex.ts, types.ts, routes.ts, controller.ts, service.ts, repository.ts, validators.ts, README.md
Shared contract exportsmoduleDefinition, ModuleStatus
Assessment readingStart with types.ts for module identity, then read routes.ts, validators.ts, service.ts, and repository.ts in that order.

This ownership model keeps the assessment boundary clear: routes expose the public HTTP surface, validators define accepted input, services describe business behavior, repositories isolate persistence, and tests document expected behavior.

16.3.2 Organization API Route Implementation

HTTP boundary, controller handoff, and route families.

The module router is created in server/src/modules/organization/routes.ts. Each route delegates to a controller handler, so HTTP parsing stays separate from business orchestration.

MethodRouteController handler
GET/api/organization/status
Route familiesstatus: 1
Controller handlersRequestHandler
Implementation ruleWhen adding endpoints, keep the route small, validate in the controller boundary, and place workflow decisions inside the module service.

For assessment review, the route table demonstrates that the module is not a loose collection of functions. It is a declared HTTP contract with explicit verbs, paths, and controller entry points.

16.3.3 Organization Controller, Service, Repository, And Data Flow

How requests move through the module implementation.

A request enters the module through Express routing, is normalized by the controller, passes through Zod validation where schemas exist, then reaches service methods that coordinate state transitions and repository calls.

Controller layercontroller.ts receives route parameters, query values, request body data, uploads, and response status decisions.
Validation layermoduleStatusQuerySchema
Service layerstatus
Repository layerhealth
Related migrations202606110001_p1_iam_access_trust, 202607020001_dual_user_capabilities

The practical maintenance rule is to keep each concern in its layer. A controller should not become a database adapter, and a repository should not decide workflow policy. This separation makes the implementation readable for both engineering handover and formal assessment.

16.3.4 Organization Frontend And Workflow Integration

How the module appears in the React workspace and user journey.

The backend boundary is paired with client feature areas where available. The client owns rendering, route guards, page state, upload controls, and user interaction, while the server owns validation and durable workflow state.

Client feature directoriesclient/src/features/identity/, client/src/features/workspace/
UI parity screensverification-status, app-launcher
Route registry sourceclient/src/app/router.tsx and client/src/features/procurexPageRegistry.tsx connect page keys to route protection and lazy-loaded React pages.
ScreenReact pathPrototype page
verification-status/identity/profileaccount-profile
app-launcher/appsapp-launcher

This pairing is important because the implementation is not only backend code. The real product behavior emerges when guarded routes, page components, API helpers, services, repositories, and tests all describe the same user workflow.

Registered backend module: evaluation.

Evaluation workspaces, criteria, workflow assignments, scores, recommendations, and approvals.

Module Skeleton Each ProcureX backend module follows the same starting shape:

7API routes in routes.ts.
8Implementation files found.
6Related test files mapped.
Base API path/api/evaluation
Implementation locationserver/src/modules/evaluation/
Primary filesindex.ts, types.ts, routes.ts, controller.ts, service.ts, repository.ts, validators.ts, README.md
Shared contract exportsmoduleDefinition, ModuleStatus, EvaluationDashboardDto, EvaluationRecordDto, EvaluationRecordsResponseDto, EvaluationDraftDto, EvaluationDraftsResponseDto, ReadyEvaluationTenderDto, ReadyEvaluationResponseDto, EvaluationRecordsQuery, EvaluationDecisionStatus, EvaluationRequestContext, EvaluationWorkspaceTenderDto, EvaluationConfigurationItemDto, EvaluationConfigurationSectionDto, EvaluationConfigurationDto, EvaluationWorkspaceSummaryDto, EvaluationWorkspaceCriterionDto
Assessment readingStart with types.ts for module identity, then read routes.ts, validators.ts, service.ts, and repository.ts in that order.

This ownership model keeps the assessment boundary clear: routes expose the public HTTP surface, validators define accepted input, services describe business behavior, repositories isolate persistence, and tests document expected behavior.

16.6.2 Evaluation API Route Implementation

HTTP boundary, controller handoff, and route families.

The module router is created in server/src/modules/evaluation/routes.ts. Each route delegates to a controller handler, so HTTP parsing stays separate from business orchestration.

MethodRouteController handler
GET/api/evaluation/status
GET/api/evaluation/dashboarddashboard
GET/api/evaluation/recordsrecords
GET/api/evaluation/draftsdrafts
GET/api/evaluation/readyready
GET/api/evaluation/tenders/:tenderId/workspaceworkspace
PUT/api/evaluation/tenders/:tenderId/workspacesaveWorkspace
Route familiestenders: 2, status: 1, dashboard: 1, records: 1, drafts: 1, ready: 1
Controller handlersRequestHandler, RequestHandler, RequestHandler, RequestHandler, RequestHandler, RequestHandler, RequestHandler
Implementation ruleWhen adding endpoints, keep the route small, validate in the controller boundary, and place workflow decisions inside the module service.

For assessment review, the route table demonstrates that the module is not a loose collection of functions. It is a declared HTTP contract with explicit verbs, paths, and controller entry points.

16.6.3 Evaluation Controller, Service, Repository, And Data Flow

How requests move through the module implementation.

A request enters the module through Express routing, is normalized by the controller, passes through Zod validation where schemas exist, then reaches service methods that coordinate state transitions and repository calls.

Controller layercontroller.ts receives route parameters, query values, request body data, uploads, and response status decisions.
Validation layermoduleStatusQuerySchema, recordsQuerySchema, workspaceParamsSchema, saveWorkspaceBodySchema
Service layerstatus, dashboard, records, drafts, ready, workspace, saveWorkspace
Repository layerhealth, getDashboardData, listRecords, listDrafts, listReadyTenders, getWorkspaceByTenderId, saveWorkspace, closeElapsedTenders, createWorkspaceFromTender
Related migrationsNo migration name maps directly to this module; it relies on shared schema foundations or future expansion.

The practical maintenance rule is to keep each concern in its layer. A controller should not become a database adapter, and a repository should not decide workflow policy. This separation makes the implementation readable for both engineering handover and formal assessment.

16.6.4 Evaluation Frontend And Workflow Integration

How the module appears in the React workspace and user journey.

The backend boundary is paired with client feature areas where available. The client owns rendering, route guards, page state, upload controls, and user interaction, while the server owns validation and durable workflow state.

Client feature directoriesclient/src/features/evaluation/
UI parity screensbid-evaluation
Route registry sourceclient/src/app/router.tsx and client/src/features/procurexPageRegistry.tsx connect page keys to route protection and lazy-loaded React pages.
ScreenReact pathPrototype page
bid-evaluation/evaluationbid-evaluation

This pairing is important because the implementation is not only backend code. The real product behavior emerges when guarded routes, page components, API helpers, services, repositories, and tests all describe the same user workflow.

16.6.5 Evaluation Validation, Security, Testing, And Maintenance

Assessment controls for stable module behavior.

The module is maintained by keeping schema checks, service decisions, repository persistence, audit behavior, and UI expectations aligned. Tests are treated as executable documentation for the implemented behavior.

Input validationmoduleStatusQuerySchema, recordsQuerySchema, workspaceParamsSchema, saveWorkspaceBodySchema
Security postureUse authenticated request context, organization context, role or capability checks, signed-action requirements, and centralized API errors where the workflow is sensitive.

server/dist/src/modules/evaluation/__tests__/service.test.js
server/dist/src/modules/procurement/seed-demo-evaluation-award.test.js

server/src/modules/evaluation/__tests__/service.test.ts
server/src/modules/procurement/seed-demo-evaluation-award.test.ts

MethodRouteController handler

16.13.3 Records Controller, Service, Repository, And Data Flow

How requests move through the module implementation.

A request enters the module through Express routing, is normalized by the controller, passes through Zod validation where schemas exist, then reaches service methods that coordinate state transitions and repository calls.

Controller layercontroller.ts receives route parameters, query values, request body data, uploads, and response status decisions.
Validation layermoduleStatusQuerySchema, recordsQuerySchema, recordsExportQuerySchema, recordParamsSchema
Service layerstatus, dashboard, records, charts, insights, detail, lifecycle, documents, recordDocuments, audit, recordAudit, exportCsv, exportPdf, accessContext
Repository layerhealth, getDashboardData, listRecords, listAllRecords, getCharts, getInsights, getRecordDetail, getLifecycle, listDocuments, getRecordDocuments, listAuditEvents, getRecordAudit, findRecord, collectRecords
Related migrations202605280001_init, 202607140001_official_documents

The practical maintenance rule is to keep each concern in its layer. A controller should not become a database adapter, and a repository should not decide workflow policy. This separation makes the implementation readable for both engineering handover and formal assessment.

16.13.4 Records Frontend And Workflow Integration

How the module appears in the React workspace and user journey.

The backend boundary is paired with client feature areas where available. The client owns rendering, route guards, page state, upload controls, and user interaction, while the server owns validation and durable workflow state.

Client feature directoriesclient/src/features/records/
UI parity screensrecords-history
Route registry sourceclient/src/app/router.tsx and client/src/features/procurexPageRegistry.tsx connect page keys to route protection and lazy-loaded React pages.
ScreenReact pathPrototype page
records-history/recordsrecords-history

This pairing is important because the implementation is not only backend code. The real product behavior emerges when guarded routes, page components, API helpers, services, repositories, and tests all describe the same user workflow.

16.13.5 Records Validation, Security, Testing, And Maintenance

Assessment controls for stable module behavior.

The module is maintained by keeping schema checks, service decisions, repository persistence, audit behavior, and UI expectations aligned. Tests are treated as executable documentation for the implemented behavior.

Input validationmoduleStatusQuerySchema, recordsQuerySchema, recordsExportQuerySchema, recordParamsSchema

server/src/modules/help-centre/service.test.ts
server/src/modules/support/email.test.ts
server/src/modules/support/service.test.ts
Maintenance checklistPreserve route contracts, keep validation schemas close to route input, add service tests for workflow decisions, and update this report when behavior or setup changes.

For engineering handover, this page explains what must stay true after change. For assessment, it shows how the module implementation can be traced from a user-facing action to validation, persistence, and regression coverage.

17. Frontend Screen And Workflow Atlas

This chapter connects the backend implementation to the React application surface. It explains how public routes, protected routes, verified workspaces, admin-only routes, page registry keys, screenshots, and feature directories combine into the user-facing platform.

35UI parity route rows.
41registered ProcureX page keys.
35/35desktop target/reference screenshots.

Frontend route, registry, workflow, and screenshot evidence.

The router defines whether a screen is public, protected, verified, or administrator-only. This keeps access policy outside individual page components and makes route behavior assessable from one file.

18. Data, Security, Testing, And Operations Traceability

This chapter documents the platform-wide implementation controls that sit below individual modules. These controls explain why the module implementation can be assessed as a system rather than isolated screens.

38Prisma migrations found.
174Prisma models detected.
265test files found.

18.1 Migration Timeline

Cross-cutting traceability for data, security, testing, and operations.

Prisma migrations document how the data model has grown from the initial foundation into identity, procurement, bidding, award, contract, post-award, compliance, document, and operations workflows.

Migration sourceserver/prisma/migrations/
Recent migrations202607130003_signed_actions
202607130004_keyphrase_recovery
202607140001_official_documents
202607140002_post_award_execution_foundation
202607150001_post_award_invoice_traceability
202607150002_goods_post_award_links
202607150003_works_post_award_links
202607150004_services_post_award_links
202607150005_consultancy_post_award_links
202607150006_post_award_operations
Implementation useRead migrations beside module repositories to understand which records are durable, which workflow states exist, and which relations support traceability.

This cross-cutting view helps reviewers connect module behavior to the infrastructure and verification assets that make the implementation maintainable.

18.2 Prisma Schema Model Traceability

Cross-cutting traceability for data, security, testing, and operations.

The Prisma schema is the database contract for the server. Repositories should be read against model names and relations, not as free-form persistence code.

Schema sourceserver/prisma/schema.prisma
Model examplesUser, UserPreference, Account, UserPolicyAcceptance, Session, MfaFactor, IdentityChallenge, VerificationProfile, VerificationProfileHistory, DigitalSignature, SigningCredential, SignedAction, KeyphraseRecovery, VerificationDocument, ScreeningCheck, Organization, OrganizationMember, OrganizationCapability, OrganizationProfile, BuyerProfile, SupplierProfile, TrustTierHistory, PermissionOverride, Tender
Assessment useMatch repository calls and migration history to Prisma models when proving that a workflow has persistent state.

This cross-cutting view helps reviewers connect module behavior to the infrastructure and verification assets that make the implementation maintainable.

18.3 Row-Level Security And Database Context

Cross-cutting traceability for data, security, testing, and operations.

Organization-aware access is implemented through request context and database context helpers. Sensitive modules should preserve organization and account scoping through every repository operation.

Application contextserver/src/db/context.ts
RLS verificationserver/src/verify-rls.ts and npm --workspace server run verify:rls
Security principleSet account and organization context before protected data access and prefer centralized policy helpers over ad hoc checks.

This cross-cutting view helps reviewers connect module behavior to the infrastructure and verification assets that make the implementation maintainable.

18.4 Centralized API Errors

Cross-cutting traceability for data, security, testing, and operations.

Client and server error helpers keep failure responses consistent so pages can show meaningful states without duplicating backend error policy.

Server API errorsserver/src/modules/shared/apiErrors.ts
Client API errorsclient/src/shared/api/errors.ts
Implementation ruleValidate early, throw structured request errors, and let the app-level error handler shape the HTTP response.

This cross-cutting view helps reviewers connect module behavior to the infrastructure and verification assets that make the implementation maintainable.

18.5 Route Guards And Access Controls

Cross-cutting traceability for data, security, testing, and operations.

Route guards centralize session, verification, administrator, permission, feature gate, and trust-tier checks before protected screens render.

Guard sourceclient/src/app/routeGuards.tsx
Guard testsclient/src/app/routeGuards.test.tsx
Assessment useA protected workflow should be checked both at the route guard and at the backend service boundary.

This cross-cutting view helps reviewers connect module behavior to the infrastructure and verification assets that make the implementation maintainable.

18.6 Uploads, Object Storage, And Official Documents

Cross-cutting traceability for data, security, testing, and operations.

Document flows cover upload validation, object keys, checksums, allowed file types, official document templates, generated PDFs, approvals, and signatures.

Document storageserver/src/modules/documents/storage.ts and officialStorage.ts
Official generationofficialDocumentBuilder.ts, officialPdfRenderer.ts, and officialTemplates.ts
Testsserver/src/modules/documents/__tests__/officialDocuments.test.ts

This cross-cutting view helps reviewers connect module behavior to the infrastructure and verification assets that make the implementation maintainable.

18.7 Support Email And Notification Delivery

Cross-cutting traceability for data, security, testing, and operations.

Support and identity delivery providers keep user-facing messages separate from workflow logic while tests verify fallback and failure behavior.

Support email sourceserver/src/modules/support/email.ts
Identity notificationsserver/src/modules/identity/notifications.ts
Delivery testsserver/src/modules/support/email.test.ts and server/src/__tests__/identity-delivery-providers.test.ts

This cross-cutting view helps reviewers connect module behavior to the infrastructure and verification assets that make the implementation maintainable.

18.8 HTTP Security Middleware And Anti-Abuse Controls

Cross-cutting traceability for data, security, testing, and operations.

The server bootstrap configures HTTP safety concerns outside individual modules, while rate limiting and Turnstile checks protect sensitive entry points.

Server bootstrapserver/src/app.ts and server/src/server.ts
Security helpersserver/src/security/rateLimit.ts, turnstile.ts, and config.ts
Client widgetclient/src/features/auth/components/procurex/TurnstileWidget.tsx

This cross-cutting view helps reviewers connect module behavior to the infrastructure and verification assets that make the implementation maintainable.

18.9 Admin Search, Audit, And Compliance Traceability

Cross-cutting traceability for data, security, testing, and operations.

Administrative modules combine searchable operational records with compliance cases, audit events, risk signals, and admin actions.

Search sourceserver/src/modules/compliance-admin/searchIndex.ts
Compliance routesserver/src/modules/compliance-admin/routes.ts
Screensadmin-dashboard, admin-users, admin-analytics, and admin-audit

This cross-cutting view helps reviewers connect module behavior to the infrastructure and verification assets that make the implementation maintainable.

18.10 Automated Test Suites

Cross-cutting traceability for data, security, testing, and operations.

The test suite is spread across server modules, client pages, route guards, shared utilities, and E2E-style flows. Tests serve as executable evidence of implemented behavior.

Server tests59
Client tests37
Root commandnpm test runs server and client workspace tests.

This cross-cutting view helps reviewers connect module behavior to the infrastructure and verification assets that make the implementation maintainable.

18.11 Seed Data And Demo Workflows

Cross-cutting traceability for data, security, testing, and operations.

Seed scripts create realistic local datasets for marketplace, evaluation, award, contract, and post-award verification.

Seed commandsdb:seed, db:seed:evaluation-intake-demo, db:seed:marketplace-demo, db:seed:award-ready-demo, db:seed:hassan-award-demo, db:seed:demo-evaluation-award, db:seed:award-contract-demo, db:seed:post-award-demo, db:seed:twice
Cleanup commandsdb:cleanup:award-contract-demo, db:cleanup:post-award-demo, db:cleanup:demo-admin
Assessment useUse seeded data when demonstrating workflows that require linked tenders, bids, awards, contracts, invoices, or post-award records.

This cross-cutting view helps reviewers connect module behavior to the infrastructure and verification assets that make the implementation maintainable.

18.12 Build And Runtime Commands

Cross-cutting traceability for data, security, testing, and operations.

Workspace scripts keep setup, build, database validation, server runtime, client runtime, and test execution discoverable from package metadata.

Root buildnpm run build builds shared, server, and client workspaces.
Client scriptsdev, dev:example, build, preview, test, audit:localization, test:e2e:awards, test:e2e:evaluation, lint, generate:procurex-pages, sync:procurex-ui, ui:parity:screenshots
Server scriptsdev, dev:example, build, start, test, db:validate, db:generate, db:migrate, db:deploy, db:seed, db:seed:evaluation-intake-demo, db:seed:marketplace-demo

This cross-cutting view helps reviewers connect module behavior to the infrastructure and verification assets that make the implementation maintainable.

18.13 Screenshot Capture And UI Parity

Cross-cutting traceability for data, security, testing, and operations.

The screenshot workflow records reference and production target views so visual implementation can be reviewed alongside code implementation.

Capture scriptscripts/capture-ui-parity.mjs
Route mapdocs/ui-parity/routes.json
Screenshot foldersdocs/ui-parity/screenshots/desktop/ and docs/ui-parity/screenshots/mobile/

This cross-cutting view helps reviewers connect module behavior to the infrastructure and verification assets that make the implementation maintainable.

18.14 Report Acceptance And Page Count Verification

Cross-cutting traceability for data, security, testing, and operations.

The expanded report is verified as an A4 print artifact. The acceptance target is at least 300 pages, with the implementation aiming for a 320 to 340 page margin.

Canonical artifactdocs/implementation-report.html
Page-count methodRender with Playwright using page.pdf({ format: "A4", printBackground: true }), then load with pdf-lib and count pages.
Non-goalsNo runtime app code, package scripts, Prisma schema, migrations, or API contracts are changed by this report expansion.

This cross-cutting view helps reviewers connect module behavior to the infrastructure and verification assets that make the implementation maintainable.

19. Technology And Stack Deep Dive

This chapter lists the technologies, frameworks, libraries, infrastructure services, and development tools used to implement ProcureX. It is based on the repository manifests, TypeScript configs, Vite config, Docker Compose services, environment examples, and implementation files.

The stack is intentionally split by responsibility: React and Vite own the browser app, Express and Prisma own the API and persistence boundary, PostgreSQL/Redis/Elasticsearch/MinIO provide local infrastructure, and TypeScript/Vitest/Playwright/ESLint keep the implementation verifiable.

Direct npm package entries62
Docker services4
Client package groups@emotion/react, @emotion/styled, @mui/icons-material, @mui/material, @procurex/shared, @reduxjs/toolkit, axios, html2pdf.js, i18next, react, react-dom, react-i18next, react-redux, react-router-dom, recharts, socket.io-client, xlsx, @eslint/js, @testing-library/jest-dom, @testing-library/react, @testing-library/user-event, @types/node, @types/react, @types/react-dom, @vitejs/plugin-react, eslint, eslint-plugin-react-hooks, eslint-plugin-react-refresh, globals, jsdom, playwright, typescript, typescript-eslint, vite, vitest
Server package groups@aws-sdk/client-s3, @elastic/elasticsearch, @prisma/client, @procurex/shared, @types/busboy, busboy, cors, dotenv, express, helmet, ioredis, nodemailer, pdf-lib, resend, zod, @types/cors, @types/express, @types/node, @types/nodemailer, @types/supertest, prisma, supertest, tsx, typescript, vitest

19.1 Npm Workspaces And Monorepo Packaging

Root package orchestration for client, server, and shared contracts.

ProcureX is packaged as a private npm monorepo. The root package owns workspace discovery and delegates development, build, test, database, infrastructure, and generation commands to the appropriate child workspace.

Configured inpackage.json with workspaces client, server, and shared.
Used forKeeping frontend, backend, and shared contract code versioned together while still allowing workspace-specific scripts.
Why it fitsProcurement workflows cross UI, API, shared permissions, database models, and tests; a monorepo keeps those contracts visible in one implementation tree.
Maintainer checkWhen adding a package, verify whether it belongs in the root, client, server, or shared workspace and avoid duplicating cross-workspace dependencies unnecessarily.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.2 Node.js 20+ And ES Modules

Runtime and module format for server tooling and scripts.

The repository declares ES module behavior and a Node.js 20.11 or newer engine. Server code, scripts, and generated tooling use modern import syntax and ES2022 targets.

Configured inpackage.json, server/package.json, and TypeScript configs.
Used forRunning Express, Prisma tooling, TypeScript execution through tsx, report verification scripts, seed scripts, and local automation.
Why it fitsNode 20 provides stable modern JavaScript APIs, native fetch support used by Turnstile verification, and compatibility with current tooling.
Maintainer checkKeep imports extension-aware in server output and confirm generated code remains compatible with NodeNext module resolution.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.3 TypeScript Strict Mode

Typed implementation language across client, server, and shared packages.

TypeScript is the primary implementation language. The client compiles through Vite with JSX support, the server uses NodeNext module resolution, and shared contracts emit declarations for reuse.

Configured inclient/tsconfig.json, server/tsconfig.json, and shared/tsconfig.json.
Used forDTOs, module contracts, React components, service inputs, repository return types, validation helper signatures, and shared permission vocabulary.
Why it fitsProcurement systems have many workflow states and cross-module records; strict typing reduces accidental contract drift.
Maintainer checkPreserve strict mode, avoid widening shared contracts without tests, and keep generated declarations valid for both client and server consumers.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.4 React 19 And React DOM

Primary frontend rendering technology.

React renders the production ProcureX application and all page-level experiences that replaced the static prototype surfaces. React DOM mounts the application in the Vite browser entrypoint.

Configured inclient/package.json and client/src/main.tsx.
Used forProcureX pages, public routes, authenticated workspaces, admin surfaces, forms, upload controls, dashboards, and reusable shared components.
Why it fitsThe platform is highly interactive and stateful; React keeps page composition and workflow UI testable.
Maintainer checkKeep user-visible behavior under Testing Library coverage when changing page components or route-level rendering.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.5 Vite And React Plugin

Frontend development server, build pipeline, and test environment.

Vite provides the client development server, production build, alias resolution, test environment configuration, and Rollup-powered bundling strategy.

Configured inclient/vite.config.ts.
Used forFast local dev on port 5173, React plugin transformation, path aliases, jsdom tests, and optimized build output.
Why it fitsThe UI is large and screenshot-heavy; Vite keeps iteration fast while preserving production bundling controls.
Maintainer checkWhen adding large libraries, update manual chunking only if bundle grouping or page load behavior needs deliberate control.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.6 Vite Manual Chunk Strategy

Explicit browser bundle grouping for predictable production assets.

The client build groups MUI, router, Redux, i18n, React, Axios, charts, and miscellaneous dependencies into named vendor chunks. This makes build output easier to reason about as the application grows.

Configured inclient/vite.config.ts under rollupOptions.output.manualChunks.
Used forSeparating large framework and library dependencies from feature code.
Why it fitsProcureX has many feature modules and a large UI surface, so predictable vendor chunks help maintenance and performance analysis.
Maintainer checkAfter adding a major dependency, inspect build output to decide whether it should join an existing vendor group.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.7 Material UI And MUI Icons

Component foundation and icon library.

MUI provides a tested component foundation while ProcureX styles layer the product-specific visual language on top. MUI Icons provide standardized action and navigation symbols.

Configured inclient/package.json with @mui/material and @mui/icons-material.
Used forInteractive controls, layout primitives, dialogs, forms, icons, and shared UI pieces where the production app benefits from accessible defaults.
Why it fitsProcurement workflows need consistent forms, tables, buttons, and navigational controls more than decorative custom widgets.
Maintainer checkKeep MUI usage consistent with the ProcureX design CSS and avoid mixing incompatible component patterns on the same surface.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.8 Emotion Styling Runtime

Style engine required by the MUI component stack.

Emotion supports MUI styled components and runtime style injection. ProcureX also uses static CSS for design-system compatibility, so Emotion is part of the component layer rather than the only styling mechanism.

Configured inclient/package.json with @emotion/react and @emotion/styled.
Used forMUI styling internals and component-level styling support.
Why it fitsIt is the supported styling engine for the selected MUI version.
Maintainer checkAvoid duplicating large style rules in Emotion when the existing ProcureX CSS layer already owns page-level design.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.9 Redux Toolkit And React Redux

Application state management for authenticated workflows.

Redux Toolkit provides structured slices and store setup while React Redux connects components to authentication, notifications, and feature state.

Configured inclient/package.json and client app store files.
Used forAuth/session state, notifications, and feature state that must survive route changes or coordinate multiple components.
Why it fitsProcureX workflows span pages and modules; central state makes guards and shared UI behavior predictable.
Maintainer checkKeep API side effects in feature helpers or thunks and keep reducers focused on state transitions.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.10 React Router

Client-side routing and access-gated navigation.

React Router maps public, protected, verified, and administrator routes to lazy-loaded ProcureX pages. It is the client-side navigation backbone.

Configured inclient/src/app/router.tsx.
Used forPublic pages, authenticated workspace pages, admin-only pages, redirects, route guards, and deep links.
Why it fitsThe platform is a suite of modules; route-level ownership makes the workspace predictable.
Maintainer checkWhen adding a protected page, decide the guard requirements in the router rather than burying access decisions inside page UI.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.11 Axios API Client

Browser HTTP client with auth and localization interceptors.

Axios is wrapped in a shared API client that applies the base URL, bearer token, ProcureX language header, and Accept-Language header before requests leave the browser.

Configured inclient/src/shared/api/http.ts.
Used forFeature API helpers, JSON requests, document downloads, error handling, and authenticated backend communication.
Why it fitsInterceptors centralize common request behavior across many modules.
Maintainer checkKeep token, language, and base URL handling centralized so feature modules do not invent their own HTTP policy.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.12 i18next And React i18next

Localization stack for English and Swahili behavior.

The i18n stack provides translated UI copy and request-language propagation. API error helpers also use language state to present localized guidance.

Configured inclient/package.json, client/src/i18n, and shared API helpers.
Used forLanguage switching, localized loading states, error notifications, static content, and request headers.
Why it fitsProcureX targets procurement users who may need English or Swahili guidance across formal workflows.
Maintainer checkWhen adding user-visible text, add localized resources and test layout behavior where translations are longer.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.13 Recharts

Charting library for dashboards and analytics surfaces.

Recharts supports dashboard, analytics, records, and admin visualization surfaces where procurement activity is summarized visually.

Configured inclient/package.json.
Used forCharts, KPIs, analytics panels, records summaries, and admin metrics.
Why it fitsIt integrates cleanly with React components and is sufficient for operational procurement analytics.
Maintainer checkKeep charts accessible with surrounding labels, tables, or text summaries so assessment evidence is not visual-only.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.14 Socket.IO Client

Realtime-capable browser dependency for future live workflow updates.

The client includes socket.io-client as the browser realtime dependency. It prepares communication and notification surfaces for live updates where the API supports them.

Configured inclient/package.json.
Used forRealtime-ready communication, notification, or workflow update channels.
Why it fitsProcurement workflows benefit from timely message, clarification, and status changes.
Maintainer checkAuthenticate realtime channels and document server-side event contracts before expanding live behavior.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.15 html2pdf.js

Client-side PDF export support for tender and annex documents.

html2pdf.js supports browser-side PDF generation where a rendered tender document or annex needs to become a downloadable artifact from the client experience.

Configured inclient/package.json and procurement page tests.
Used forTender document and annex export flows in the client.
Why it fitsSome procurement documents are assembled from browser-visible structured data and can be exported without a server round trip.
Maintainer checkKeep generated document content testable and avoid relying on visual-only checks for official records.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.16 xlsx

Spreadsheet import/export support.

The xlsx package supports structured spreadsheet data workflows such as procurement tables, records, financial schedules, or operational exports.

Configured inclient/package.json.
Used forExcel-compatible import or export behavior where feature pages need tabular procurement data.
Why it fitsProcurement users often exchange commercial, BOQ, supplier, or report data in spreadsheet form.
Maintainer checkValidate imported data before sending it to APIs and preserve explicit column mapping in tests.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.17 Shared Package Contracts

Cross-workspace types, permissions, localization, and domain vocabulary.

The shared package exposes contracts consumed by both client and server. It keeps account types, permissions, feature gates, localization helpers, and domain vocabulary consistent.

Configured inshared/package.json and shared/src/index.ts.
Used forPermission names, feature gate types, API error contracts, localization data, and shared procurement domain types.
Why it fitsShared vocabulary prevents the client and server from drifting on access rules or DTO semantics.
Maintainer checkTreat shared exports as public internal API: update both consumers and tests when changing them.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.18 ProcureX CSS And Design Assets

Static design-system, prototype compatibility, and visual parity assets.

The client uses global styles, token CSS, prototype compatibility CSS, and ProcureX UI design-system CSS to preserve the reference interface while supporting production React behavior.

Configured inclient/src/styles/ and client/public/procurex-ui/.
Used forLayout, visual tokens, page-specific presentation, PDF-render classes, screenshot parity, and static assets.
Why it fitsThe platform migrated from a static design prototype, so preserving visual parity requires a stable CSS layer.
Maintainer checkAvoid unrelated style churn and check desktop/mobile screenshots after changing page-level CSS.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.19 Testing Library And jsdom

React component and page interaction testing.

Testing Library checks user-visible UI behavior in a jsdom environment. It is used heavily for route guards, page states, forms, uploads, and feature workflows.

Configured inclient/vite.config.ts test settings and client dev dependencies.
Used forRendering React pages, simulating user actions, checking DOM output, and validating guard redirects.
Why it fitsProcurement screens need behavior tests that read like user flows rather than implementation-detail snapshots.
Maintainer checkAssert accessible labels, text, roles, and visible outcomes rather than fragile component internals.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.20 Playwright

Browser automation for screenshots, E2E flows, and report verification.

Playwright is used for UI parity screenshot capture, selected browser flow tests, and A4 PDF page-count verification for the implementation report.

Configured inclient/package.json and scripts under client/e2e/ and scripts/.
Used forOpening real browser pages, capturing desktop/mobile screenshots, checking flows, and rendering print artifacts.
Why it fitsThe report and UI parity process require browser-accurate rendering evidence.
Maintainer checkKeep screenshot routes and seeded test assumptions synchronized when adding pages.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.21 ESLint, typescript-eslint, React Hooks, And React Refresh Rules

Static quality tooling for frontend code.

ESLint combines JavaScript recommended rules, TypeScript rules, React Hooks rules, and React Refresh export checks for the client workspace.

Configured inclient/eslint.config.js.
Used forCatching common TypeScript and React mistakes before runtime.
Why it fitsLarge multi-page React code benefits from consistent hooks and component export rules.
Maintainer checkRun client lint when changing shared hooks, route code, or component modules.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.22 Express

Backend HTTP framework and module router host.

Express hosts the ProcureX API. It applies global middleware, health checks, registered module routers, 404 handling, and centralized error handling.

Configured inserver/src/app.ts and module routes.ts files.
Used forAPI routing, middleware composition, request parsing, error handling, and module mounting.
Why it fitsThe modular API pattern maps cleanly to Express routers and controller/service boundaries.
Maintainer checkKeep global middleware before module mounts and keep module routes narrow.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.23 Prisma Client And Prisma CLI

Typed database client, schema contract, migrations, validation, and seed tooling.

Prisma provides the database schema, generated client, migrations, seed scripts, and validation commands used by the server workspace.

Configured inserver/prisma/schema.prisma and server package scripts.
Used forRepository persistence, migrations, schema validation, data seeding, and typed query access.
Why it fitsThe database is large and multi-domain; generated types make repository code easier to maintain.
Maintainer checkRun schema validation after schema changes and keep migration names aligned with workflow intent.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.24 PostgreSQL

Primary relational database for procurement workflow state.

PostgreSQL stores identity, organizations, procurement, bidding, evaluation, contracts, financial data, communication, compliance, documents, and audit records.

Configured indocker-compose.yml and server/.env.example.
Used forTransactional workflow records, row-level security policies, audit trails, and relational module data.
Why it fitsProcurement requires durable relational consistency, tenant-aware access, and complex workflow history.
Maintainer checkPreserve RLS assumptions and verify migrations against realistic seeded data.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.25 Zod

Runtime schema validation for API inputs.

Zod schemas validate route params, query strings, request bodies, document metadata, workflow payloads, and module status queries before service methods run.

Configured inModule validators.ts files and server/src/app.ts error normalization.
Used forRejecting malformed or incomplete input with structured field errors.
Why it fitsTypeScript types disappear at runtime; Zod supplies runtime guardrails at API boundaries.
Maintainer checkAdd or update schema tests when changing accepted payload shapes.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.26 Helmet

HTTP security headers and content security policy.

Helmet applies browser security headers and a configured content security policy before domain routes are mounted.

Configured inserver/src/app.ts.
Used forDefault security headers, CSP directives, frame restrictions, object restrictions, and allowed script/connect/image/style/font sources.
Why it fitsThe API must establish browser security posture centrally instead of leaving each route to decide headers.
Maintainer checkWhen adding external browser services, update CSP narrowly and document the reason.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.27 CORS Middleware

Browser origin allow-listing for API calls.

CORS is configured from security settings. Requests from missing origins or allowed configured origins are accepted; unapproved browser origins receive a structured security error.

Configured inserver/src/app.ts and server/src/security/config.ts.
Used forRestricting which browser origins can call the API.
Why it fitsThe frontend and API can run on different hosts locally and in deployment, but the API should not accept arbitrary browser origins.
Maintainer checkSet production CORS_ORIGINS explicitly and avoid wildcard origins.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.28 Redis And ioredis

Fast shared store for auth rate limiting and local infrastructure readiness.

Redis is available through Docker Compose and ioredis. Authentication rate limiting uses Redis when configured, with memory fallback for non-production development.

Configured indocker-compose.yml, server/.env.example, and server/src/security/rateLimit.ts.
Used forRate-limit counters and local service infrastructure.
Why it fitsRate limiting must be shared across server instances in production.
Maintainer checkProduction requires REDIS_URL and should not rely on memory fallback.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.29 Elasticsearch

Search infrastructure for admin and marketplace-oriented indexing.

Elasticsearch is provided in local infrastructure and the server includes the official client for indexed search and reindex workflows.
