Configured indocker-compose.yml, server/package.json, and compliance admin search files.
Used forAdmin search, platform oversight, and searchable operational records where configured.
Why it fitsCompliance and admin use cases need search across records that are not convenient to inspect only through relational tables.
Maintainer checkKeep relational source of truth clear and treat search indexes as derived read models.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.30 AWS S3 Client And MinIO

Object storage abstraction for document uploads and local development.

The server can store documents locally or through S3-compatible object storage. MinIO supplies the local S3-compatible service in Docker Compose.

Configured inserver/src/modules/documents/storage.ts, server/.env.example, and docker-compose.yml.
Used forDocument object storage, official documents, attachments, and uploaded evidence.
Why it fitsProcurement records often include binary files that should not live directly in relational columns.
Maintainer checkKeep object keys safe, validate file content, and store metadata/checksums beside database records.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.31 Busboy Multipart Upload Parser

Streaming file upload parser for document endpoints.

Busboy parses multipart document uploads with file and field limits, allowing the server to validate and store files while controlling payload size.

Configured inserver/src/modules/documents/storage.ts.
Used forSingle-file document upload parsing with size, file count, and field count limits.
Why it fitsProcurement evidence can be large, so upload parsing should stream and enforce limits early.
Maintainer checkKeep file limits aligned with documented upload policy and never bypass safety validation.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.32 pdf-lib

Server-side PDF generation and report page-count verification.

pdf-lib is used by the server for official PDF rendering and by report verification tooling to count generated A4 pages.

Configured inserver/package.json and document renderer files.
Used forOfficial document PDFs, metadata, signatures, and verification of generated report page counts.
Why it fitsIt can create and inspect PDFs in Node without a browser dependency for server-side document workflows.
Maintainer checkKeep official document tests checking PDF headers, size, and checksum shape.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.33 Nodemailer

SMTP-capable email delivery for local/testing support flows.

Nodemailer supports configurable email delivery paths, especially local or testing email behavior around support and identity-related messages.

Configured inserver/package.json, server/.env.example, and support email files.
Used forSupport email and SMTP-style delivery where configured.
Why it fitsIt is a flexible Node email transport for non-production and controlled delivery scenarios.
Maintainer checkDo not enable local-only SMTP policy in production when security config requires production providers.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.34 Resend

Production-oriented transactional email provider.

Resend is the production-oriented identity email provider expected by security configuration when running in production mode.

Configured inserver/package.json, server/.env.example, and identity delivery provider code.
Used forTransactional identity email delivery such as activation, password reset, and verification flows.
Why it fitsIdentity events need reliable provider-backed delivery rather than console-only local output.
Maintainer checkProduction requires RESEND_API_KEY and RESEND_FROM when Resend is selected.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.35 dotenv And Environment-Driven Runtime Configuration

Configuration loading for local and deployment-specific behavior.

dotenv and helper scripts load example environment values for local runs while production validation enforces security-sensitive variables.

Configured inserver/package.json, scripts/run-with-env.mjs, and server/.env.example.
Used forDatabase URLs, Redis, S3, provider credentials, upload limits, CORS, Turnstile, and server port configuration.
Why it fitsThe same codebase needs local development, test, and deployment-specific settings without committing secrets.
Maintainer checkDocument new environment variables and add production validation when they affect security.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.36 Docker Compose Infrastructure

Local PostgreSQL, Redis, Elasticsearch, and MinIO services.

Docker Compose provides a reproducible local infrastructure layer for development and validation. It starts the services needed by the server without requiring manual host installation.

Configured indocker-compose.yml.
Used forPostgreSQL, Redis, Elasticsearch, MinIO, persistent volumes, ports, and service health checks.
Why it fitsProcureX depends on multiple infrastructure services; Compose keeps local setup repeatable.
Maintainer checkKeep example environment ports and Compose service ports synchronized.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.37 tsx

TypeScript execution for development server and scripts.

tsx runs server TypeScript in watch mode and executes Prisma seed and verification scripts without a separate manual compile step.

Configured inserver/package.json scripts.
Used forServer development, seed scripts, RLS verification, and help-centre validation.
Why it fitsIt keeps TypeScript-based operational scripts close to runtime code.
Maintainer checkKeep long-running dev scripts separate from one-shot validation scripts.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.38 Vitest And Supertest

Unit, integration, and HTTP boundary testing.

Vitest runs server, client, and shared tests. Supertest exercises Express app endpoints without starting a real network listener.

Configured inclient/package.json, server/package.json, and shared/package.json.
Used forModule services, API routing, app health, route guards, React pages, shared utilities, and document workflows.
Why it fitsThe test stack covers both pure logic and HTTP behavior while staying fast enough for local development.
Maintainer checkAdd focused tests beside the module being changed before relying on broad smoke checks.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.39 Prisma Migration, Generate, Validate, And Seed Tooling

Database lifecycle commands in npm scripts.

The server workspace exposes commands for Prisma validation, generation, development migrations, deployment migrations, and multiple seed datasets.

Configured inserver/package.json and root database scripts.
Used forSchema validation, client generation, migration execution, seeded demos, cleanup flows, and local repeatability.
Why it fitsProcurement workflows require realistic linked data for testing bids, awards, contracts, invoices, and records.
Maintainer checkKeep seed scripts idempotent or provide cleanup scripts when creating demo workflows.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

19.40 Type Definitions And Developer Support Packages

Typed integration with Node, React, Express, CORS, Nodemailer, Supertest, and Busboy.

The repository includes direct @types packages and TypeScript ESLint tooling so external libraries remain type-aware in implementation and tests.

Configured inClient and server devDependencies.
Used forCompile-time safety around framework APIs, test helpers, file uploads, email, browser globals, and Node APIs.
Why it fitsType-aware external APIs reduce accidental misuse of middleware, request handlers, and component props.
Maintainer checkUpdate type packages with their runtime packages when upgrading major versions.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20. Security Implementation Deep Dive

This chapter explains how security is implemented in ProcureX at the HTTP, browser, identity, authorization, database, document, signing, audit, and operations layers. It uses actual source paths so the reader can trace each security claim to implementation evidence.

The security model is defense in depth: the client prevents incorrect navigation, the API validates and authorizes requests, PostgreSQL enforces row-level policies, document storage validates binary evidence, and signed actions create proof for high-risk workflow decisions.

20.0 Security Traceability Matrix

ControlImplementation file(s)Threat addressedValidation method
Helmet/CSP headersserver/src/app.tsClickjacking, unsafe sources, object injectionApp header tests or browser response inspection
CORS allow-listserver/src/app.ts, server/src/security/config.tsUnapproved browser originsAllowed/denied origin API tests
Production config validationserver/src/security/config.tsUnsafe provider or missing secret deploymentProduction-auth configuration tests
Auth rate limitingserver/src/security/rateLimit.tsCredential stuffing and OTP abuseRate-limit tests and audit event checks
Turnstileserver/src/security/turnstile.tsAutomated public entry abuseWidget/provider tests
Route guardsclient/src/app/routeGuards.tsxUnauthorized UI navigationRoute guard tests
Access policyserver/src/security/accessPolicy.tsPermission drift and role confusionAccess-policy unit tests
Database contextserver/src/db/context.tsMissing tenant contextRLS verification
RLS policiesserver/prisma/sql/rls/*.sqlCross-tenant data leakageRLS verification
Signed actionsserver/src/modules/identity/sensitiveActionSigning.tsRepudiation and tamperingSigning tests and module workflow tests
Signing credentialsserver/src/modules/identity/signing.tsPlain private keys and weak signaturesSigning helper tests
Document uploadsserver/src/modules/documents/storage.tsMalicious or oversized filesDocument upload tests
API errorsserver/src/modules/shared/apiErrors.ts, client/src/shared/api/errors.tsLeaked internals and inconsistent error UXAPI error tests
Audit eventsidentity repository, compliance admin, rateLimit.tsUntraceable security activityService and repository tests

20.1 Security Architecture Overview

Layered controls across browser, API, identity, database, documents, and operations.

ProcureX security is implemented as a layered model. Browser requests are constrained by headers and CORS, sensitive entry points use anti-abuse controls, route guards protect client navigation, services enforce workflow rules, database context enables row-level security, and document/signature flows add evidence controls.

Primary filesserver/src/app.ts, server/src/security/*, client/src/app/routeGuards.tsx, server/src/db/context.ts.
Threats addressedUnauthorized browser origins, unverified users, over-posted payloads, tenant data leaks, unsigned sensitive actions, unsafe uploads, and weak production configuration.
Implementation shapeCentral app middleware first, route guards and service checks second, database context and RLS below persistence, and tests as verification evidence.
Maintainer checkDo not treat any single layer as sufficient; protected workflows need both UI and server-side enforcement.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.2 Helmet Headers And Content Security Policy

Browser-side security headers applied before module routes.

The Express app configures Helmet with explicit CSP directives. Defaults restrict content to self, block object sources, prevent framing by other pages, and allow only known script, connect, image, style, font, and frame sources needed by ProcureX and Turnstile.

Implemented inserver/src/app.ts.
Threats addressedClickjacking, malicious object embedding, unexpected script sources, unexpected API connections, and unsafe frame ancestors.
How implementedHelmet middleware is registered before CORS, JSON parsing, health routes, and module router mounting.
Validation methodApp tests and manual browser checks should confirm headers are present and external services fail closed unless explicitly listed.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.3 CORS Origin Allow-Listing

Controlled browser-origin access to the API.

CORS reads configured allowed origins and falls back to known local development origins when no explicit list is supplied. Unknown origins receive a structured 403 security error instead of silent acceptance.

Implemented inserver/src/app.ts and server/src/security/config.ts.
Threats addressedUnapproved browser origins calling authenticated API endpoints.
How implementedThe origin callback accepts empty origins and configured origins, otherwise it calls the centralized request-error helper with a CORS denial code.
Validation methodAPI boundary tests should check allowed and denied origins when CORS behavior changes.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.4 Production Security Configuration Validation

Fail-fast checks for production-only requirements.

Production runtime calls validateProductionSecurityConfig before the app starts. This prevents local-only notification providers, missing Redis, missing CORS origins, missing Turnstile secrets, missing registry provider credentials, and missing signing secrets from slipping into production.

Implemented inserver/src/security/config.ts.
Threats addressedAccidental deployment with console-only OTP delivery, incomplete anti-abuse controls, missing provider secrets, or weak signing configuration.
How implementedThe config helper detects production mode, normalizes provider selections, checks required environment variables, and throws before Express starts.
Validation methodProduction-auth tests should exercise invalid provider combinations and missing configuration cases.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.5 Request Parsing And Body Limits

JSON parsing control for API payload size.

The Express app applies JSON parsing with a configurable request body limit. This is separate from multipart document upload limits and protects JSON endpoints from unexpected payload size growth.

Implemented inserver/src/app.ts using REQUEST_BODY_LIMIT.
Threats addressedOversized JSON payloads, accidental memory pressure, and unbounded request bodies.
How implementedexpress.json is mounted once before module routes with a default limit of 160mb unless overridden.
Validation methodEndpoint tests should cover oversized or malformed payload behavior for high-risk modules.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.6 Authentication Rate Limiting

Redis-backed rate limiting with audited lockout events.

Auth rate limiting hashes the request IP, increments a limiter counter, expires counters by window, audits limit hits where database access is configured, and returns a structured 429 error when a threshold is exceeded.

Implemented inserver/src/security/rateLimit.ts.
Threats addressedCredential stuffing, OTP brute force, password reset abuse, and high-frequency authentication attempts.
How implementedRedis is used when configured. Non-production can fall back to an in-memory map; production requires Redis.
Validation methodIdentity production-auth tests should verify rate-limited behavior and audit resilience.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.7 Cloudflare Turnstile Verification

Anti-automation challenge verification for sensitive public entry points.

Turnstile verification accepts special local development and Cloudflare test tokens only outside production. Otherwise it sends the token and optional remote IP to Cloudflare siteverify and rejects unavailable or failing checks.

Implemented inserver/src/security/turnstile.ts and client Turnstile widget code.
Threats addressedAutomated registration, support, or authentication abuse.
How implementedThe server posts form data to the Turnstile verification endpoint and returns success plus provider error codes.
Validation methodWidget tests and identity/support tests should verify local, missing-secret, and provider-failure behavior.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.8 Session Token Transport And Client API Headers

Bearer token and language headers applied by the shared HTTP client.

The client stores the auth token in localStorage and adds it as a Bearer token through an Axios request interceptor. The same interceptor adds ProcureX language headers to support localized API responses.

Implemented inclient/src/shared/api/authToken.ts and client/src/shared/api/http.ts.
Threats addressedMissing auth headers, inconsistent request language, and duplicated feature-level token logic.
How implementedFeature modules use the shared Axios client instead of constructing their own raw HTTP policy.
Validation methodAPI helper tests and auth-flow tests should verify protected requests receive the expected Authorization header.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.9 ProtectedRoute Client Guard

Client-side route protection for signed-in and verified users.

ProtectedRoute blocks unauthenticated users, redirects expired sessions, optionally requires verification, checks minimum trust tier, enforces permissions and feature gates, and can redirect admin users away from user surfaces.

Implemented inclient/src/app/routeGuards.tsx.
Threats addressedAccidental exposure of protected screens to unauthenticated, unverified, or under-trusted users.
How implementedThe guard reads auth state from Redux, checks route options, and returns redirects before rendering page children.
Validation methodclient/src/app/routeGuards.test.tsx verifies redirect and access behavior.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.10 AdminRoute Enforcement

Administrator-only client surfaces.

AdminRoute requires an authenticated user with ADMIN account type and admin.access permission. Non-admin users are redirected to the verified dashboard or identity verification depending on their status.

Implemented inclient/src/app/routeGuards.tsx.
Threats addressedUser accounts opening administrator dashboards, audit screens, compliance tools, or user management pages.
How implementedThe guard checks session state, account type, and permission before rendering admin children.
Validation methodRoute guard tests and admin page tests should verify both allowed and rejected states.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.11 Permissions And Feature Gates

Shared access vocabulary computed on the server.

Access policy converts account type, verification status, capabilities, trust tier, risk level, and screening status into permissions and feature gates. Admins receive all permission names; user permissions depend on operating rules.

Implemented inserver/src/security/accessPolicy.ts and shared permission contracts.
Threats addressedInconsistent buyer/supplier/admin authorization decisions across modules.
How implementedcomputeAccessContext returns permission arrays and featureGate booleans consumed by sessions and route guards.
Validation methodAccess-policy tests should cover admin, buyer, supplier, unverified, blocked, and low-trust subjects.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.12 Organization Capability Model

Buyer and supplier powers are organization capabilities, not login roles.

ProcureX separates account type from procurement capability. A normal user can operate as a buyer or supplier depending on organization capabilities; administrator status is a platform authority rather than a procurement party role.

Implemented inShared contracts, identity/session DTOs, organization models, and access-policy computation.
Threats addressedRole confusion, incorrect cross-organization actions, and hard-coded buyer/supplier login assumptions.
How implementedCapabilities are included in access subjects and can be evaluated by app policy and RLS helper functions.
Validation methodModule tests should verify buyer-only and supplier-only operations with realistic organization context.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.13 Trust Tier, Risk Level, And Screening Status

Risk-aware access context for sensitive procurement operations.

Access computation uses verification status, trust tier, risk level, and screening status to decide whether a user can operate. Blocked screening status and critical risk prevent normal operation when gates are enabled.

Implemented inserver/src/security/accessPolicy.ts and identity service workflows.
Threats addressedHigh-risk or blocked organizations continuing sensitive procurement activity.
How implementedTrust tiers are ranked and compared; risk and screening defaults are normalized when absent.
Validation methodIdentity and access-policy tests should cover rejected, expired, blocked, critical-risk, and approved states.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.14 Prisma Database Context

Request identity values injected into PostgreSQL transaction settings.

withDbContext wraps Prisma work in a transaction and sets app.current_user_id, app.current_organization_id, app.current_account_type, and app.current_capabilities. RLS SQL functions then read those values.

Implemented inserver/src/db/context.ts.
Threats addressedRepository code running without tenant or user context for RLS-protected tables.
How implementedThe helper uses set_config in the transaction before executing the repository work callback.
Validation methodnpm --workspace server run verify:rls should verify context-dependent data access.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.15 PostgreSQL RLS Context Functions

Database-side helpers for current user, organization, account type, and capabilities.

The RLS SQL creates app.current_user_id, app.current_organization_id, app.current_account_type, app.current_capabilities, app.is_admin, app.same_org, and app.has_capability functions.

Implemented inserver/prisma/sql/rls/001_app_context.sql.
Threats addressedAd hoc tenant checks repeated inconsistently in application queries.
How implementedStable SQL functions read transaction settings and expose reusable policy predicates.
Validation methodRLS verification should test user, admin, same organization, public marketplace, buyer, and supplier paths.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.16 PostgreSQL Row-Level Security Policies

Tenant isolation and workflow-specific database policies.

The RLS SQL enables and forces row-level security on organization, document, procurement, bidding, contract, financial, communication, compliance, identity, tender, bid, and evaluation tables with policies based on user, organization, admin status, public visibility, and capabilities.

Implemented inserver/prisma/sql/rls/002_enable_rls.sql.
Threats addressedCross-tenant reads/writes, unauthorized bid mutation, improper tender visibility, and identity record exposure.
How implementedGeneral tenant_isolation policies are created for org-column tables, then specialized policies are created for users, accounts, sessions, verification profiles, tenders, bids, and evaluation scores.
Validation methodRun RLS verification and add policy tests whenever schema tables are added.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.17 Zod Validation As A Security Boundary

Runtime request validation before services mutate workflow state.

Module validators reject invalid params, query strings, and bodies before service methods perform domain work. Zod errors are converted into localized structured API error responses.

Implemented inModule validators.ts files and server/src/app.ts.
Threats addressedMalformed payloads, over-posting, missing required fields, invalid workflow action data, and unsafe query parameters.
How implementedControllers parse input with schemas and app-level error handling maps ZodError to VALIDATION_FAILED responses.
Validation methodModule validator tests and controller/service tests should cover accepted and rejected payloads.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.18 Centralized API Errors And Localized Client Guidance

Safe, consistent error shaping across server and client.

The server uses shared API error helpers to shape status, code, user message, reason, actions, and field errors. The client converts API failures into localized notifications and safer user-facing guidance.

Implemented inserver/src/modules/shared/apiErrors.ts and client/src/shared/api/errors.ts.
Threats addressedLeaking raw server failures, inconsistent permission messages, confusing validation output, and unlocalized failure states.
How implementedThe app error handler normalizes Zod and request errors; the client maps status/code/message patterns to actionable copy.
Validation methodAPI error tests should cover validation, auth, permission, not found, conflict, rate limit, upload, and document errors.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.19 Identity Registration, Verification, And Session Security

Account lifecycle security around OTP, activation, password setup, sign-in, MFA, and profile verification.

The identity service coordinates registration, OTP challenges, email activation, password setup, sign-in sessions, MFA flows, password recovery, verification profiles, registry checks, screening, and trust evaluation.

Implemented inserver/src/modules/identity/service.ts and related identity provider files.
Threats addressedUnverified accounts, weak account activation flows, missing delivery validation, unauthorized profile changes, and incomplete verification evidence.
How implementedIdentity workflows create challenges, verify submitted codes, persist sessions, calculate access context, and record audit-relevant events.
Validation methodIdentity production-auth tests and delivery-provider tests verify registration and security-sensitive edge cases.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.20 Provider Configuration Restrictions

Production rules for email, SMS, WhatsApp, registry, screening, and signing providers.

Production security validation rejects local-only dev-console, SMTP, and WhatsApp test-style identity paths and requires provider credentials for Resend, SMS providers, registry lookup, email validation, and signing.

Implemented inserver/src/security/config.ts and server/.env.example.
Threats addressedProduction deployment without real delivery, registry, validation, or signing providers.
How implementedEnvironment values are normalized and checked during app startup. Missing required production variables produce a hard failure.
Validation methodConfiguration tests should assert that unsafe production provider combinations fail before serving traffic.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.21 Digital Signing Credential Security

Ed25519 keys protected by keyphrase-derived AES-256-GCM encryption.

Signing credentials are generated as Ed25519 key pairs. Private keys are encrypted with AES-256-GCM using a key derived from the user keyphrase with scrypt. Fingerprints and provider metadata support later verification.

Implemented inserver/src/modules/identity/signing.ts.
Threats addressedPlaintext private-key storage, weak signing evidence, invalid keyphrase usage, and inactive credential signing.
How implementedThe module validates keyphrase length, derives encryption keys with scrypt, encrypts private keys with random salt and IV, and signs canonical hashes.
Validation methodSigning tests should cover credential creation, repeated keyphrase validation, decrypt failure, re-encryption, revoke, and sign behavior.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.22 Sensitive Action Signing

Canonical payload hashes and signed action records for high-risk workflow events.

Sensitive action signing builds a canonical payload containing module key, action key, entity details, user, organization, payload, and signed time. It hashes the canonical JSON, signs it with the user credential, and stores a signed action record.

Implemented inserver/src/modules/identity/sensitiveActionSigning.ts.
Threats addressedRepudiation of important workflow approvals, unsigned contract actions, and tampered payload evidence.
How implementedCanonical JSON sorting creates stable hashes; Ed25519 signature metadata and key fingerprint are persisted with the signed action.
Validation methodModule tests should require keyphrases for protected actions and verify signed action records are created.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.23 Document Upload Size, Count, And Field Limits

Multipart controls before files enter storage.

Document upload parsing allows one file at a time, limits field count, enforces max bytes, and deletes partially stored objects if parsing or validation fails.

Implemented inserver/src/modules/documents/storage.ts.
Threats addressedOversized files, upload flooding, partial-object leftovers, and malformed multipart payloads.
How implementedBusboy limits fileSize, files, and fields; file streams are monitored and failed uploads clean up storage.
Validation methodDocument tests should cover missing file, too many files, too-large files, and invalid multipart fields.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.24 MIME, Extension, And Magic-Byte Validation

Content safety checks for uploaded procurement evidence.

Uploaded documents must use allowed extensions and MIME types, reject explicitly unsafe extensions, and pass magic-byte checks for PDFs, PNGs, JPGs, DOCX, and XLSX files.

Implemented inserver/src/modules/documents/storage.ts.
Threats addressedExecutable uploads, disguised scripts, mismatched PDF/image/Office content, shell scripts, HTML payloads, and unsafe archive/script formats.
How implementedValidation checks extension, MIME type, first bytes, script/HTML prefixes, MZ executables, and PDF/image/ZIP signatures.
Validation methodUpload tests should include accepted files, blocked executable extensions, MIME mismatches, bad magic bytes, and invalid metadata.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.25 Safe Object Keys And Local Path Protection

Storage paths sanitized before local or S3 persistence.

Object keys are built from sanitized source module, entity type, entity id, timestamp, random suffix, and safe filename. Local paths are normalized and checked to remain under the configured upload root.

Implemented inserver/src/modules/documents/storage.ts.
Threats addressedPath traversal, unsafe filenames, object-key injection, and accidental writes outside the document storage root.
How implementedFilename, display name, path part, document type, and UUID-like values are normalized before storage.
Validation methodStorage tests should cover traversal attempts, unsafe characters, and localPathForObjectKey root enforcement.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.26 Storage Drivers, Checksums, And Metadata Filtering

Local/S3 storage with hashes and user metadata controls.

The storage layer supports local files and S3-compatible object storage. It computes SHA-256 checksums, stores size/content-type metadata, and filters user metadata fields that could expose storage paths, encrypted payloads, or key references.

Implemented inserver/src/modules/documents/storage.ts.
Threats addressedTampered document evidence, unsafe user-controlled metadata, leaked storage internals, and misconfigured S3 storage.
How implementedStorage driver selection reads environment variables; checksums are computed while streaming or buffering; blocked metadata keys are removed.
Validation methodDocument repository tests should verify stored checksums, metadata, content retrieval, and failure cases.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.27 Profile Image Upload Security

Separate image-specific upload controls for identity profile media.

Profile image handling is separated from general document storage so image size, type, storage location, and account profile behavior can evolve independently from procurement evidence documents.

Implemented inserver/src/modules/identity/profileImageStorage.ts and identity profile routes.
Threats addressedOversized media, unsupported image content, and mixing profile media policy with official document policy.
How implementedProfile image storage uses dedicated environment settings and identity service integration.
Validation methodProfile image tests should verify allowed types, max size, replacement behavior, and content retrieval.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.28 Audit Events And Security Evidence

Security-relevant events recorded for accountability.

Audit events are created for important actions such as rate limiting and broader identity/admin workflows. Audit storage is designed not to block enforcement when recording fails in a secondary path.

Implemented inserver/src/security/rateLimit.ts, identity repository code, and compliance/admin modules.
Threats addressedUntraceable abuse, missing administrative evidence, and lack of security operation history.
How implementedRate-limit auditing hashes IP and user-agent values before storage and catches audit failures so enforcement still proceeds.
Validation methodService tests should verify audit creation for sensitive operations without making audit storage a single point of failure.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.29 Support Email And Contact Security

Controlled public contact and support ticket delivery.

Support email and ticket workflows separate public contact input, authenticated support tickets, email delivery, and comments/status changes. Email tests verify provider behavior and failure handling.

Implemented inserver/src/modules/support/email.ts and support service files.
Threats addressedUnvalidated contact payloads, unhandled email failures, support spam, and confused support-ticket ownership.
How implementedSupport validators shape input; service methods create tickets/comments/status changes and call delivery helpers where required.
Validation methodserver/src/modules/support/email.test.ts and support service tests should remain focused on delivery and workflow outcomes.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.30 Localization-Safe Security Responses

Language-aware request and error handling without leaking internals.

The server reads request language and shapes error responses through shared helpers. The client maps technical errors into localized, user-facing notification titles, reasons, and actions.

Implemented inserver/src/modules/shared/localization.ts, server/src/app.ts, and client/src/shared/api/errors.ts.
Threats addressedRaw stack traces, confusing security failures, and inconsistent bilingual user guidance.
How implemented404 and error middleware call apiErrorResponseBody with request language; client code curates safe copy by status and code.
Validation methodLocalization and API error tests should verify English/Swahili response behavior for common security failures.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.31 Security Test Evidence

Executable checks for production auth, providers, route guards, signing, uploads, and RLS.

Security behavior is covered through focused tests across server and client workspaces. The implementation treats tests as executable documentation for what must remain true.

Implemented inserver/src/__tests__/identity-production-auth.test.ts, server/src/__tests__/identity-delivery-providers.test.ts, client/src/app/routeGuards.test.tsx, signing tests, document tests, and RLS verification.
Threats addressedRegression of security-sensitive behavior after feature work.
How implementedTests exercise service methods, route guards, provider setup, signing helpers, upload storage, and app-level routing.
Validation methodnpm test plus npm --workspace server run verify:rls for database policy checks.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

20.32 Security Maintenance Checklist

How future changes should preserve the implemented security model.

Every security-sensitive change should be reviewed across the same layers used by the implementation: route exposure, validation, service authorization, repository context, RLS policy, document safety, signed action evidence, audit behavior, and user-facing error handling.

Implementation checklistRoute guard, API validator, service permission check, database context, RLS policy, audit event, upload/signature control, and tests.
Threats addressedPartial security changes that protect the UI but not the API, or protect the API but leave persistence policies open.
How implementedKeep controls centralized where possible and add module-local checks only for domain-specific workflow rules.
Validation methodRun narrow tests first, then broader workspace tests and RLS verification when data-access policy is touched.

For assessment and engineering handover, this page should be read as implementation evidence: it names the stack element, explains where it lives, describes how ProcureX uses it, and states what maintainers must protect when changing it.

21. Deployment And Production Hosting Deep Dive

This chapter defines the concrete VM/Docker/Nginx production hosting model for ProcureX, including server layout, environment setup, domain/TLS, release flow, migration handling, rollback, and scaling.

These pages are production operating design for ProcureX unless an item explicitly names repository evidence as already implemented. The hosting baseline is a VM or VPS estate running Docker services behind Nginx with Let's Encrypt TLS.

21.1 VM, Docker, And Nginx Hosting Baseline

Concrete production topology for the first ProcureX launch.

Run ProcureX on one or more Linux VMs. Each VM hosts Docker services for the API, worker-style operational commands, Nginx, and optional local infrastructure services. Nginx is the only public process: it terminates TLS, serves the built React assets, proxies API traffic to the Node/Express container, applies upload-size limits, and exposes health routing.

Primary host roleHardened Ubuntu LTS VM/VPS with Docker Engine, Docker Compose, Nginx, Certbot, log rotation, firewall rules, and SSH-key-only deploy access.
Application containersprocurex-api runs node dist/server.js internally; optional containers run Redis, Elasticsearch, PostgreSQL, and MinIO when not moved to dedicated hosts.
Public entrypointshttps://procurex.example.tld for React and https://api.procurex.example.tld or /api proxying for Express.
Repository evidenceprocurement-platform/package.json, docker-compose.yml, server/.env.example, server/src/app.ts, .github/workflows/procurex-ci-cd.yml

VM/Docker/Nginx Deployment

InternetBrowser HTTPS
EdgeNginx + Certbot
AppDocker API container
DataPostgreSQL
InternetDNS A/AAAA record
EdgeStatic React assets
AppNode 20 Express server
DataRedis
DataElasticsearch
DataMinIO/S3

Private services bind to Docker networks or localhost; only HTTP/HTTPS and controlled SSH are externally reachable.

21.2 Production Server Layout

Filesystem layout operators can create on each VM.

Use a predictable server layout so rollback, backup, and incident response commands never depend on a developer workstation path. The deploy user owns application files, while secrets remain outside the repository checkout.

/opt/procurex/
  releases/<git-sha>/              # checked-out or unpacked release
  current -> releases/<git-sha>/    # active symlink
  shared/env/server.env             # production secrets, mode 0600
  shared/nginx/procurex.conf        # reverse proxy config
  shared/backups/                   # short-lived local staging only
  shared/logs/                      # host-side deployment logs
  docker-compose.prod.yml           # production service composition
Release isolationDeploy each SHA into a new release directory and move the current symlink only after build, migration, and smoke checks pass.
Secret placementKeep environment files in shared host storage or secret manager; never write real credentials into repo files or this report.
OwnershipUse a non-root deployment user in the docker group; reserve root for package, firewall, Nginx, and Certbot maintenance.

21.3 Production Docker Compose Design

How to map the local compose services into production.

The repository currently defines local PostgreSQL, Redis, Elasticsearch, and MinIO in Docker Compose. Production can begin with the same service families, but must use production images, persistent volume paths, restart policies, resource limits, private networks, and secure credentials.

API servicenode:20-alpine or a built ProcureX image; runs npm --workspace server start after npm run build has produced server/dist.
PostgreSQLPostgreSQL 16 with persistent volume, WAL archiving/backup automation, private network exposure only, and no public port binding.
RedisRedis 7 with append-only file, maxmemory policy sized for auth rate limits and future queues, private network only.
ElasticsearchSingle-node for pilot only; memory pinned explicitly, disk alerts enabled, and migration path to dedicated search VM for growth.
MinIO/S3MinIO container for self-hosted S3-compatible storage or external S3 endpoint with bucket versioning and lifecycle rules.

This is production operating design: the repo has local Compose, while production Dockerfiles and production Compose manifests remain future work.

21.4 Domain, TLS, And Reverse Proxy

Concrete Nginx behavior for HTTPS, routing, compression, and uploads.

Nginx should own domain-level concerns so the Express app can focus on API behavior. Keep HTTP-to-HTTPS redirects, TLS renewal, static file cache headers, API proxy headers, and upload limits in one reviewed config.

server {
  listen 443 ssl http2;
  server_name procurex.example.tld;

  root /opt/procurex/current/client/dist;
  index index.html;

  location /assets/ {
    add_header Cache-Control "public, max-age=31536000, immutable";
    try_files $uri =404;
  }

  location /api/ {
    client_max_body_size 170m;
    proxy_set_header Host $host;
    proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto https;
    proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
    proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:4000;
  }

  location / {
    try_files $uri /index.html;
  }
}
TLS sourceUse Let's Encrypt certificates from Certbot or an equivalent ACME client and monitor renewal before expiry.
Upload limitREQUEST_BODY_LIMIT is currently 160mb by default; Nginx client_max_body_size must be equal or slightly higher.
Proxy correctnessapp.set('trust proxy', 1) is already configured in Express, so Nginx must send X-Forwarded-* headers.

21.5 Production Environment Matrix

Required runtime variables grouped by operational concern.

Production startup should fail when critical security settings are missing. The current security config already validates production notification providers, CORS, Redis, Turnstile, registry providers, Mailboxlayer, and signing secret presence.

ConcernVariablesProduction rule
Runtime modeNODE_ENV, APP_ENV, PORT, APP_PUBLIC_URLSet production mode explicitly and expose only Nginx, not the Node port.
DatabaseDATABASE_URL, DIRECT_URLUse least-privilege application credentials and migration credentials only during deploy.
SecurityCORS_ORIGINS, TURNSTILE_SECRET_KEY, SIGNATURE_HASH_SECRET, AUTH_RATE_LIMIT_*Use exact public origins, no wildcard CORS, and strong rotated signing secrets.
StorageS3_ENDPOINT, S3_REGION, S3_ACCESS_KEY_ID, S3_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, S3_DOCUMENT_BUCKETUse private buckets, server-side encryption where available, and lifecycle policies.
ProvidersRESEND_*, BEEM_*, BRIQ_*, TRA_*, BRELA_*Use production credentials only in secret storage; never put values in repo docs.

21.6 Server Deployment Flow

Step-by-step production release process.

The current GitHub workflow validates, builds, can run Prisma migrations, and can call a deploy webhook. In the VM model, that webhook should trigger a deploy receiver or guarded SSH job that pulls the exact SHA, builds or downloads artifacts, updates containers, and performs smoke checks.

1. Merge to main or run workflow_dispatch.
2. GitHub Actions runs npm ci, Prisma validate, tests, and build.
3. Deploy job runs prisma migrate deploy when PRODUCTION_DATABASE_URL exists.
4. Deploy job posts SHA/ref/workflow to DEPLOY_WEBHOOK_URL.
5. VM deploy hook fetches the SHA into /opt/procurex/releases/<sha>.
6. VM builds or loads Docker images and updates the current symlink.
7. docker compose up -d --remove-orphans restarts services.
8. Smoke checks call /health and selected user/API flows.
9. Release is recorded with SHA, migration version, operator, and smoke status.
Migration placementRun Prisma migrations before traffic switch for backward-compatible changes; use a maintenance window for destructive or long-running migrations.
Smoke checks/health, static index load, sign-in page load, and at least one authenticated admin or dashboard API check.
Rollback triggerRollback immediately on failed health, failed smoke checks, elevated 5xx rate, failed migration verification, or broken document upload/download.

21.7 Scaling From Single VM To Multi-VM

Growth path without changing the application architecture.

Start with one VM for a pilot only. Move stateful services off the app VM, then add API replicas behind Nginx or a load balancer. React assets remain static and cacheable; the API must stay stateless except for PostgreSQL, Redis, search, and object storage.

PilotOne app VM, local Docker network, local PostgreSQL/Redis/Elasticsearch/MinIO, daily backups, strict firewall.
Early productionSeparate database/storage VM or managed equivalents, app VM runs only Nginx/API, backups copied off-host.
Scale-outTwo or more API VMs behind a load balancer, shared Redis, shared object storage, PostgreSQL primary with replicas.
Bottleneck rulesScale PostgreSQL IOPS and connection pooling before adding many API replicas; scale object storage bandwidth for bid-deadline upload peaks.

21.8 Rollback Runbook

Operational rollback decision tree.

Rollback must restore the previous application release quickly while respecting database migration compatibility. Every migration should be reviewed as backward-compatible, forward-only, or maintenance-window-only before release.

Application-only rollback:
  ln -sfn /opt/procurex/releases/<previous-sha> /opt/procurex/current
  docker compose up -d --build api nginx
  curl -fsS https://procurex.example.tld/health

Migration-aware rollback:
  1. Stop writes if data corruption is suspected.
  2. Do not run ad hoc down migrations in production.
  3. Restore from verified backup only when data state is unsafe.
  4. Record incident timeline and affected records.
Fast rollback targetApplication release rollback under 15 minutes for compatible failures.
Data rollback targetRestore from backup only after incident owner approves impact and data-loss window.
EvidenceKeep workflow run URL, release SHA, migration IDs, logs, and smoke check output with the incident record.

22. CI/CD Pipeline Deep Dive

This chapter documents the existing GitHub Actions workflow and the additional production release controls needed around build, test, lint, migration, deploy, rollback, and supply-chain safety.

These pages are production operating design for ProcureX unless an item explicitly names repository evidence as already implemented. The hosting baseline is a VM or VPS estate running Docker services behind Nginx with Let's Encrypt TLS.

22.1 Current GitHub Actions Workflow

What the repository already validates.

The workflow named ProcureX CI/CD runs on pushes to main and develop, pull requests, and manual dispatch. It uses Node 20, installs with npm ci, validates Prisma, runs tests, builds the platform, uploads artifacts, and conditionally deploys on main or manual runs.

StepRoleProduction expectation
Check out repositoryWorkflow setupMust be visible in the release audit trail.
Set up Node.jsWorkflow setupMust be visible in the release audit trail.
Install dependenciesWorkflow setupMust be visible in the release audit trail.
Validate Prisma schemaQuality gateMust be visible in the release audit trail.
Lint clientQuality gateCurrently non-blocking; make blocking before hard production launch.
Test shared packageQuality gateMust be visible in the release audit trail.
Test server and clientQuality gateMust be visible in the release audit trail.
Build platformQuality gateMust be visible in the release audit trail.
Upload client buildQuality gateMust be visible in the release audit trail.
Upload server buildQuality gateMust be visible in the release audit trail.
Check out repositoryWorkflow setupMust be visible in the release audit trail.
Set up Node.jsWorkflow setupMust be visible in the release audit trail.
Install dependenciesWorkflow setupMust be visible in the release audit trail.
Build releaseQuality gateMust be visible in the release audit trail.
Deploy database migrationsRelease triggerMust be visible in the release audit trail.
Trigger deployment webhookRelease triggerMust be visible in the release audit trail.
Report skipped deployment targetRelease triggerMust be visible in the release audit trail.
Workflow file.github/workflows/procurex-ci-cd.yml
Artifact outputsprocurex-client-dist and procurex-server-dist
Deployment triggerDEPLOY_WEBHOOK_URL receives SHA/ref/workflow payload when configured.

22.2 Build, Test, Lint, And Migration Gates

Minimum gates before a release reaches production.

CI should separate quick feedback from production gates, but production deployment must require all critical gates to pass. The current lint step is continue-on-error, which is acceptable during transition but should become blocking before launch.

Install gatenpm ci using the root lockfile to guarantee reproducible workspace dependencies.
Schema gatenpm run db:validate catches Prisma schema errors before build or deploy.
Testsnpm --workspace shared test and npm test cover shared contracts, server modules, and client behavior.
Lintnpm run lint:client should become blocking for production readiness.
Buildnpm run build compiles shared, server, and client outputs.
Migration deploynpx prisma migrate deploy --schema prisma/schema.prisma runs only when production DB secret exists.

22.3 Deployment Stages

Promotion model from code review to production.

Use environment promotion even in a VM deployment. The same artifact SHA should move from validation to staging to production. Production deployment requires GitHub Environment protection, reviewer approval, and a release note that names migration risk.

Pull request: install -> schema validate -> tests -> build
Develop/staging: deploy to staging VM -> migration dry run where possible -> smoke tests
Main/production: protected approval -> migrate deploy -> webhook deploy -> production smoke checks
Post-release: monitor dashboards, alert channel, and support queue for at least one business cycle
Staging dataUse masked or synthetic data; never copy live tender documents into a loose staging VM.
Production approvalRequire one engineering approver and one product/operations approver for migration-bearing releases.
Audit recordRecord SHA, workflow URL, deploy time, migration IDs, and smoke result.

22.4 CI/CD Rollback Process

How the pipeline supports recovery.

The pipeline should keep artifacts and deployment metadata long enough to redeploy the previous known-good SHA. The VM deploy hook should understand a rollback payload or accept an operator-specified SHA through a protected manual workflow.

Rollback commandManual workflow dispatch with target SHA, environment, reason, and approval.
Artifact policyRetain successful build artifacts and Docker image tags for at least 30 days, longer during launch.
Migration policyApplication rollback is allowed only when DB migrations are backward-compatible; otherwise use hotfix-forward or restore procedure.
VerificationRollback is complete only after health, static app, authenticated API, and error-rate checks pass.

22.5 Supply Chain And Secret Safety

Controls around dependencies and secrets.

ProcureX handles procurement and bid data, so CI/CD must treat dependency and secret risk as production risk. Add secret scanning, dependency audit review, artifact provenance, and restricted environment secrets before launch.

SecretsUse GitHub Environments or host secret storage; never print production env values in logs.
DependenciesReview npm audit output and pin major upgrades deliberately.
ImagesScan API/Nginx images before production deploy once production Dockerfiles exist.
PermissionsUse least-privilege GitHub token permissions and protect production environment secrets.

23. Monitoring And Observability Deep Dive

This chapter defines logs, error tracking, metrics, uptime checks, alerting, and admin/system health dashboards for operating ProcureX safely in production.

These pages are production operating design for ProcureX unless an item explicitly names repository evidence as already implemented. The hosting baseline is a VM or VPS estate running Docker services behind Nginx with Let's Encrypt TLS.

23.1 Application Logs

Log shape, retention, and sensitive-data rules.

Production logs should be structured JSON from the API container and Nginx access/error logs from the host. Logs must carry request ID, method, route, status, latency, user/organization identifiers where safe, and error codes, but never passwords, OTPs, signing keyphrases, bid payload bodies, uploaded document contents, or provider secrets.

API logsRequest start/end, controller errors, provider failures, migration/deploy status, and audit-relevant workflow transitions.
Nginx logsRemote address, request path, status, upstream latency, body size, and user agent for edge diagnostics.
RetentionOperational logs 30-90 days; security/audit logs follow audit retention rules.
RedactionRedact Authorization, cookies, OTPs, token-like strings, file contents, and personally sensitive payload fields.

23.2 Error Tracking

Server and client exception visibility.

Use an error tracker such as Sentry, GlitchTip, or OpenTelemetry-backed exception collection. The client captures render and route errors; the API captures unhandled exceptions, normalized ApiErrorResponse metadata, dependency failures, and slow requests.

Grouping keysError code, route, controller handler, release SHA, environment, and module key.
SeverityCritical for failed bid submission/opening, document corruption, auth bypass suspicion, database unavailable, and payment-state corruption.
PrivacySend record IDs and opaque references, not full bid contents or document bodies.
Release correlationEvery error event includes release SHA from deploy metadata.

23.3 Metrics And SLOs

Signals needed to operate procurement workflows.

Metrics should cover the edge, API, database, Redis, Elasticsearch, object storage, providers, and business workflows. Business metrics matter because a technically healthy server can still be failing procurement users if bids cannot be submitted or documents cannot be opened.

SignalTargetAlert threshold
API availability99.5% pilot, 99.9% mature production5xx rate above 2% for 5 minutes
API latencyp95 below 800ms for normal JSON endpointsp95 above 2s for 10 minutes
Document uploadSuccessful 25MB upload within agreed network budgetFailure rate above 5% or sustained 413/415 spike
DatabaseLow lock waits, healthy connections, backup age currentConnection saturation, replication lag, or backup older than 24h
Identity deliveryOTP/email delivery within provider SLAProvider failure rate above 5% for 10 minutes

23.4 Uptime Checks And Alerting

External checks and escalation behavior.

External checks should hit the public site, the API health endpoint, sign-in route, and selected lightweight authenticated flows from more than one region. Alerts go to the on-call channel and create an incident ticket when sustained.

ChecksGET /, GET /health, GET /api/public, static asset fetch, and synthetic sign-in/dashboard smoke where safe.
Alert routingCritical to on-call immediately; warning to engineering channel; business workflow alerts to operations lead.
Noise controlPage on sustained errors, not single transient failures; include runbook link in alert payload.
MaintenanceMute planned maintenance windows only after stakeholder notice and rollback plan approval.

23.5 Admin/System Health Dashboards

Operational views inside and outside ProcureX.

ProcureX already has admin analytics and dashboard modules. A production health dashboard should add operational state: dependency status, version, migration level, background jobs, provider health, backup age, object-storage status, and recent incidents.

External dashboardGrafana/Prometheus or hosted equivalent for infrastructure and API metrics.
Internal admin dashboardRead-only system health page for trusted admins, showing high-level status without leaking secrets.
Health fieldsRelease SHA, app uptime, DB connectivity, Redis status, storage write/read check, search health, provider health, backup freshness.
AuditAdmin health dashboard access should be logged if it exposes operational metadata.

24. Backup And Disaster Recovery Deep Dive

This chapter defines PostgreSQL backups, object storage backups, restore procedure, recovery objectives, and expected behavior during database or storage failures.

These pages are production operating design for ProcureX unless an item explicitly names repository evidence as already implemented. The hosting baseline is a VM or VPS estate running Docker services behind Nginx with Let's Encrypt TLS.

24.1 PostgreSQL Backup Plan

Database backup strategy for procurement records.

PostgreSQL is the source of truth for identity, organizations, tenders, bids, evaluation, contracts, records, audit, and document metadata. Use daily full logical backups for portability plus WAL/PITR or frequent physical backups for recovery precision.

Backup cadenceDaily full backup, WAL archiving or 15-minute incremental/PITR equivalent, and pre-deploy snapshot before migration-bearing releases.
Retention7 daily, 4 weekly, 12 monthly backups minimum; adjust for legal retention and storage cost.
EncryptionEncrypt backups at rest and in transit; store credentials separately from backup archives.
VerificationAutomated restore drill at least monthly during early production, quarterly after stabilization.
pg_dump --format=custom --no-owner --no-acl "$DATABASE_URL" > procurex-$(date +%F-%H%M).dump
pg_restore --clean --if-exists --dbname "$RESTORE_DATABASE_URL" procurex-<timestamp>.dump

24.2 Object Storage Backup Plan

Protecting uploaded documents and generated official files.

Object storage contains bid documents, tender documents, contract evidence, profile images, official generated PDFs, and communication attachments. Metadata in PostgreSQL is not enough; object bytes must be versioned and replicated or backed up independently.

VersioningEnable bucket versioning where supported to survive accidental overwrite/delete.
LifecycleMove older versions to cheaper storage after the legal active period; do not delete records under legal hold.
ReplicationCopy MinIO/S3 buckets to an off-host bucket or secondary storage location at least daily.
IntegrityCompare object counts, object keys, sizes, and stored SHA-256 checksums against DocumentObject metadata.

24.3 Restore Procedure

How to recover database and storage together.

Restore must keep PostgreSQL metadata and object storage bytes synchronized. A database restored to 10:00 with object storage restored to 09:00 can create missing-document failures; object restore must cover at least the same time horizon as the database restore.

1. Declare incident and freeze writes if corruption or destructive delete is suspected.
2. Pick restore point and confirm business impact.
3. Provision isolated restore environment.
4. Restore PostgreSQL backup and run Prisma validation/smoke queries.
5. Restore object bucket prefix/version set matching the database restore point.
6. Run document integrity check: metadata rows -> object exists -> checksum matches.
7. Promote restored environment or export corrected records.
8. Record RPO/RTO achieved and follow-up fixes.
RPO targetPilot: 24 hours for low-criticality data; 15-60 minutes for production procurement windows.
RTO targetPilot: 4-8 hours; mature production: 1-2 hours for core workflows.
Restore ownerIncident commander approves restore; database owner executes; application owner validates flows.

24.4 Database Or Storage Failure Behavior

What operators and users should expect during failures.

When PostgreSQL fails, the API should return controlled 5xx responses, Nginx should keep serving static assets, alerts should fire, and writes must stop. When object storage fails, metadata-only pages may load, but uploads/downloads must fail safely and show clear user-facing errors.

Database downDisable writes, alert critical, preserve logs, restore service or fail over, run data consistency checks before reopening.
Storage downStop upload/download workflows, keep tender metadata visible when possible, queue no uploads unless durable retry exists.
Redis downAuth rate limiting loses shared backend; treat as degraded security and restore quickly.
Search downFallback to database search where implemented or mark admin search degraded; do not block core bid submission.

25. Accessibility Deep Dive

This chapter documents WCAG compliance, keyboard navigation, screen-reader support, color contrast, and form accessibility expectations across ProcureX workflows.

These pages are production operating design for ProcureX unless an item explicitly names repository evidence as already implemented. The hosting baseline is a VM or VPS estate running Docker services behind Nginx with Let's Encrypt TLS.

25.1 WCAG Compliance Policy

Accessibility target and review cadence.

ProcureX should target WCAG 2.2 AA for public pages, authentication, procurement workflows, bidding, evaluation, admin dashboards, and document-heavy forms. Procurement users may be under deadline pressure, so accessibility bugs can become business-critical defects.

StandardWCAG 2.2 AA as the acceptance baseline.
CoveragePublic pages, auth, marketplace, tender creation, bidding workspace, evaluation, awards/contracts, admin, records, support.
Review cadenceRun automated accessibility checks in CI where practical and manual keyboard/screen-reader passes before major releases.
Defect severityKeyboard trap, invisible focus, inaccessible bid submission, inaccessible required forms, and unreadable errors are release blockers.

25.2 Keyboard Navigation

Required keyboard behavior for all workflows.

Every workflow must be usable without a mouse. This includes app navigation, tabs, data tables, menus, dialogs, upload controls, date pickers, filters, multi-step tender creation, bid submission, and admin investigations.

Focus orderMatches visual order and workflow priority.
Focus visibilityVisible focus indicator on buttons, links, inputs, tabs, menus, rows, and custom controls.
DialogsFocus moves into modal, Escape closes when safe, focus returns to opener.
TablesRows and row actions are reachable with keyboard; bulk actions expose labels and status.

25.3 Screen Reader Support

Names, roles, states, and live status.

Screen-reader users need accurate page headings, labels, error associations, status announcements, and route changes. Icons without text must have accessible labels or be hidden when decorative.

FormsEvery input has a programmatic label, help text, error text, and required-state indication.
Dynamic updatesUse polite live regions for save/upload status and assertive regions for blocking errors.
NavigationUse landmarks for header, nav, main, aside, and footer.
DocumentsGenerated PDFs and official documents should be reviewed for readable text and meaningful structure where possible.

25.4 Color Contrast And Form Accessibility

Visual readability and high-stakes form behavior.

Color cannot be the only signal for status, risk, compliance, bid validity, or form errors. Procurement forms must keep labels visible, preserve entered values after validation failures, and expose clear corrective guidance.

ContrastNormal text at least 4.5:1, large text at least 3:1, non-text controls at least 3:1.
Error handlingError summary at top of forms plus field-level messages connected by aria-describedby.
Status badgesUse icon/text/shape as well as color for pending, approved, rejected, overdue, risk, and sealed states.
UploadsAnnounce selected file, upload progress, rejected type/size, checksum/receipt status, and retry outcome.

26. Threat Model Deep Dive

This chapter identifies actors, assets, attack surfaces, procurement-specific risks, and mitigations mapped to security controls and verification evidence.

These pages are production operating design for ProcureX unless an item explicitly names repository evidence as already implemented. The hosting baseline is a VM or VPS estate running Docker services behind Nginx with Let's Encrypt TLS.

26.1 Actors, Assets, And Trust Boundaries

What the threat model protects.

ProcureX protects procurement confidentiality, bid integrity, organization identity, auditability, official documents, communication records, and payment/contract workflows. The main trust boundary is between public browser traffic, authenticated users, API services, private data stores, and external providers.

ActorsAnonymous visitors, suppliers, buyers, evaluators, approvers, admins, support operators, external providers, malicious insiders, compromised accounts, infrastructure attackers.
AssetsCredentials, sessions, OTPs, bids, bid documents, tender records, evaluation scores, award decisions, contracts, invoices, audit logs, signatures, object storage.
Attack surfacesPublic auth endpoints, document uploads, admin APIs, bid submission/opening, provider callbacks, CI/CD, Nginx, database, object storage, search, Redis.
Repository controlsHelmet/CSP, CORS allowlist, Turnstile, auth rate limiting, validation, audit models, Prisma schema indexes, RLS verification foundations.

26.2 Procurement-Specific Risk Map

Risks mapped to controls and residual work.

RiskImpactControlsResidual work
Account takeoverFraudulent bids, approvals, or document access.Turnstile, rate limiting, OTP flows, session lookup, admin verification, production provider checks.MFA enrollment/challenge and admin-required MFA before launch.
Bid leakageUnfair competition and legal exposure.Authz checks, optional bid encryption, document metadata, audit events, sealed hashes.Mandatory encryption and formal bid opening ceremony.
Document tamperingChanged tender/bid/contract evidence.SHA-256 checksums, signature workflows, version models, audit trails.Generic document versioning, malware scanning, retention/hold states.
Admin abuseUndetected data exposure or workflow manipulation.Admin routes, compliance audit pages, action recording.Segregation of duties, high-risk action approval, immutable audit export.
CI/CD compromiseMalicious release or leaked secrets.GitHub workflow gates and environment secrets.Secret scanning, image scanning, protected deploy approvals, provenance.

26.3 Document Upload Threats

Malicious files, oversized payloads, and storage misuse.

Document upload is one of the highest-risk surfaces because it accepts untrusted bytes. The bid upload service already validates file type, size, magic bytes, metadata, storage, and checksum behavior. Production must generalize those controls and add malware scanning and quarantine states.

ThreatsMalware, polyglot files, content-type spoofing, oversized uploads, path traversal, duplicate tampering, forbidden disclosure.
ControlsNginx body limit, Express request limit, Busboy parsing, extension/MIME/magic checks, SHA-256, S3/private storage, authorization checks.
NeededVirus scanning, quarantine, presigned URL discipline, document-wide audit, object lifecycle, legal hold, content security for downloads.

26.4 Control Verification Matrix

How to prove controls remain working.

Every threat control needs evidence. For ProcureX, evidence should include automated tests, RLS verification, workflow screenshots where useful, audit records, deployment logs, backup restore logs, and incident exercises.

Auth controlsIdentity tests, rate limit tests, production config startup failure tests.
Data accessnpm --workspace server run verify:rls plus module service authorization tests.
DocumentsUpload tests for allowed/blocked types, size limits, checksum persistence, and denied access.
OperationsCI run logs, release smoke checks, backup restore reports, alert drill reports.

27. Performance And Load Testing Deep Dive

This chapter defines expected users, API load, large document upload behavior, database/index performance, and frontend bundle performance checks.

These pages are production operating design for ProcureX unless an item explicitly names repository evidence as already implemented. The hosting baseline is a VM or VPS estate running Docker services behind Nginx with Let's Encrypt TLS.

27.1 Expected Users And Workload

Initial load model for planning tests.

Performance targets should be based on procurement calendar peaks, not average daily traffic. Bid deadlines, evaluation meetings, contract signing, and admin audits create bursty workloads.

Pilot baseline100-500 registered users, 20-50 concurrent active users, 5-20 active tenders, 25MB document uploads.
Medium baseline2,000-10,000 registered users, 200 concurrent users, hundreds of tenders, heavy dashboard/search use.
Peak scenariosSupplier uploads in the final hour before closing, buyer publishes amendments, evaluators score simultaneously, admins export audit records.
Targetsp95 API below 800ms for normal endpoints, clear progress for uploads, zero data loss, controlled backpressure under saturation.

27.2 API Load Expectations

Scenario-based API tests.

Load tests should exercise workflows: registration/sign-in, marketplace search, tender detail, bid draft/save/upload/submit, evaluation workspace, award recommendation, contract workspace, support ticket, and admin search. Single endpoint tests are useful only as baselines.

Toolingk6, Artillery, or Playwright-driven API flows with seeded data.
MetricsThroughput, p50/p95/p99 latency, 4xx/5xx rate, DB query time, memory, CPU, connection count.
AcceptanceNo uncontrolled 500s, no duplicate submitted bids, no partial document metadata, no migration-time timeouts.
EvidenceStore test scripts, data seed version, release SHA, metrics screenshot/export, and bottleneck notes.

27.3 Large Document Upload Behavior

Uploads under deadline pressure.

The production upload path must handle large bid and contract documents without blocking unrelated API traffic. The current environment includes 25MB document limits for several document types and a larger JSON body limit; Nginx, Express, Busboy, S3, and client progress UI must agree.

LimitsDocument max bytes from env; Nginx limit slightly higher; user-facing copy shows exact allowed file types and sizes.
BehaviorStreaming upload where available, checksum after write, metadata transaction, cleanup on failure.
Failure handlingReject too-large and unsupported files before business workflow state changes.
Test casesAllowed PDF, rejected executable, spoofed MIME, max-size boundary, interrupted upload, concurrent deadline uploads.

27.4 Database Indexing And Query Performance

PostgreSQL performance controls.

The Prisma schema currently defines 174 models, 335 explicit indexes, and 44 explicit unique constraints. Production query tuning should focus on organization-scoped lists, deadline/status filters, bid/evaluation joins, audit/event search, and contract dashboards.

Review methodEXPLAIN ANALYZE for slow queries, pg_stat_statements review, index hit ratio, lock wait review.
