Hot pathsMarketplace tender lists, my tenders, bid drafts/submissions, evaluation workspace, award contracts, admin search/audit, dashboards.
Connection managementLimit Prisma connection pool per API replica and size PostgreSQL max connections for peak replicas plus maintenance.
MaintenanceVacuum/autovacuum visibility, slow query threshold, bloat review, migration impact review.

27.5 Frontend Bundle Performance

React/Vite performance targets.

The client is React/Vite/MUI with many workflow pages and charts. Production should keep the initial route responsive, cache hashed assets aggressively, and consider route-level splitting for heavy admin, evaluation, and document-generation surfaces.

Build evidencenpm --workspace client run build creates Vite assets in client/dist.
TargetsFast first render on public pages, acceptable dashboard hydration, no layout shifts that hide form controls.
ChecksBundle size review, Lighthouse/Web Vitals, route smoke screenshots, slow-network test for sign-in and bid workspace.
CachingImmutable cache for hashed assets; short cache for index.html to make deployments visible quickly.

28. API Documentation Deep Dive

This chapter provides OpenAPI-style endpoint documentation generated from the current Express route files, including method, path, handler, auth expectation, request/response shape, and likely error responses.

These pages are production operating design for ProcureX unless an item explicitly names repository evidence as already implemented. The hosting baseline is a VM or VPS estate running Docker services behind Nginx with Let's Encrypt TLS.

28.1 API Documentation Overview

How to read the endpoint catalog.

The API catalog below is generated from the current Express route files. It found 445 endpoints across 18 modules. The documentation is OpenAPI-style guidance inside this report, not a generated machine-readable OpenAPI document.

ModuleMountEndpointsRoute file
award-contract/api/award-contract91procurement-platform/server/src/modules/award-contract/routes.ts
bidding/api/bidding14procurement-platform/server/src/modules/bidding/routes.ts
communication/api/communication12procurement-platform/server/src/modules/communication/routes.ts
compliance-admin/api/compliance-admin46procurement-platform/server/src/modules/compliance-admin/routes.ts
dashboard/api/dashboard3procurement-platform/server/src/modules/dashboard/routes.ts
documents/api/documents12procurement-platform/server/src/modules/documents/routes.ts
evaluation/api/evaluation7procurement-platform/server/src/modules/evaluation/routes.ts
financial/api/financial1procurement-platform/server/src/modules/financial/routes.ts
help-centre/api/help-centre7procurement-platform/server/src/modules/help-centre/routes.ts
identity/api/identity35procurement-platform/server/src/modules/identity/routes.ts
integration/api/integration1procurement-platform/server/src/modules/integration/routes.ts
intelligence/api/intelligence4procurement-platform/server/src/modules/intelligence/routes.ts
organization/api/organization1procurement-platform/server/src/modules/organization/routes.ts
post-award/api/post-award148procurement-platform/server/src/modules/post-award/routes.ts
procurement/api/procurement44procurement-platform/server/src/modules/procurement/routes.ts
public/api/public3procurement-platform/server/src/modules/public/routes.ts
records/api/records8procurement-platform/server/src/modules/records/routes.ts
support/api/support8procurement-platform/server/src/modules/support/routes.ts
Global healthGET /health returns server status and registered module keys/base paths.
Global middlewareHelmet CSP, CORS allowlist, JSON body limit, trust proxy, Zod error normalization, and shared ApiErrorResponse behavior.
Auth languageRows describe expected auth posture; exact authorization is enforced in controllers/services and should be tested per workflow.
Error shapeApiErrorResponse is produced through shared API error helpers for validation, permission, not-found, and workflow errors.

28.2 award-contract API Endpoints

OpenAPI-style endpoint catalog for award-contract.

This module exposes 91 routes mounted at /api/award-contract from procurement-platform/server/src/modules/award-contract/routes.ts. The table documents auth expectations and request/response shape per endpoint; controller/service code remains the source of truth for exact DTO fields.

MethodPathHandlerAuthRequestResponseErrors
GET/api/award-contractstatusAuthenticated endpoint; enforce organization, capability, workflow, and ownership rules in service layer.Path/query parameters only; no JSON body.200 JSON DTO/list/dashboard/file/status response.400 validation failure, 401 missing or expired session, 403 permission or tenant denial
GET/api/award-contract/contractslistContractsAuthenticated endpoint; enforce organization, capability, workflow, and ownership rules in service layer.Path/query parameters only; no JSON body.200 JSON DTO/list/dashboard/file/status response.400 validation failure, 401 missing or expired session, 403 permission or tenant denial
GET/api/award-contract/contracts/:idcontractAuthenticated endpoint; enforce organization, capability, workflow, and ownership rules in service layer.Path/query parameters only; no JSON body.200 JSON DTO/list/dashboard/file/status response.400 validation failure, 401 missing or expired session, 403 permission or tenant denial, 404 record not found
POST/api/award-contract/contracts/:id/acceptancescreateAcceptanceAuthenticated endpoint; enforce organization, capability, workflow, and ownership rules in service layer.JSON body validated by controller/Zod schema.201 created DTO, command result, or workflow transition.400 validation failure, 401 missing or expired session, 403 permission or tenant denial, 404 record not found, 409 workflow conflict
POST/api/award-contract/contracts/:id/change-requestscreateContractChangeRequestAuthenticated endpoint; enforce organization, capability, workflow, and ownership rules in service layer.JSON body validated by controller/Zod schema.201 created DTO, command result, or workflow transition.400 validation failure, 401 missing or expired session, 403 permission or tenant denial, 404 record not found, 409 workflow conflict
PUT/api/award-contract/contracts/:id/clausesupsertClauseAuthenticated endpoint; enforce organization, capability, workflow, and ownership rules in service layer.Full replacement/upsert JSON body validated by module schema.200 upserted/replaced DTO.400 validation failure, 401 missing or expired session, 403 permission or tenant denial, 404 record not found, 409 workflow conflict
DELETE/api/award-contract/contracts/:id/clauses/:itemIddeleteClauseAuthenticated endpoint; enforce organization, capability, workflow, and ownership rules in service layer.Path parameters, optional deletion reason only if controller validates it.200/204 delete or workflow result.400 validation failure, 401 missing or expired session, 403 permission or tenant denial, 404 record not found

Representative Requests

GET /api/award-contract
Authorization: Bearer <token when required>
Accept: application/json

Response: module DTO or structured ApiErrorResponse
GET /api/award-contract/contracts
Authorization: Bearer <token when required>
Accept: application/json

Response: module DTO or structured ApiErrorResponse
GET /api/award-contract/contracts/:id
Authorization: Bearer <token when required>
Accept: application/json

Response: module DTO or structured ApiErrorResponse

Production OpenAPI generation should eventually be derived from route definitions plus Zod schemas so the formal spec cannot drift from implementation.

28.3 bidding API Endpoints

OpenAPI-style endpoint catalog for bidding.

This module exposes 14 routes mounted at /api/bidding from procurement-platform/server/src/modules/bidding/routes.ts. The table documents auth expectations and request/response shape per endpoint; controller/service code remains the source of truth for exact DTO fields.

MethodPathHandlerAuthRequestResponseErrors
GET/api/biddingstatusAuthenticated endpoint; enforce organization, capability, workflow, and ownership rules in service layer.Path/query parameters only; no JSON body.200 JSON DTO/list/dashboard/file/status response.400 validation failure, 401 missing or expired session, 403 permission or tenant denial
GET/api/bidding/:bidIdgetBidAuthenticated endpoint; enforce organization, capability, workflow, and ownership rules in service layer.Path/query parameters only; no JSON body.200 JSON DTO/list/dashboard/file/status response.400 validation failure, 401 missing or expired session, 403 permission or tenant denial, 404 record not found

OpenAPI-style endpoint catalog for procurement.

Authorization: Bearer <token when required>
Accept: application/json

Response: module DTO or structured ApiErrorResponse

Production OpenAPI generation should eventually be derived from route definitions plus Zod schemas so the formal spec cannot drift from implementation.

28.18 records API Endpoints

OpenAPI-style endpoint catalog for records.

This module exposes 8 routes mounted at /api/records from procurement-platform/server/src/modules/records/routes.ts. The table documents auth expectations and request/response shape per endpoint; controller/service code remains the source of truth for exact DTO fields.

MethodPathHandlerAuthRequestResponseErrors
GET/api/recordsrecordsAuthenticated endpoint; enforce organization, capability, workflow, and ownership rules in service layer.Path/query parameters only; no JSON body.200 JSON DTO/list/dashboard/file/status response.400 validation failure, 401 missing or expired session, 403 permission or tenant denial
GET/api/records/:iddetailAuthenticated endpoint; enforce organization, capability, workflow, and ownership rules in service layer.Path/query parameters only; no JSON body.200 JSON DTO/list/dashboard/file/status response.400 validation failure, 401 missing or expired session, 403 permission or tenant denial, 404 record not found
GET/api/records/chartschartsAuthenticated endpoint; enforce organization, capability, workflow, and ownership rules in service layer.Path/query parameters only; no JSON body.200 JSON DTO/list/dashboard/file/status response.400 validation failure, 401 missing or expired session, 403 permission or tenant denial

GET/api/records/export/csvexportCsvAuthenticated endpoint; enforce organization, capability, workflow, and ownership rules in service layer.Path/query parameters only; no JSON body.200 JSON DTO/list/dashboard/file/status response.400 validation failure, 401 missing or expired session, 403 permission or tenant denial
GET/api/records/export/pdfexportPdfAuthenticated endpoint; enforce organization, capability, workflow, and ownership rules in service layer.Path/query parameters only; no JSON body.200 JSON DTO/list/dashboard/file/status response.400 validation failure, 401 missing or expired session, 403 permission or tenant denial

29. Data Retention And Privacy Policy Implementation Deep Dive

This chapter explains retention, deletion, anonymization, audit log retention, user export/removal, legal/privacy implementation, and data dictionary classification.

These pages are production operating design for ProcureX unless an item explicitly names repository evidence as already implemented. The hosting baseline is a VM or VPS estate running Docker services behind Nginx with Let's Encrypt TLS.

29.1 Record Retention Policy

How long procurement records are kept.

ProcureX records are procurement, legal, and audit evidence. Default behavior should preserve tender, bid, evaluation, award, contract, financial, communication, and admin audit records for the statutory period required by the operating jurisdiction and organizational policy.

Procurement recordsRetain active lifecycle records for the full procurement/contract period plus legal retention period.
Audit logsRetain security and administrative audit events in immutable or append-only storage for at least the legal audit window.
Support/contactRetain support tickets long enough for service quality, dispute investigation, and privacy obligations.
BackupsBackup retention must not silently extend deleted personal data beyond documented legal and operational windows.

29.2 Deletion And Anonymization Rules

Handling user removal without destroying procurement evidence.

Procurement evidence often cannot be hard-deleted on user request because bids, awards, contracts, signatures, and audit events must remain legally verifiable. The platform should separate personal profile data that can be deleted/anonymized from official records that must be retained.

User profileAnonymize display fields and contact fields where legal basis no longer applies.
Official actionsRetain signed actions, audit references, organization references, and document checksums as legal evidence.
DocumentsDelete only where no retention, contract, dispute, or legal-hold obligation exists. Otherwise restrict access and mark retained basis.
Search indexesPropagate deletion/anonymization to Elasticsearch indexes and cached exports.

29.3 User Data Export And Removal Workflow

Operational privacy request handling.

Privacy requests should be handled through an auditable admin workflow. Operators verify identity, scope the request, export eligible data, identify retained records, apply anonymization/removal, and record the legal basis for any refusal or partial action.

1. Receive privacy request and verify requester identity.
2. Identify user, accounts, organizations, documents, support tickets, audit actions, and communication records.
3. Export eligible personal data in a structured archive.
4. Classify records: delete, anonymize, retain by legal basis, or legal hold.
5. Apply changes transactionally where possible.
6. Reindex search and purge caches.
7. Notify requester and store audit record.
Export formatJSON/CSV archive plus document list; include explanations for retained procurement evidence.
Admin auditEvery export/removal action records actor, timestamp, legal basis, affected models, and outcome.
VerificationPost-action check confirms profile, search, and document access state.

29.4 Data Dictionary And Privacy Classification

Model families and sensitivity.

The Prisma schema contains 174 models. The report should treat the database as grouped data families so privacy and retention are understandable to assessors.

Data familyRepresentative modelsPrivacy handling
IdentityUser, Account, Session, MfaFactor, IdentityChallenge, VerificationProfileHigh sensitivity; export/anonymize where possible; protect credentials and sessions.
OrganizationOrganization, OrganizationMember, BuyerProfile, SupplierProfileBusiness data; retain active legal/procurement records.
Procurement and biddingTender, Bid, BidVersion, BidDocument, BidReceiptOfficial procurement evidence; deletion is restricted.
Evaluation and awardEvaluationWorkspace, EvaluationScore, AwardRecommendation, AwardNoticeHighly sensitive before disclosure; retain for audit and dispute handling.
Contracts and financeContract, ContractSignature, ContractPayment, InvoiceStatusLegal and financial records; strict retention and role-based access.
Documents and auditDocumentObject, SignedAction, AuditSeverityEvidence layer; checksum, access control, retention, and legal hold required.

30. Incident Response Deep Dive

This chapter defines what operators should do during breach, outage, failed payment, corrupted document, deployment, provider, and data-integrity incidents.

These pages are production operating design for ProcureX unless an item explicitly names repository evidence as already implemented. The hosting baseline is a VM or VPS estate running Docker services behind Nginx with Let's Encrypt TLS.

30.1 Incident Response Command Structure

Who does what during production incidents.

Every serious incident needs a named incident commander, technical lead, communications lead, and recorder. Procurement incidents often require legal/compliance participation because bid leakage, document tampering, and award disputes can have statutory consequences.

Incident commanderOwns severity, timeline, decisions, escalation, and closure.
Technical leadDiagnoses systems, coordinates fixes, validates recovery.
Comms leadUpdates stakeholders, support, users, and regulators where required.
RecorderMaintains timeline, commands run, evidence links, decisions, and postmortem actions.

30.2 Breach, Outage, Payment, And Document Playbooks

Scenario-specific response.

IncidentImmediate actionInvestigationRecovery
Breach/account takeoverRevoke sessions, freeze high-risk actions, rotate secrets if needed.Review auth logs, audit events, affected users, IP/user-agent patterns.Reset credentials, notify affected parties, add controls, postmortem.
OutageDeclare incident, check Nginx/API/DB/storage, update status.Correlate deploys, metrics, logs, infrastructure state.Rollback, restart, fail over, or restore from backup.
Failed paymentFreeze payment workflow state and preserve provider response.Compare internal payment state with provider/bank evidence.Correct state through audited admin action, never ad hoc SQL without approval.
Corrupted documentsStop document writes/downloads for affected scope.Compare checksums, object versions, metadata, audit events.Restore object version or backup, verify checksum, reopen workflow.

30.3 Admin Investigation Workflow

How platform admins investigate safely.

Admins should investigate through the application and read-only dashboards first. Direct database access is reserved for emergency diagnostics and must be logged with reason, operator, query summary, and approval.

1. Open incident record and assign owner.
2. Capture release SHA, affected tenant/org/user/tender/document IDs.
3. Review admin audit events and application logs.
4. Check object checksums and database row history where available.
5. Preserve evidence before corrective action.
6. Execute approved recovery action.
7. Record user impact and close with postmortem actions.
Evidence preservationDo not overwrite logs, documents, or rows before capturing identifiers and timestamps.
Access controlUse least privilege and time-bound emergency access.
CommunicationBusiness owners approve external communications for procurement-impacting incidents.

30.4 Postmortem And Corrective Action

Learning loop after recovery.

Postmortems should be blameless but precise. The goal is to reduce recurrence by improving tests, monitoring, rollback, docs, training, and product controls.

Required fieldsTimeline, impact, root cause, contributing factors, detection gap, recovery steps, RPO/RTO, action items.
Action trackingEvery action has owner, due date, severity, and verification method.
Review cadenceReview critical postmortems with engineering, operations, compliance, and product.

31. Cost And Infrastructure Sizing Deep Dive

This chapter provides practical sizing and cost planning for VM resources, database/storage growth, S3/MinIO, Elasticsearch, Redis, logs, monitoring, CI/CD, and providers.

These pages are production operating design for ProcureX unless an item explicitly names repository evidence as already implemented. The hosting baseline is a VM or VPS estate running Docker services behind Nginx with Let's Encrypt TLS.

31.1 Expected Server Resources

VM sizing for pilot and early production.

Sizing should start conservative and be adjusted from real metrics. The initial VM design separates app capacity from stateful data growth as soon as procurement volume increases.

TierAPI/Nginx VMDatabase/storageUse case
Pilot2-4 vCPU, 4-8GB RAM, 80GB SSDSame VM only for pilot, with off-host backupsDemo, UAT, limited production pilot.
Early production4 vCPU, 8-16GB RAM, 100GB SSDDedicated DB/storage VM or managed equivalentsDozens of organizations and active tenders.
Growth2+ API VMs, load balancer, shared Redis/search/storageDedicated PostgreSQL with backups/PITR and monitored IOPSHigh tender volume and deadline upload peaks.

31.2 Database And Storage Growth

Main cost drivers over time.

The largest long-term cost drivers are document bytes, backups, logs, audit retention, search indexes, and provider messages. Procurement records accumulate and are not freely deletable because of retention duties.

PostgreSQLRows grow from tenders, bids, evaluation scores, contracts, audit events, communications, and operational metadata.
Object storageBid attachments, tender documents, contract evidence, generated PDFs, communication attachments, profile images.
BackupsDatabase and object retention multiplies raw storage; versioning protects integrity but increases cost.
LogsStructured logs and Nginx logs need retention limits and archival strategy.

31.3 S3/MinIO, Redis, And Elasticsearch Sizing

Stateful service assumptions.

MinIO/S3Start with 100-500GB usable storage for pilot depending on expected document count; enable alerts at 70%, 85%, and 95%.
RedisSmall instance is enough for current rate limiting; reserve memory for future queues/cache and monitor evictions.
Elasticsearch512MB heap only suits local/pilot usage; production search needs dedicated memory, disk, snapshot, and index lifecycle planning.
PostgreSQLSize for IOPS and backups before raw CPU; dashboard/search/report queries can pressure disks.

31.4 Cost Review And Optimization

How to keep operating cost predictable.

Cost review should be monthly during the first production quarter, then quarterly. Reviews compare actual usage against tender volume, document growth, provider messages, CI minutes, and incident/backup storage.

Monthly reviewVM CPU/RAM/disk, DB size, object storage, backup size, logs, provider messages, CI/CD minutes.
OptimizationArchive old logs, tune indexes, lifecycle old object versions, clean failed uploads, right-size search heap.
Do not optimize awayBackups, audit logs, legal retention, TLS, monitoring, or security controls.

32. Visual Diagrams And Architecture Appendix

This chapter adds self-contained diagrams and sequence views that can be read directly in the standalone HTML report without external assets.

These pages are production operating design for ProcureX unless an item explicitly names repository evidence as already implemented. The hosting baseline is a VM or VPS estate running Docker services behind Nginx with Let's Encrypt TLS.

32.1 Production Deployment Diagram

Self-contained architecture diagram for VM/Docker/Nginx hosting.

Production Deployment

UserBrowser
DNS/TLSDomain records
NginxStatic React
DockerExpress API
DataPostgreSQL
UserMobile browser
DNS/TLSLet's Encrypt
Nginx/api proxy
DockerRelease SHA
DataRedis
DataElasticsearch
DataMinIO/S3

Nginx is the public boundary; Docker and data services stay private.

32.2 Security Architecture Diagram

Layered controls from browser to database.

Security Architecture

BrowserRoute guards
EdgeHTTPS
APIHelmet CSP
DomainPermissions
PersistencePrisma
BrowserAccessible forms
EdgeCORS origins
APIValidation
DomainSigned actions
PersistenceRLS checks
BrowserTurnstile widget
EdgeBody limits
APIRate limit
DomainAudit events
PersistenceChecksums

Controls are layered so UI checks are never the only protection.

32.3 Request Lifecycle Sequence Diagram

Typical API request lifecycle.

Request Lifecycle Sequence

ClientReact page
ClientAxios API helper
EdgeNginx HTTPS proxy
APIExpress route
APIController validation
DomainService workflow rule
DataRepository/Prisma
StoragePostgreSQL/object storage
ReturnDTO response
EvidenceAudit/log/metric

The UI receives DTOs only after route validation, service authorization, persistence, and evidence capture have completed.

32.4 CI/CD Flow Diagram

Release path from commit to VM.

CI/CD Flow

CodePR
Validatenpm ci
PackageClient dist
ReleaseGitHub environment
VMFetch SHA
CodeMain push
ValidatePrisma validate
PackageServer dist
ReleaseMigration deploy
VMCompose up
ValidateTests/lint/build
ReleaseWebhook
VMSmoke checks

Production deploy happens only after validation and protected environment approval.

32.5 Database ERD/Data Dictionary Diagram

High-level model families.

Database ERD/Data Dictionary Flow

IdentityUser, Session, MFA, verification
OrganizationMembers, capabilities, profiles
ProcurementTender, plan, requirements
BiddingBid, bid document, receipt
EvaluationWorkspace, score, recommendation
AwardNotice, winner, negotiation
ContractContract, signature, payment
Post-awardDelivery, evidence, claims
GovernanceCommunication, records, admin audit
DocumentsDocumentObject and object storage

The schema is organized around lifecycle evidence: identity and organization context flow into procurement, bid, evaluation, award, contract, and audit/document records.

Schema scale174 Prisma models, 335 indexes, 44 unique constraints.
Sourceserver/prisma/schema.prisma

32.6 Backup And Restore Flow

Data recovery flow.

Backup And Restore

ProductionPostgreSQL
BackupFull dump/PITR
StorageEncrypted archive
RestoreIsolated restore
ReturnSmoke checks
ProductionObject bucket
BackupBucket version/copy
StorageOff-host copy
RestoreChecksum validation
ReturnRecovery report

Database and object restore points must be mutually consistent.

32.7 Monitoring Signal Flow

How signals become action.

Monitoring Signal Flow

SourcesNginx/API logs
SourcesDB/Redis/Search/Storage metrics
CollectionMetrics and error tracker
ReviewDashboards
ActionAlert rules
ResponseOn-call triage
ResponseIncident runbook
RecoveryFix or rollback
LearningPostmortem actions
PreventionNew checks/tests

Monitoring is useful only when signals become alerts, alerts become runbook action, and incidents become preventive changes.

32.8 Threat Mitigation Map

Threats mapped to controls and evidence.

Threat Mitigation Map

ActorExternal attacker
SurfaceAuth/document/admin/API
PreventCORS, Turnstile, validation, authz
DetectLogs, alerts, audit events
EvidenceSigned actions, checksums
ActorCompromised account
SurfaceBid, award, contract workflow
PreventMFA, workflow rules, segregation
DetectAnomaly and admin review
EvidenceIncident/postmortem record

Threat review links each actor and surface to preventive controls, detective signals, and auditable evidence.

32.9 Incident Escalation Flow

Operational escalation path.

Incident Escalation Flow

IntakeAlert or user report
TriageConfirm scope
SeverityBusiness impact
CommandIncident commander
ContainFreeze/rollback/revoke
RecoverRestore service/data
CommunicateStakeholders and support
VerifySmoke and integrity checks
PostmortemRoot cause and timeline
PreventAction items

Procurement-impacting incidents require both technical recovery and evidence-preserving business communication.

32.10 Accessibility Review Flow

Accessibility validation path.

Accessibility Review Flow

InventoryRoute and workflow list
KeyboardTab order and focus
Screen readerNames, roles, states
VisualContrast and status cues
FormsLabels and errors
AutomationLint/check tools
FixUI and copy changes
RegressionTests and screenshots
ReleaseBlocker review
EvidenceAccessibility notes

The accessibility review flow keeps public, bid, evaluation, and admin workflows usable without relying on mouse-only or color-only behavior.

This report was prepared from the implementation guide, repository structure, UI parity assets, backend module structure, Prisma database foundation, and screenshot capture workflow in the ProcureX workspace.

  • procurement-platform/server/prisma/sql/rls/002_enable_rls.sql - row-level security policy setup.
